regions.name: Forithilien
regions.supplement: Tolkien (LotR) with Ithilien / Gondor regional material
regions.description: Forithilien, or North Ithilien, is the northern part of Gondor’s eastern Anduin province between the Great River and the Ephel Dúath, stretching from the Dead Marshes and Nindalf southward to the valley of the Ithilduin (later Morgulduin). It is the section of Ithilien most clearly shown in The Lord of the Rings, where Frodo, Sam and Gollum travel towards Cirith Ungol and where Faramir’s rangers maintain secret refuges like Henneth Annûn. In Gondor’s strength it is famed as a fair, fertile land of climbing woods, swift streams and rich gardens; by the late Third Age it is largely depopulated and serves as a hidden war-ground between Gondor’s rangers and the forces moving out of Mordor.

lotr_refs: The Two Towers, Book IV (chapters in Ithilien: 'Of Herbs and Stewed Rabbit', 'The Window on the West')
The Return of the King (references to Ithilien’s beauty and restoration)
Appendices (division into North and South Ithilien and the Morgulduin as boundary)
web_refs: Ithilien entries summarizing geography and flora/fauna [web:221][web:224][web:228][web:229]

regions.timeframe_note: Late Third Age, during the War of the Ring, but using environmental traits that also apply to earlier Third Age when Ithilien was occupied and cultivated.

climate.description: Forithilien has a mild, Mediterranean‑like temperate climate, warmer and more sheltered than Anórien but not as hot or dry as Harad. It lies in the rain shadow of the Ephel Dúath but benefits from the moderating influence of the Anduin and the general Anduin‑valley regime shared with Lebennin and Anórien: relatively mild winters, early and prolonged springs, hot but not desert‑dry summers, and soft autumns.
climate.temperature_pattern: Tolkien depicts North Ithilien in spring as a land where new leaves are already out and many flowers and herbs are in full growth while the Dead Marshes behind are still dreary.[web:221][web:226] This implies an early, mild spring and a generally warm regime. Winters are cool but not severe at river level—there is no mention of lingering snow in the story passages—and the steep, eastward‑rising slopes towards the Ephel Dúath capture sun and warmth. Summers can be hot, especially on exposed slopes and rocky outcrops, but shade from climbing woods and ravines tempers extremes.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Being between Anduin and the Ephel Dúath, Forithilien receives rain from Anduin‑valley systems rather than direct ocean storms. Summaries and analyses describe Ithilien as 'very fertile and green', suggesting regular rainfall and enough moisture to sustain lush woods and gardens.[web:221][web:224] Rain and mist are common in the shoulder seasons, with many streams descending rapidly from the mountain side; in summer, the climate turns drier but not to the point of destroying the vegetation, thanks to deep soils, shade and spring‑fed brooks.
climate.notes: For climate modelling you can treat Forithilien as a warm, moist Anduin‑valley subregion: slightly warmer and more sheltered than Anórien, with more year‑round foliage and a longer effective growing season. It is Tolkien’s archetypal 'garden-land of Gondor'.[web:221][web:224][web:226]

fauna.description: Forithilien’s fauna is that of a rich temperate woodland and garden landscape with layered human history: small game in thickets and fields, many songbirds and woodland birds, forest mammals and predators in the steeper country, and war‑related animals (horses, mûmakil) along roads and camps.

creatures.name: Conies and small game
creatures.note: In 'Of Herbs and Stewed Rabbit' Gollum catches small rabbits (conies) for Sam to cook, signalling that rabbits and similar small game are plentiful in the scrub and grassland patches of North Ithilien.[web:225][web:226] Other small mammals—hares, voles, field mice—fit the same ecological niche in hedges and field margins.

creatures.name: Songbirds and woodland birds
creatures.note: Tolkien repeatedly notes birds singing in the trees and bushes when Frodo and Sam enter Ithilien, in strong contrast to the silence of the Dead Marshes and Emyn Muil.[web:221][web:226] This suggests a dense community of songbirds in woods and thickets, plus corvids and raptors hunting along the edges of clearings and ravines.

creatures.name: Forest mammals and larger predators
creatures.note: Secondary syntheses based on the text describe Ithilien as rich in 'woods and swiftly-falling streams' with varied flora and fauna, including larger forest mammals such as deer and boar and corresponding predators like wolves or big cats in the rougher forested country along the Ephel Dúath.[web:228][web:225] These do not appear explicitly in the narrative but are implied by the intact, semi-wild state of much of the region and by its description as a forested frontier.

creatures.name: War animals (horses and mûmakil)
creatures.note: Tolkien explicitly describes Haradrim moving through Ithilien with horses and mûmakil (oliphaunts) and Gondorian rangers and messengers also using horses in the region.[web:225][web:226] While these are not native fauna, in the War of the Ring period they are part of the living ecosystem and leave trails, dung, trampled vegetation and carcasses that influence scavengers and predators.

flora.description: Forithilien is one of the most floristically rich regions described by Tolkien: a land of climbing woods, waterfalls and terraces of wild and semi‑wild plants. Trees range from temperate broadleaves to more southern‑leaning species; understorey layers of herbs, shrubs and flowers provide strong scents and colour, particularly in the spring season seen in the story.

plants.name: Climbing woods and mixed trees
plants.note: Ithilien is described as 'a fair region of climbing woods and swift-falling streams', with many trees covering foothills and slopes.[web:221][web:226] Tolkien‑based summaries mention pines, cedars, larches and cypresses as part of Ithilien’s tree mixture, alongside more typical temperate species;[web:228] these give North Ithilien’s woods a mixed, almost Mediterranean montane character.

plants.name: Aromatic shrubs and herbs
plants.note: The 'Herbs and Stewed Rabbit' chapter focuses on herbs and aromatic plants: Sam finds fragrant herbs suitable for cooking, and secondary references list bay, thyme‑like plants and other sweet‑smelling shrubs as characteristic of Ithilien’s slopes.[web:225][web:228] This points to a herb‑rich understorey, with many culinary and medicinal plants thriving in warm, well‑drained soils.

plants.name: Flowering bushes and wildflowers
plants.note: Multiple encyclopaedic entries describe Ithilien as 'flora‑rich', with many flowering shrubs and plants giving strong colour in season.[web:225][web:228] Tolkien contrasts its profusion of greenery and blossoms with the desolation of Mordor and the Dead Marshes, underlining an abundance of wildflowers in glades, banks and old garden sites.

plants.name: Streams, falls and moist ravine vegetation
plants.note: The landscape Tolkien describes includes 'swift-falling streams' and hidden falls like those at Henneth Annûn.[web:221][web:226] These ravines support moisture-loving plants—mosses, ferns, shade herbs and riparian shrubs—that differ from the sun‑exposed slopes above, making North Ithilien botanically varied over short distances.

people.description: In Forithilien’s late Third Age state, permanent civilian settlement has largely withdrawn across the Anduin, but its land still bears the imprint of Gondor’s earlier occupation and its present use as a hidden war-front. The primary human presence is Faramir’s rangers, operating from secret refuges among the woods and cliffs, with enemy movements from Mordor crossing the region along roads and tracks.

list.name: Gondorian rangers of Ithilien
list.description: By the War of the Ring, Ithilien’s main Gondorian inhabitants are the Rangers under Faramir, who use secret refuges such as Henneth Annûn and move stealthily through the woods, observing and ambushing enemy forces.[web:221][web:226] They are Dúnedain of Gondor or men closely allied with them, deeply familiar with the land’s paths, streams and cover.

list.name: Displaced Gondorian folk (earlier Third Age context)
list.description: Earlier in the Third Age, Ithilien was one of Gondor’s core provinces, governed from Minas Ithil and described as very fertile and well-settled.[web:224][web:225] Over time, growing threats from Mordor and raids forced most of its inhabitants back over the Anduin; remnants of villages, gardens and roads remain in North Ithilien as ruins and overgrown clearings.

list.name: Haradrim and other enemy forces in transit
list.description: During the War of the Ring, Haradrim and other allies of Sauron cross North Ithilien on their way to the Black Gate and the assaults on Gondor; Tolkien shows Frodo and Sam witnessing a Harad column and a mûmak in this region.[web:225][web:226] These forces establish temporary camps, use the old roads and further disturb the land.

land.name: North Ithilien wooded hill-belt
land.description: The main band of climbing woods and hills between the Anduin valley floor and the lower slopes of the Ephel Dúath, forming the landscape through which Frodo, Sam and Gollum travel and where most of Faramir’s ranger activity occurs.[web:221][web:226]
land.biome_hint: warm-temperate wooded hills with mixed broadleaf and conifer trees, many herbs and flowers, and frequent small streams

land.name: Ravines and waterfall basins (Henneth Annûn-type sites)
land.description: Steep-sided ravines and cliff basins where swift streams drop from the slopes into sheltered bowls; these host waterfalls like Henneth Annûn and provide ideal locations for hidden refuges, with dense vegetation and complex microclimates.[web:221][web:226]
land.biome_hint: cool, moist microclimates with shade-tolerant plants, mosses and ferns within an otherwise warm region

land.name: Anduin-margin terraces and clearings
land.description: Terraces and clearings along the westward edge of North Ithilien overlooking or near the Anduin, where earlier Gondorian settlement had fields, gardens and small harbours, now mostly overgrown but still forming more open, fertile patches within the general woodland.[web:221][web:224]
land.biome_hint: fertile, well-watered ground with a mix of wild herbs, former garden species and meadow plants

regions.notes: For your map and biome system, treat Forithilien as Tolkien’s primary Ithilien zone: a north-central, warm-temperate Anduin–Ephel Dúath belt with exceptional plant richness, many small streams and ravines, and a human geography dominated in the late Third Age by rangers and war traffic rather than settled towns.[web:221][web:224][web:225][web:226][web:228]

regions.name: Anórien
regions.supplement: Tolkien (LotR) with Gondor regional material (Sea-Lords of Gondor / Minas Tirith)
regions.description: Anórien is the northern fief of Gondor that occupies the strip of land between the northern slopes and valleys of the White Mountains and the west bank of the Anduin, opposite northern Ithilien. Established towards the end of the Second Age along with Ithilien and Lebennin as part of Gondor’s Anduin heartland, it later remains the only Gondorian fief north of the White Mountains when Calenardhon is ceded to the Éothéod to become Rohan. The region is defined by mountain-fed valleys, tall grasslands and scattered woods, pierced by the Great West Road and guarded by beacon-peaks and the Drúadan Forest.

pages: 2.2 Establishment of Anórien / Ithilien / Lebennin as early Anduin settlements
3.x Climate and geography of the lower Anduin and White Mountains
Minas Tirith module 4.0 Flora and Fauna (Pelennor / Anduin valley analogue)
lotr_refs: The Lord of the Rings, Book V (description of Anórien and the beacons)
Appendices (fiefs of Gondor, Drúadan Forest, Mering Stream border)

regions.timeframe_note: Third Age shortly before and during the War of the Ring; Gondor still holds Anórien as a frontier fief, sparsely settled but strategically vital.

climate.description: Anórien has a cool-temperate, valley-and-upland climate influenced both by the White Mountains to the south and the Anduin valley to the east. Winters are cooler and windier than in coastal Lebennin, but still relatively mild for its latitude; springs and autumns are long and suited to travel along the Great West Road, while summers are warm with dry spells on the tall grasslands.
climate.temperature_pattern: As part of Gondor’s Anduin-valley core, Anórien shares the general pattern Sea-Lords of Gondor gives for Lebennin and neighbouring regions: mild winters, long springs and autumns, and warm, often hot summers in the lowlands.[file:216] Its position north of most of Lebennin and further from the Bay of Belfalas means colder winter nights and more frequent frosts on the open uplands, especially in valleys that drain cold air off the White Mountains. Summers in the valleys can be quite warm, but elevation and mountain breezes temper extremes.
climate.precipitation_pattern: The lower Anduin system is described as having relatively even precipitation through the year, with no long, Harad-like droughts; most rain falls in spring and autumn when moist maritime air meets cooler inland flows.[file:216] Anórien, being more inland and shielded by the White Mountains, receives slightly less overall rainfall than Lebennin but still enough for fertile grasslands and woods. Snow falls in winter on higher beacon peaks and the upper forested slopes, while at valley level winter precipitation is often cold rain or mixed sleet. The Drúadan Forest and other wooded pockets mark zones where local topography captures extra moisture.
climate.notes: For your climate model, treat Anórien as a slightly cooler, more continental variant of the Lebennin inland-valley climate column in Sea-Lords of Gondor: similar season lengths and moderate annual rainfall, with added orographic effects from the White Mountains and a stronger frost/snow signal on uplands.[file:216][web:238][web:243]

fauna.description: Fauna in Anórien combines lowland Anduin-valley species with upland White Mountain and Drúadan Forest communities. Grazing herds and field animals use the tall grasslands and river-margin farms, while wooded valleys and the Drúadan Forest shelter forest mammals, predators and the secretive Drúedain. War-time movements add horses, messenger trains and armies moving along the Great West Road.

creatures.name: Grazing herds and field animals
creatures.note: The Anduin plains in Gondor are described as supporting herds of domestic stock and wild grazers, with tall grasses and almost year-round floral cover in places like Lebennin’s Talath Lethen.[file:216] Anórien’s tall grasslands and Anduin‑facing vales host similar assemblages: cattle and horses from Gondor and Rohan using the road corridor, sheep and goats in smaller numbers, plus wild deer and smaller field mammals such as rabbits and ground-dwelling rodents.

creatures.name: Forest mammals and predators (White Mountain and Drúadan belt)
creatures.note: Sea-Lords of Gondor portrays the White Mountain valleys as forested uplands with numerous animals and significant predators.[file:216] In Anórien’s southern band the Drúadan Forest and other woods shelter boar, deer, bears and wolves, together with smaller forest mammals and birds. Some of these have been pushed out of denser settled lands like the Pelennor and Lebennin and persist here in more rugged, sparsely farmed terrain.

creatures.name: Birds of the Anduin corridor
creatures.note: The Anduin valley is a migration and travel corridor with abundant bird life; Sea-Lords notes numerous birds and waterfowl along rivers and wetlands in Lebennin and the Ethir Anduin.[file:216] Anórien’s proximity to the Great River and its mixed woodland and grassland provide habitat for songbirds, raptors hunting over the plains, and waterfowl on the Anduin margins and smaller streams.

creatures.name: Horses and war animals
creatures.note: During the War of the Ring, Anórien is crossed repeatedly by mounted messengers, Gondorian troops and the Rohirrim on their ride to Minas Tirith; Tolkien describes lines of riders using the Great West Road here.[web:234][web:235] In game terms, horses and associated camp animals (mules, pack-beasts) are a regular part of the living landscape in war-time Anórien.

flora.description: Anórien’s vegetation reflects its position between mountains and river: tall grasslands and cultivated fields in the central strip; pockets of rich woodland on lower slopes and in sheltered valleys; and denser forests, including the Drúadan Forest, along the mountain foot and on rougher ground. Compared to Ithilien, it is less of a garden-land and more of a mixed pasture and woodland frontier.

plants.name: Tall grasslands and open fields
plants.note: Secondary summaries based on Tolkien describe Anórien as a land of 'tall grasslands' stretching between the White Mountains and Anduin.[web:238] Like the flowering fields of Lebennin, these support deep grasses and numerous wildflowers, but with more emphasis on open pasture and less on intensive garden agriculture than in Ithilien. Fields along the Great West Road and near Anduin are cultivated for grain and fodder.

plants.name: Mixed deciduous and evergreen woods
plants.note: Sea-Lords presents the White Mountain valleys south of Lebennin as bearing a mix of deciduous and evergreen forests.[file:216] Anórien’s southern and eastern slopes follow the same pattern: oaks, beeches and other broadleaf trees with pines and firs at higher elevations or on poorer soils, forming belts of woodland that frame the grasslands and offer both timber and cover.

plants.name: Drúadan Forest vegetation
plants.note: The Drúadan Forest, sitting in the hill country west of Minas Tirith and within or on the edge of Anórien, is depicted in Tolkien as a dense, ancient wood where the Drúedain live in secret.[web:234][web:243] Its undergrowth is thick, with old trunks, tangles of shrubs and a comparatively undisturbed flora compared to more exploited woods; many shade-tolerant herbs and ferns persist here.

plants.name: River-margin and wet ground plants
plants.note: Near the Anduin and along tributary streams descending from the White Mountains, Anórien shares the riverine flora Sea-Lords describes for Lebennin: willows, reeds, sedges and other moisture-loving plants in low-lying ground.[file:216] These form narrow belts rather than broad swamps but are important ecologically and for local use.

people.description: Anórien’s human landscape in the late Third Age is shaped by its status as a strategic corridor rather than a dense core province: Dúnedain and mixed-descendant farmers in scattered villages and steadings, Drúedain clans in the Drúadan Forest, and military traffic along the Great West Road and beacon line.

list.name: Dúnedain and Gondorian farmers of Anórien
list.description: The founding of Gondor concentrated people in the Anduin valley, settling Anórien, Ithilien and Lebennin together as early heartlands.[file:216] By the War of the Ring, Anórien’s lowlands host scattered villages and farmsteads of Gondorians of mixed Númenórean and local descent, who work fields and pastures and supply traffic along the Great West Road and river.

list.name: Drúedain (Woses) of the Drúadan Forest
list.description: The Drúadan Forest in Anórien shelters the Drúedain, 'Wild Men' who guide the Rohirrim to Minas Tirith by hidden ways in the War of the Ring.[web:234][web:243] Descended from very ancient populations akin to the Drughu of Lebennin’s first inhabitants,[file:216] they maintain secretive communities in the forest, avoiding most outsiders.

list.name: Messengers, soldiers and Rohirrim riders
list.description: Anórien’s Great West Road is heavily used by Gondorian messengers and troops, and during the War of the Ring the Rohirrim ride through its northern strip on their way to Minas Tirith; Tolkien explicitly describes the beacon fires behind Anórien and the riders answering them.[web:234][web:235] The region’s population therefore includes transient military columns and supply trains on top of the resident peasantry.

land.name: Anórien proper (valleys and grasslands)
land.description: The main strip of Anórien between the northern White Mountain valleys and the Anduin: a narrow belt of tall grasslands, fields and scattered woods, with the Great West Road running east–west as the principal artery.[web:238][web:242]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate grassland and mixed farmland with scattered woodland and a strong mountain influence

land.name: Beacon peaks of the White Mountains
land.description: The chain of peaks and shoulders north of the main White Mountain crest that host Gondor’s beacons (Amon Dîn, Eilenach, Nardol, etc.), rising above Anórien and sending warning fires towards Rohan.[web:234][web:242] These heights are rocky, colder and more sparsely vegetated than the valleys below, but they anchor the region’s identity and communications.
land.biome_hint: rocky upland with thin soils, grass and scrub, snow in winter and extensive views

land.name: Drúadan Forest
land.description: A dense, ancient woodland on the lower northern slopes of the White Mountains within or adjacent to Anórien, home of the Drúedain, characterised by heavy canopy, thick undergrowth and relatively little Gondorian exploitation.[web:234][web:243]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate mixed forest with dense undergrowth and minimal clearance

land.name: Anduin margin opposite northern Ithilien
land.description: The low river-edge lands and bluffs along the west bank of the Anduin opposite northern Ithilien, including the approaches to Cair Andros and downstream crossings; this forms Anórien’s eastern limit and a transit interface between river traffic and land routes.[web:234][web:240]
land.biome_hint: temperate riverbank with narrow belts of moist-soil vegetation and adjacent farmland

regions.notes: For mapping, treat Anórien as a narrow Anduin-valley and mountain-foot fief: cooler and more sparsely settled than Lebennin, with tall grasslands and scattered farms in the central strip, beacon peaks and the Drúadan Forest along the White Mountains, and the Anduin margin linking it to northern Ithilien and Cair Andros.[file:216][web:234][web:238][web:243]

regions.name: Rammas Formen
regions.supplement: Arnor: The Land (with material from Rangers of the North / Northern Waste / Angmar)
regions.description: The Rammas Forod (Sindarin 'Great Wall of the North'; Adûnaic Ramênytunêny, actually 'South-wall') is a low chain of hills dividing the down-lands of northern Arthedain from the watershed of the Wash Tundra. To the west it widens into the Talath Muil, while to the east its hills thin out and merge into the Everlasting Plain. Born as debris from the collapse of the Iron Mountains in the War of Wrath, the ridge has shed rock and soil into the valleys on either side, allowing modest forests to grow along its flanks. Clear air and narrow defiles offer room for small farms and gardens, and for a time the Rammas served as a religious retreat for the Faithful of Arnor.

climate.description: The Rammas Forod is a cold, semi-arid upland with a highly seasonal pattern: a brief, brilliant spring when snowmelt triggers a burst of grass and wildflowers, followed by a long dry season in which the plateau turns brown and parched. Annual precipitation is low and strongly biased toward winter snow blown down from the far north, with rainstorms rare because of the ridge’s interior position and flanking highlands.
climate.temperature_pattern: When winter snows melt in spring, the plateau comes to life for a few weeks as grasses surge and wildflowers cover the hills in many colours. Once spring passes into summer, the lack of moisture and rainfall quickly bleaches the grass and causes the flowers to wilt and shrivel. This produces cool to cold winters with frequent snow and wind, contrasted with relatively mild but very dry summers with marked day–night swings typical of an inland plateau.
climate.precipitation_pattern: The Rammas Forod receives on the order of 10 to 20 inches of precipitation a year, most of it as snow driven south from Forodwaith in winter. Rainstorms are uncommon and occur only when conditions align, because the Blue Mountains to the west and the vast Everlasting Plain to the east block many spring and autumn storm tracks. Moisture is concentrated in the snow season and early melt; the rest of the year is predominantly dry, with only scattered showers.
climate.notes: Contemporaries often blame the Witch-king of Angmar for the dry fields of the Rammas, but the text emphasizes that the aridity is a natural consequence of the plateau’s landlocked setting and flanking barriers. In a Third Age, pre–War of the Ring frame, the ridge is already operating under this dry, continental regime: snowy winters, fleeting wet springs and extended dry periods dominating summer and autumn.

fauna.description: Fauna on the Rammas Forod combines hardy upland grazers and small domestic herds with predators and war-related scavengers. During the wars between Arthedain and Angmar, orcs and their allies are a constant presence around the ridge and its forts.

creatures.name: Plateau grazers (deer and similar game)
creatures.note: The short, intense growing season and long dry spell resemble a cold steppe or semi-arid plateau; this favours deer-like grazers and other herbivores able to track the brief flush of spring grass and retreat to sheltered valleys or forest fringes as the surface browns.

creatures.name: Small sheep and goat herds
creatures.note: The clear air and narrow defiles are noted as excellent sites for small farms and gardens. Faithful communities and frontier farmers maintain modest flocks of sheep and goats in and around these pockets, taking advantage of local moisture and shelter to support limited pastoralism.

creatures.name: Orcs of Angmar
creatures.note: Once the fortification line along the ridge is built and especially after the fall of Dol Gormaen, orcs from Angmar are common in the area. The Rammas becomes a contested front, and lone travellers risk running into orc patrols or camps if they attempt to cross or camp on the high ground.

creatures.name: Predators and carrion-feeders
creatures.note: Chronic skirmishing and the movement of armies create a steady supply of corpses and offal, encouraging wolves, wargs and scavenging birds to shadow the armies and haunt the ridgeline. These predators rely on both wild game and war dead, and they help define the ecological tone of the plateau during the conflict years.

flora.description: Vegetation on the Rammas Forod is defined by extreme seasonality and shallow upland soils: a sudden spring flush of grass and wildflowers, then a rapid shift to brown, drought-stressed cover. Eroded material from the ridge accumulates in side-valleys and defiles, where somewhat deeper soils and slight moisture advantages allow small forest patches and intensive gardens.

plants.name: Highland grasses
plants.note: As soon as the snow melts, grasses race through their growth cycle, drawing on meltwater to produce a brief carpet of green. These are short-lived, drought-tolerant grasses that quickly dry and turn brown as the plateau loses moisture through summer and early autumn.

plants.name: Multicoloured wildflowers
plants.note: The plateau is described as erupting into multicoloured splendour in spring as wildflowers bloom alongside the grasses. These are ephemeral species adapted to germinate, flower and set seed within a narrow window, then persist as seeds until the next snowmelt.

plants.name: Modest forests along the flanks
plants.note: The origin of the ridge as Iron Mountain debris means it has gradually shed rock and soil into the adjacent valleys, where deeper pockets of sediment allow 'modest forests' to take hold. These woodland strips cling to the sides and bases of the hills, where slight increases in soil depth and moisture offset the otherwise dry regime.

plants.name: Gardens and small fields in defiles
plants.note: Narrow gullies and sheltered passes, with clear air and some water capture, are singled out as excellent places for small farms and gardens. Religious communities and local farmers cultivate intensive plots here, likely focusing on short-season vegetables, herbs and hardy fruit suited to the brief wet period and the long dry tail.

people.description: In the late Third Age before the War of the Ring, the Rammas Forod functions as both a fortified frontier between Arthedain and Angmar and a zone of religious retreat. Permanent population is sparse, concentrated in strongpoints, tiny farmsteads in sheltered defiles and seasonal hunter camps along the northern edge.

list.name: Faithful of Arnor (religious communities)
list.description: For a time the Rammas, like the lower but more abrupt down-lands to the south, serves as a religious retreat for the Faithful of Arnor. Monastic houses, hermitages and small religious enclaves occupy hilltops and defiles, combining contemplative life with subsistence gardening and herding in the sheltered pockets of the ridge.

list.name: Arthadan garrisons and fort line
list.description: When the tide of war turns against Arthedain in T.A. 1325, a line of fortifications is constructed along the Rammas, anchored on its eastern flank by the great keep of Dol Gormaen. After the fall of that citadel in T.A. 1408, the ridge becomes an exposed frontier, fiercely contested by Arthadan and Angmarean forces, with blockhouses, watchposts and fieldworks strung along the hills.

list.name: Angmarean forces and raiders
list.description: Following Dol Gormaen’s fall, Angmar gains direct access to the ridge. Orcs, human contingents and other servants of the Witch-king use the Rammas as a corridor for raids southwards. Anyone wandering here risks stumbling into a battle or being seized as a suspected spy by either side.

list.name: Lumënyihmënyt
list.description: The Lumënyihmënyt roam the northernmost stretches of the Rammas Forod from early spring through mid-autumn. Their camps, perched on rocky slopes, are crude hunting sites intended to last days or at most weeks. Reserved and wary, they usually break camp and move rather than deal with rough strangers, but if approached carefully they can provide detailed information about local geography, hazards and notable features.

land.name: Main Rammas ridge
land.description: The primary low hill line that separates northern Arthedain’s down-lands from the Wash Tundra watershed. Formed from the wreckage of the Iron Mountains, it acts as a natural 'great wall' with slopes that drop into sediment-filled valleys hosting small forests and farms.
land.biome_hint: cold, semi-arid ridge and plateau with short-season grasses, spring blooms and expansive views

land.name: Western widening into Talath Muil
land.description: To the west, the ridge broadens and grades into the Talath Muil, the drear, windswept upland plain that connects the Rammas to the Evendim Hills and the valley of the Lhûn.
land.biome_hint: transition from dry ridge to colder, windier upland grassland tied to Talath Muil

land.name: Eastern fade into the Everlasting Plain
land.description: To the east, the hills diminish and gradually blend into the Everlasting Plain, a wide interior expanse that carries the Rammas’s steppe-like character out onto a broader, lower plateau with even fewer structural breaks.
land.biome_hint: cold, dry plain edge extending the steppe-like regime beyond the ridge

land.name: Valleys and defiles with forests and farms
land.description: On both sides of the ridge, erosion has created valleys and narrow defiles where transported soil accumulates. These niches support small forests and patches of farmland or gardens and host religious houses, isolated farmsteads and Lumënyihmënyt camps.
land.biome_hint: relatively greener microclimates embedded in an otherwise dry upland, with small woods and intensive plots

regions.notes: In your climate and biome model, Rammas Forod should be treated as a cold, semi-arid plateau and ridge system under strong rain shadow, with a very short green season, long dry periods and snowy, windy winters. It is also a key military and cultural frontier between Arthedain and Angmar and a corridor where garrisons, religious communities and Lumënyihmënyt hunters overlap, all in the Third Age before the War of the Ring.

regions.name: Evendim Hills
regions.supplement: Arnor: The Land (compilation from Rangers of the North, Lost Realm of Cardolan, etc.)
regions.description: The Evendim Hills (Sindarin Emyn Uial, 'Hills of Twilight') are the upland region around the valley of the Cornalas and the northern shore of Lake Evendim (Nenuial) in western Arthedain. For one season they are pale brown under constant cool mists cupped in the valley, for another they lie soft and white under snow, and in the Awakening and the Poem the valley becomes a shifting tapestry of lush greens. Though unproductive by southern standards—steep hillsides strain shepherds’ knees and narrow vales have thin soil—the hills have long been the heart of Arthedain, their rippling waters and mists inspiring poets and musicians.

climate.description: Evendim’s climate is cool-temperate and oceanic, marked by constant cool mists, luminous alabaster skies in winter and a vividly green growing season. Increasingly harsh winters in the later Third Age give the more exposed hills a permanently brown cast, forcing flocks into sheltered valleys, but overall the region remains milder and more hospitable than the northern tundra or Talath Muil.
climate.temperature_pattern: For one season of the year, the Cornalas is 'a pale brown, blurred by constant cool mists,' with sounds muted by bog; for another, 'all is soft white,' and the hills look like clouds brought down to touch the earth, with luminous alabaster skies and axe-blows echoing from hill to bluff to steading. During the Awakening and the Poem (spring and high summer), the valley is 'green, lush shades of it blurring as though tinted by a painter's brush,' shifting tone with storms and rain so that one might never tire of watching its moods. Over the later Third Age the weather grows colder, and increasingly harsh winters give exposed hills a brown cast, pushing shepherds to keep flocks closer to valleys.[file:179]
climate.precipitation_pattern: The hills receive abundant precipitation: cool mists that pool in the valley like wine in a goblet, soft snows that cover hills in white and frequent rains that feed the lush greens of the growing season. Rain 'seemed eager to wash away the soil behind the terraces and retaining walls,' threatening agriculture and infrastructure; constant cool dampness breeds chilling fevers and fluxes of throat and lungs among the people.[file:179]
climate.notes: Compared to Talath Muil or the Wash Tundra, the Evendim Hills are wet and cool rather than outright bleak: more like a misty, Atlantic hill country. Your Third Age timeframe (pre-War of the Ring) corresponds to the colder phase described by Eärnur and the Emyn Uial passages, but before the full depopulation and later Fourth Age resettlement. In game terms, expect a high moisture, moderate-temperature regime with strong seasonality in visual appearance but relatively narrow thermal extremes.[file:179]

fauna.description: The Evendim Hills support a mix of upland pastoral and wild fauna: flocks of sheep, some cattle and goats on terraces and downs; wild deer and boar in groves and steeper slopes; and predators and scavengers taking advantage of both domestic animals and wild game. Harsh winters and dampness select for hardy stock and resilient wild species.

creatures.name: Sheep, goats and limited cattle
creatures.note: The Twilight Hills narrative emphasises that 'every sheaf of grain and basket of apples, every rack of mutton and bundle of herbs' comes only through hard labour; steep hills and thin soils force shepherds to terrace slopes and build stone-walled fields. Flocks of sheep (and some goats) are the primary domestic animals in this terrain, with cattle limited to the better valley floors.[file:179]

creatures.name: Wild sheep and upland grazers (later years)
creatures.note: In later years, after wars and the Banes of Angmar, 'a few trees and wild sheep appeared on the hills' as the climate improved slightly. In your Third Age timeframe, these wild sheep and other upland grazers are already part of the fauna, sharing slopes with domestic flocks and providing game.[file:179]

creatures.name: Deer, boar and woodland game
creatures.note: The general 'Animals of Eriador' section lists deer, boar and other woodland game; the groves and narrow vales of the Evendim Hills provide habitat for such species, especially in less terraced, more 'wild' patches where gorse, heather and grass are ready to engulf human works.[file:132][file:179]

creatures.name: Wolves, wargs and scavengers
creatures.note: As winters grow harsher and human population declines, wolves, wargs and carrion birds range through the hills. The later Emyn Uial description notes continued raids by Orcs, wargs and 'other unclean terrors,' implying robust predator and scavenger communities exploiting both livestock and travellers.[file:179]

flora.description: Evendim’s flora is a classic cool-temperate hill mix shaped by both nature and generations of hard human labour: gorse, heather and grass ready to surge over terraces; stone-walled fields of grain and orchards in narrow vales; planted and thinned groves for wood; and bogs and mists in valley bottoms. Despite cultivation, the hills retain an impression of wildness.

plants.name: Gorse, heather and grass
plants.note: Even with terraces and stone walls, 'the hills gave an impression of wildness, as though the gorse, heather, and grass, if not carefully watched, were ready to spring forth and engulf the little works of Men.' These low shrubs and grasses dominate uncultivated slopes and abandoned fields, defining the visual character of the Emyn Uial.[file:179]

plants.name: Terraced fields and orchards
plants.note: Fields in narrow vales are surrounded by stone walls built from rocks once strewn among the crops; slopes are terraced to retain soil, and groves are planted and thinned 'to ensure the growth of wood.' Every sheaf of grain and basket of apples is the product of careful, labour-intensive agriculture on marginal soils.[file:179]

plants.name: Bog plants and valley-floor vegetation
plants.note: In the Cornalas valley, cool mists and bogs blur sounds and visuals; the bog supports wetland vegetation—sedges, reeds, mosses—consistent with Eriador’s wetland flora but here confined to narrow, mist-filled bottoms.[file:179][file:132]

plants.name: Later regrowth (Fourth Age context, for reference only)
plants.note: After the Banes of Angmar recede in the Fourth Age, 'a few trees and wild sheep appeared on the hills.' In your Third Age setting, the hills have not yet fully recovered, but pockets of spontaneous woodland regrowth already exist in less worked or abandoned areas, echoing this later trend.[file:179]

people.description: The Evendim Hills (Emyn Uial) are home primarily to the Commons of Arthedain—the hillfolk of the Twilight Hills—with stern but just Dúnedain lords. Their culture is shaped by hard labour, cool damp climate and relative peace under Arthadan rule. Later, wars and the Banes of Angmar depopulate the hills, but in your period they remain a living, if harsh, homeland.

list.name: Commons of Arthedain (Men of the Twilight Hills)
list.description: The 'Commons of Arthedain, and particularly the Men of the Twilight Hills,' are described as hard-working hillfolk who wrest grain, apples, mutton and herbs from steep slopes and thin soils. Though famed for 'bland frugality of speech and coin,' they are said to be 'happier than most' thanks to the unusual law and peace of Arthedain: 'a man can still walk the country from the River to the Downs with naught in his hand but a stick to ward off the stray dogs.'[file:179]

list.name: Dúnedain lords of the Twilight Hills
list.description: Dúnedain who rule the Emyn Uial are 'stern and aloof, but also just and fair-spoken to all.' They maintain roads, terraces, groves and law, and 'would have words with strangers who caused a fuss,' yet offer hospitality and advice to travellers. Their presence ensures comparatively safe travel and structural investment (roads, terraces) across the hills.[file:179]

list.name: Tinkers, peddlers and travellers
list.description: Tinkers and peddlers move from steading to steading along hardpacked and paved roads, doing odd jobs, selling goods and carrying news and gossip. They are described as friendly and talkative, equal in information value to merchants and often more approachable, shaping the social fabric of the hills.[file:179]

land.name: Cornalas valley
land.description: A central valley of the Evendim Hills where cool mists are 'cupped in the valley like wine in a goblet,' muting sounds and painting the landscape pale brown in one season, white in another and lush green in the Awakening and the Poem. Boggy ground and terraced fields coexist here under constant damp.[file:179]
land.biome_hint: cool, mist-filled upland valley with bogs, terraced fields and mixed shrub/grass cover

land.name: Twilight Hills (Emyn Uial) proper
land.description: Steep hills with narrow vales and thin soils, surrounded by stone-walled fields and terraces, planted groves and ubiquitous gorse, heather and grass. The hills give an impression of wildness despite human works; steadings are typically a half-day’s march apart.[file:179]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate upland hills with mixed managed and wild vegetation, high rainfall and strong seasonality

land.name: Roads and terraces network
land.description: Hardpacked earth roads on flat ground and stone-shouldered, paved roads on steep sections tie the hills together, allowing travellers to 'always arrive where they are headed.' Terraces and retaining walls support fields, but frost-heaves and erosion constantly push rocks up and wash soil away.[file:179]
land.biome_hint: human-modified upland landscape with intensive small-scale agriculture and infrastructure

regions.notes: For your climate and biome system, treat the Evendim Hills (Emyn Uial) as a cool, maritime upland with high rainfall, heavy seasonal mist and snow, and a strong human imprint through terraces and roads, yet always on the edge of rewilding by gorse, heather and grass. In the pre–War of the Ring Third Age, they are still inhabited and functioning as a heartland of Arthedain, though already feeling the strain of colder winters and creeping decline.[file:179]

regions.name: Talath Muil
regions.supplement: Arnor: The Land (compilation from Rangers of the North / Angmar-related material)
regions.description: Talath Muil ('Drear Plain') is the rugged highland plain forming the northwestern marches of Arthedain, dividing northern Arthedain from the Wash Tundra and the Vale of the Lhûn. It is a windswept, largely treeless upland of low grass and open grassland, dotted with large wildflower meadows and small copses of ash and birch. To the east it abuts the rocky western edges of the northern Hills of Evendim, whose rough ground is difficult to traverse even on foot, and whose rocky hills gradually mellow into grass-covered downs and finally spill northeast into the dry plateau of the Rammas Forod.

climate.description: Talath Muil lies at the transition between Arthedain’s cool-temperate hill country and the colder Wash Tundra and Vale of the Lhûn. It is a grey, windswept upland with cold, damp weather, frequent fog and drizzle, and increasingly harsh winters over the course of the Third Age.
climate.temperature_pattern: Rugged highlands formed the northwestern marches of Arthedain, and 'low grass was the only living thing on this windswept plain, shunned even by the hardy Lossoth.' Winters are long, cold and often severe, with frost and snow lingering on the exposed heights; summers are cool and short, barely allowing grasses and wildflowers a growing season. Through the long years between the Second Northern War and the Third, the weather in Eriador grows increasingly cold; in the Twilight Hills/Emyn Uial, this trend gives hills a permanently brown cast, and Talath Muil—northwest of these hills and closer to tundra—experiences even harsher conditions.[file:179]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Talath Muil is a 'grey wasteland, where every day brings fog and drizzle, and every scrap of cloth drips cold moisture.' Crown Prince Eärnur notes that his soldiers are stricken with flux in this climate, rising each damp morning to a rolling, rumbling cough. Rain and drizzle are nearly constant, with only brief breaks; snow and sleet in winter add to the moisture load. Poor, thin soils on the rocky uplands shed water quickly into hollows, but the constant damp keeps the plain soaked for much of the year.[file:179]
climate.notes: Climatically Talath Muil is one of the bleakest parts of Arthedain, closer in feel to the Wash Tundra than to the more hospitable Evendim Hills. Its bleakness becomes more pronounced as the Third Age progresses, with cold intensifying and winters lengthening. Your timeframe—Third Age before the War of the Ring—corresponds to this colder phase, but before the complete depopulation and climatic recovery described for the Fourth Age.[file:179]

fauna.description: Fauna in Talath Muil consists of hardy grassland and upland species—wild sheep, goats, small herbivores and their predators—together with more dangerous creatures taking shelter in the rocky edges of the Evendim Hills. The climate and exposure limit both diversity and density compared to richer parts of Arthedain.

creatures.name: Wild sheep and upland grazers
creatures.note: The Emyn Uial/Twilight Hills narrative notes that over centuries 'a few trees and wild sheep appeared on the hills' as climate improved slightly; in the harsher Talath Muil, wild sheep and similar hardy grazers are characteristic animals, clinging to grass patches on the windswept plain.[file:179]

creatures.name: Wolves and carrion beasts
creatures.note: The bleak, sparsely settled upland provides hunting ground for wolves and scavengers. The 'Ban es of Angmar' later concentrate blights and diseases in the Emyn Uial, and similar magical or natural plagues affect animals in northern Arthedain; predators and carrion birds thrive on the weakened stock and battlefield remains.[file:179][file:132]

creatures.name: Orcs, Trolls and bandits in the rocky Evendim fringe
creatures.note: The rocky western edges of the northern Hills of Evendim are 'an excellent hiding place for Orcs, Trolls and bandits'; in winter, however, the cold can turn a hiding place into a last resting place. These creatures move through the Talath Muil uplands to reach or flee the Evendim hills, making them part of the functional 'fauna' adventurers must account for.[file:179]

creatures.name: Small mammals and birds of Eriador uplands
creatures.note: Animals of Eriador lists rabbits, hares, voles and numerous birds in downs and uplands. Talath Muil’s grass and wildflower meadows host these smaller species, though constant damp and cold keep populations lower than in milder regions.[file:132]

flora.description: Talath Muil’s flora is a sparse upland cover: low grasses, hardy wildflowers, gorse and heath on the exposed plain, with small copses of ash and birch in sheltered pockets near streams and in the lee of rocky outcrops. Toward the Evendim Hills, vegetation becomes rougher and more broken, merging into the grass-covered downs and, farther northeast, into the dry plateau of Rammas Forod.

plants.name: Low grass and wildflower meadows
plants.note: The Talath Muil is described as 'primarily open grassland, dotted occasionally by large wildflower meadows,' with low grass as 'the only living thing' on parts of the windswept plain. Short, wind-tolerant grasses and hardy flowering plants dominate, giving brief colour in the short summer before browning under cold and wind.[file:179]

plants.name: Copses of ash and birch
plants.note: Small copses of ash and birch dot the drear plain where soil and moisture permit, especially in slight depressions or where rocks break the wind. These trees are explicitly mentioned as the main arboreal elements interrupting the otherwise open grassland.[file:179]

plants.name: Rocky Evendim fringe vegetation
plants.note: Eastwards, the Talath Muil 'suddenly abutted the rocky western edges of the northern Hills of Evendim.' This rough, rocky region has thinner soils and more exposed rock, supporting patches of scrub, gorse and hardy grasses clinging to ledges and hollows; as the hills spread eastward they become less rocky and barren, merging into low, grass-covered downs.[file:179]

plants.name: Rammas Forod dry plateau edge
plants.note: The northeastern edge of the hills 'spilled out into the great, dry plateau of Rammas Forod'; here vegetation thins further, approaching a cold steppe or semi-tundra mix of sparse grasses and low shrubs, linking Talath Muil’s biota to that of the Wash Tundra and far northern Eriador.[file:179][file:132]

people.description: Talath Muil sits at the far edge of Arthedain’s settled world. It has no dense population; its human presence consists of frontier shepherds, border watchposts and moving armies during the wars against Angmar. Even hardy Lossoth avoid this 'drear plain.'

list.name: Arthadan frontier folk and militias
list.description: Frontier shepherds, small steads and watch-forts on the northwestern marches of Arthedain use the fringes of Talath Muil for grazing and as a staging ground, but the bleak climate and exposure prevent large-scale settlement. During the wars with Angmar, local militias are pinned on frontiers and armies cross these uplands, as described in the accounts of battles around Fornost and the Emyn Uial.[file:179][file:132]

list.name: Lossoth and northern neighbours (peripheral)
list.description: The description notes that even the hardy Lossoth shun this windswept plain; they are present more as a climatic benchmark than as inhabitants, emphasizing Talath Muil’s severity even by northern standards.[file:179]

list.name: Orcs, Trolls and bandits (non-human threats)
list.description: While not 'people' in a civic sense, Orcs, Trolls and bandits regularly use the rocky Evendim fringe and Talath Muil as hiding and transit zones, preying on caravans, scouts and retreating armies. The module explicitly calls out their presence in this terrain.[file:179]

land.name: Talath Muil upland (Drear Plain)
land.description: The core rugged highland plain forming the northwestern marches of Arthedain, an open, windswept grassland with low vegetation and occasional wildflower meadows, shunned even by the hardy Lossoth.[file:179]
land.biome_hint: cold, windswept upland grassland with low vegetation and frequent fog/drizzle

land.name: Rocky western edges of the northern Evendim Hills
land.description: A rough, rocky zone where Talath Muil meets the western faces of the northern Hills of Evendim. Difficult to traverse, it offers hiding places for Orcs, Trolls and bandits, but winter cold can make it deadly.[file:179]
land.biome_hint: rocky upland fringe with thin soils, scrub and grass patches, used as lairs by hostile creatures

land.name: Grass-covered downs (eastern transition)
land.description: As the northern Evendim hills spread eastward from Talath Muil, they become less rocky and barren, melding into a low line of grass-covered downs that link the upland to the more hospitable Emyn Uial/Twilight Hills and central Arthedain.[file:179]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate downs with grasses and scattered shrubs, less severe than the drear plain

land.name: Rammas Forod plateau edge
land.description: The northeastern spillover of the hills onto the great dry plateau of Rammas Forod, marking the transition from Talath Muil’s drear uplands into a broader dry steppe plateau bordering the Wash Tundra and far north.[file:179][file:132]
land.biome_hint: cold, dry plateau edge with sparse grasses and low shrubs, leading towards tundra

regions.notes: Talath Muil, as framed here, is a cold, foggy, windswept upland between Arthedain and the far northern tundra, ecologically distinct from the more populated Evendim Hills and Emyn Uial. For your map and climate model (Third Age, pre-War of the Ring), treat it as a marginal, low-productivity biome with strong wind exposure, constant damp, and limited human presence, but with important military and ecological links to the Hills of Evendim, Rammas Forod and the Wash Tundra.[file:179][file:132]

regions.name: Fangorn
regions.description: Fangorn is an ancient forest on the southeastern slopes of the Misty Mountains, bordered to the north by the plains running to the Silverlode (Celebrant), to the west by the high peaks, to the east by the Wold and to the south by the open plains of Calenardhon. It is the last great remnant of the immense forest that once covered much of Eriador and Calenardhon and is uniquely tended by the Ents, who act as tree-herds and guardians of this 'giant garden'. The module also treats the surrounding borderlands along the Entwash and Limlight as part of the same regional system.

climate.description: Fangorn’s climate is broadly similar to the adjacent plains and foothills—cool-temperate with cold, moist winters, warm humid summers and stormy springs—but the dense canopy creates a buffered microclimate under the trees, with reduced wind, moderated temperatures and high humidity. The borders of Fangorn share the same general regime but transition quickly to more open, wind-exposed conditions in the Wold and Calenardhon.
climate.temperature_pattern: Winters are cold and moist with normal snow cover; light snows sometimes fail to penetrate the canopy, leaving only treetops white. In heavy snowfalls, an 'igloo effect' occurs: temperatures under the trees become warmer than beyond the forest edge, making Fangorn’s eaves a refuge for plains animals during particularly harsh storms. Spring brings rapid temperature oscillations as thunderstorms rise over the Misty Mountains in minutes, but under the boughs conditions are somewhat muted. Summers are warm and, because of tree cover, oppressively humid for Men—'like a great terrarium'—though ideal for Ent nurseries. Autumn is cool with fairly regular patterns, occasionally disrupted by storm branches from coastal systems sweeping through the gap between mountain ranges.[file:154]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Precipitation is frequent year-round. Winter brings cold, moist conditions and regular snow; tree cover often intercepts light falls so only heavy storms reach the forest floor. Spring thunderstorms build quickly over the mountains, bringing intense but localized rain, wind and lightning. Summer humidity arises from repeated rainfall combined with limited evaporation under the canopy. In autumn, fairly regular rains dominate, with rare but intense storms carrying large quantities of water and wind through the mountain gap, soaking the woods and borderlands.[file:154]
climate.notes: The climate section emphasizes how Fangorn’s closed canopy decouples under-canopy conditions from the open plains: snow and wind impacts are reduced beneath the trees, while humidity rises. This explains both the Ents’ preference for the forest and the tendency of plains fauna and travellers to seek shelter at the forest edge during severe winter storms despite Fangorn’s fearsome reputation.[file:154]

fauna.description: Fangorn’s fauna is a mix of typical Rohan plains and Misty Mountain foothill species with a distinctive overlay of Entish and Huorn-related hazards. The forest proper supports a rich assemblage of forest animals, while the borders of Fangorn host herds, predators and human or semi-human groups whose presence shapes encounter tables and ecological interactions.

creatures.name: Ents (Onodrim)
creatures.note: The Onodrim (Ents) are the defining 'fauna' of Fangorn—huge, intelligent, tree-like beings acting as tree-herds and guardians. While classed as a people in the module, ecologically they function as keystone megafauna, pruning, transplanting and protecting trees, and violently eliminating threats (e.g., Orc bands devoured by Black Huorns) as described in the narrative introduction.[file:154]

creatures.name: Huorns (active trees)
creatures.note: Huorns, especially Black Huorns, form groves capable of destroying entire bands of Orcs; Orcs know them as 'tree demons'. They are semi-sentient or fully sentient trees that move and attack, forming deadly thickets and ambush zones in the darker parts of Fangorn.[file:154]

creatures.name: Forest mammals and birds
creatures.note: The fauna section (4.13) notes a variety of forest mammals and birds typical of cool-temperate woodlands: deer, boar, wolves, small herbivores and numerous birds, all thriving in the rich, Ent-groomed 'giant garden'. Many species take advantage of the forest edge’s shelter in winter storms, reflecting the 'igloo effect' where temperatures under the trees are higher than outside.[file:154]

creatures.name: Plains herds and predators at the borders
creatures.note: The borders of Fangorn (section 4.2) describe the Wold and Calenardhon plains where herds of horses, cattle and other grazers roam, alongside predators such as wolves and mounted raiders (Calenardhons, bandits). These animals frequently interact with the forest, seeking shelter at the eaves or being preyed upon by Huorns and Ent allies.[file:154]

creatures.name: Misty Mountain foothill species
creatures.note: The western edge of Fangorn rises quickly into rocky, sloping hills and mountain foothills; fauna includes creatures adapted to stony terrain and higher altitudes, similar to those of the southern Misty Mountains, including goats, mountain predators and possibly rare highland species descending into the forest fringes.[file:154]

creatures.name: Master Animal Table creatures
creatures.note: The Master Animal Table (10.4) extends the bestiary with additional animals and encounter probabilities, integrating generic MERP fauna (bears, great cats, birds of prey, etc.) into the Fangorn context; these are intended to be localized according to whether the PCs are in dense forest, river edges, plains or foothills.[file:154]

flora.description: Fangorn is described as unique primarily because it has been carefully groomed for thousands of years by Ents; it is 'actually a giant garden'. Ent intervention has preserved and enhanced species diversity, produced unusual sectionalization and created groves where trees that would normally be outcompeted are given protected space. The flora of the borders transitions from forest to open plains and rocky hills.

plants.name: Ent-gardened canopy forest
plants.note: The soil in Fangorn is very rich and kept that way by the Ents, though it would be fairly fertile even without them. Massive overhead coverage of the forest results in very little undergrowth despite the rich soil. This Ent-managed canopy supports the largest representatives of many tree species in Middle-earth and defines the forest’s dark, towering interior.[file:154]

plants.name: Sectionalized groves by species
plants.note: Fangorn shows a 'high degree of sectionalization': old forests naturally develop patches dominated by particular species, but here the process is amplified by Ents, who deliberately sectionalize areas so that trees which would normally be choked out by dominant species can thrive in their own groves. This produces mosaics of single-species stands embedded within the larger forest.[file:154]

plants.name: Ent home-groves
plants.note: Ents habitually surround their homes with their favourite trees, causing 'strange growth patterns' in these zones. Trees from any locale can be planted around an Ent home, leading to out-of-place groves such as Mountain Ash or Aspen near the eastern border barely 1000 feet above sea level—species that would otherwise prefer higher elevations.[file:154]

plants.name: Limestone hills and short cliffs (eastern Fangorn)
plants.note: The eastern hills under Fangorn’s shadow are formed of limestone like the Wold but with heavier bedrock similar to the downs. Weather and streams erode these rock hills into short cliff formations, exemplified by Treebeard’s watch where Merry and Pippin first meet him. These limestone structures support cliff-ledge vegetation and specialized shrubs adapted to thin soils.[file:154]

plants.name: Forest floor and undergrowth
plants.note: Despite rich soil, 'little undergrowth is seen' due to the massive overhead coverage of the forest. Shade-tolerant mosses, ferns and low plants dominate the forest floor, while thick leaf litter accumulates under the Ent-groomed canopy, providing fuel for Entish soil management and contributing to the forest’s damp, muffled character.[file:154]

plants.name: Borderlands vegetation (Wold and Calenardhon)
plants.note: Beyond the forest, the Wold and Calenardhon are open plains with grasses and low shrubs adapted to wind, variable moisture and grazing. Along the Entwash (Onodló) and Limlight (Limlaith), riparian vegetation forms green corridors: reeds, willows and other riverbank plants that connect Fangorn’s forest biota with the plains.[file:154]

people.description: Fangorn’s 'people' straddle the line between fauna and cultures: Ents and Huorns are central, while human and other groups inhabit or contest the borders along the Entwash and Limlight. The module splits these between 'Politics and Power: Fangorn' (Ents, Huorns) and 'Politics and Power: The Borders' (Calenardhons, Silvan Elves, Barz Thrugrim, etc.).

list.name: Onodrim (Ents of Fangorn)
list.description: The Ents are the ancient tree-herds of Fangorn—huge, slow-moving, deeply thoughtful beings who nurture and protect the forest. They maintain soils, sculpt groves, move trees and act decisively when roused, as seen in their legendary assaults on Orcs and Isengard. Treebeard, Skinbark and Leaflock are among the best-known leaders.[file:154]

list.name: Huorns
list.description: Huorns are semi-sentient or fully awakened trees that can move and act with will, especially in defense of the forest. Black Huorns form particularly dangerous groves capable of annihilating Orc warbands; to Orcs they are the dreaded 'tree demons'. They blur the line between plant and person in Fangorn’s ecology.[file:154]

list.name: Calenardhons (Men of Calenardhon)
list.description: The Calenardhons are Men inhabiting the plains south of Fangorn (precursors or analogues to the Rohirrim). The 'Borders' chapter (6.11) describes their settlements along the Entwash and their interactions with Fangorn, including trading, grazing near the forest and occasional conflicts or alliances with Ents and border powers.[file:154]

list.name: Silvan Elves of the borders
list.description: Silvan Elves are noted inhabitants of the border region, perhaps remnants or offshoots of Lórien or other woodland realms. They dwell in or near the forest edge, sometimes mediating between Ents and Men, sometimes pursuing their own hidden agendas in the liminal zone between Fangorn and the plains.[file:154]

list.name: Barz Thrugrim and other border factions
list.description: The module lists additional border powers such as the Barz Thrugrim (likely Dwarves or a specific Mannish clan, depending on interpretation), bandit groups and fortified sites like Tir Limlight. These factions exploit river traffic, trade routes and the protection (or threat) of Fangorn, adding human-scale politics around the forest.[file:154]

land.name: Fangorn Forest (core)
land.description: The core of Fangorn lies under the dense canopy between the southern Misty Mountain foothills and the eastern limestone hills. Rich soil, massive trees and Ent-managed sectionalization define this area, with minimal undergrowth and a dark, humid, terrarium-like atmosphere in summer. Ent homes, nurseries and Huorn groves are scattered throughout.[file:154]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate, closed-canopy old-growth forest with high humidity, rich soils and low undergrowth

land.name: Eastern limestone hills and Treebeard’s watch
land.description: On the eastern side of Fangorn, sloping rocky hills of limestone rise from the plains, forming short cliffs as weather and streams erode them. Treebeard’s watch, where Merry and Pippin meet him, is an example of these rock formations, which create natural lookouts and microhabitats within the forest edge.[file:154]
land.biome_hint: rocky limestone hills with short cliffs, forested slopes and patchy soil over bedrock

land.name: Misty Mountain foothills (western edge)
land.description: To the west, Fangorn quickly transitions into higher mountain foothills as it meets the Misty Mountains. The land rises through sloping, rocky ground into high combs where springs of the Entwash and Limlight emerge. Vegetation shifts toward more cold-tolerant species and open rocky areas near the treeline.[file:154]
land.biome_hint: cool, rocky foothills with mixed forest and open rock, source of major rivers

land.name: Entwash (Onodló) and Limlight (Limlaith) river corridors
land.description: Two major rivers, the Entwash and Limlight, arise from springs in the combs of the Misty Mountains and flow through Fangorn before entering the plains. Numerous tributaries join them inside the forest, carving channels and creating moist microhabitats along their routes. The Entwash in particular links Fangorn to the plains of Calenardhon and beyond.[file:154]
land.biome_hint: riparian forest and wetland strips within the larger forest, transitioning to open river plains

land.name: The Wold (eastern border)
land.description: To the east of Fangorn lies the Wold, a limestone-based upland or plain whose rocks and soils mirror those of Fangorn’s eastern hills. It is largely open, with grasses and low shrubs and only scattered trees, making a sharp visual and ecological contrast with the dense Entwood.[file:154]
land.biome_hint: open, windy limestone upland/steppe adjoining forest, with grassland and sparse trees

land.name: Calenardhon plains (southern border)
land.description: South of Fangorn stretch the open plains of Calenardhon, the future Rohan: broad grasslands used by the Calenardhons for herding and mounted warfare. These plains are broken only by rivers and occasional hills, providing a stark edge to the forest and a major corridor for human movements near Fangorn’s southern eaves.[file:154]
land.biome_hint: temperate grassland suitable for horse-herding and agriculture, with strong wind exposure

regions.notes: This object treats 'Fangorn Forest and Borders' as a single region with an Ent-managed ancient forest core, flanked by limestone hills and Misty Mountain foothills, and bordered by the Wold and Calenardhon plains. The climate and flora sections emphasize Fangorn’s uniqueness as a 'giant garden' with high tree diversity, sectionalized groves and minimal undergrowth, while the fauna and people sections highlight Ents, Huorns and mixed border populations. For your Middle-earth-over-Europe map, Fangorn should be mapped as a dense, humid, cool-temperate old-growth forest with strong microclimatic buffering, adjacent to continental grasslands and mountain foothills, and serving as a key ecological and cultural node between Rohan and the southern Misty Mountains.[file:154]
regions.administrative_organization: None apparent; property held in common.
regions.military: 150 Ents. 2,000 Huorns.

regions.name: West Rhudaur, Ettenmoors, Trollshaws, En Egladil, Lone-lands
regions.description: Rhudaur is the eastern successor state of Arnor, a rugged, sparsely settled land between the Ettenmoors, the Trollshaws and the Angle. It stretches from the Ettenmoors and Ettendales in the north down through the Pinefells and Trollshaws to the rolling lowlands between the rivers Mitheithel and Bruinen, forming a harsh frontier of jagged hills, evergreen forests, boggy vales and predator-haunted woodlands.

climate.description: Rhudaur’s climate is cool to cold, with long, harsh winters and mild to cool summers. The highlands (Ettenmoors, Ettendales, Pinefells) experience severe cold, frequent snow and persistent cold winds, while the Trollshaws and the Angle have a slightly milder, windy climate with rich but limited soils along riverbanks.
climate.temperature_pattern: The highlands of Rhudaur consist of large fells rising up to 1,500 feet above their bases; they are 'classic fells', young, jagged hills created by violent uplift, with little topsoil. Cold winds ('the cool winds and waters of this locale are legend') sweep the Pinefells/Coldfells, implying low winter temperatures with extensive snow cover and short, cool summers. The lowlands of the Angle, though somewhat more hospitable, still suffer constant northeast winds that strip topsoil; only sheltered riverbanks offer relatively fertile microclimates.[file:132]
climate.precipitation_pattern: The Ettendales are noted for poor drainage and many stagnant bogs, especially during spring melt, indicating heavy winter snowfall and significant spring runoff. The Mitheithel becomes a 'raging torrent' in spring as snows of the Ettenmoors and Ettendales melt, remaining swift and difficult to ford even in early fall. Constant rain cascading off the fells erodes topsoil in the Angle, suggesting frequent rainfall or drizzle in all but the coldest months.[file:132]
climate.notes: The combination of harsh winters, heavy snow and frequent storms in the highlands, plus persistent wind and rain in the lowlands, makes Rhudaur one of the less hospitable parts of Arnor despite its position south of the Ettenmoors. The climate strongly shapes land use: grazing and herding in the Trollshaws, limited agriculture only in protected riverine pockets of the Angle, and virtually no cultivation in the Ettenmoors and Ettendales.[file:132]

fauna.description: Rhudaur’s fauna is dominated by predators and hardy herd animals adapted to rugged hills, dense woods and boggy vales. Regional text emphasizes Trolls, wolves and the great herds of losrandir, along with a broader Eriador fauna of deer, boar, bears, smaller mammals and birds.

creatures.name: Hill Trolls
creatures.note: The Trollshaws take their name from the Hill Trolls who favor and inhabit them. These Trolls prowl the rugged hill country and wooded patches, often using the broken terrain and brushy edges of groves for ambush. They are the characteristic large predator of central Rhudaur’s woodlands.[file:132]

creatures.name: Forest Trolls (Coed Drug)
creatures.note: The most significant forest in Rhudaur, the Coed Drug ('Evilwood'), is home to Forest Trolls—smaller than other Trolls but numerous. They dwell in the ancient, dense stands where little light penetrates; stories say some places within are never touched by sun or moon, and both Elves and Hillmen fear to enter.[file:132]

creatures.name: Losrandir ('Snow-wanderers')
creatures.note: Rhudaur’s 'vast herds of losrandir' (Snow-wanderers) use the Trollshaws as their winter ground. They are large hoofed herd animals adapted to snow and rough, woody terrain; this is the only region in Rhudaur where the soil is rich enough to support such herds at scale, though even here it is poor compared to the rest of Eriador.[file:132][file:129]

creatures.name: Wolves
creatures.note: Heavy brush along the edges of Trollshaws groves and within the woods wherever trees are thin is described as 'quite popular with wolves.' Wolves use this brush to conceal themselves, hunting both losrandir and the flocks and travellers of Rhudaur’s Hillmen.[file:132]

creatures.name: Huge wolves (Coed Drug fringes)
creatures.note: The fringes of the Coed Drug are roamed by 'huge wolves', adding to the forest’s ill repute. These larger predators likely represent especially powerful wolf packs or Warg-like beasts associated with the latent evil of the forest.[file:132]

creatures.name: Trolls of the Ettenmoors
creatures.note: The northern highlands (Ettenmoors/Môr Ithvaen) are classic Troll-country: jagged fells with boulder fields ranging from child-sized rocks to villa-sized outcrops, forming 'crazy jumbles' through which passage is impossible for anything larger than a rabbit. This terrain favors Stone or Hill Trolls and other large, rock-associated predators.[file:132]

creatures.name: General animals of Eriador (deer, boar, bears, smaller fauna)
creatures.note: The Flora & Fauna chapter on 'Animals of Eriador' (3.2) covers typical regional fauna: red deer, boar, bears, wolves, foxes, small herbivores (rabbits, hares, voles) and numerous birds. Rhudaur’s forests, moors and bogs host this full suite, with predators particularly common due to sparse human settlement.[file:132]

creatures.name: Creatures of the Far North spillover
creatures.note: Rhudaur’s northernmost highlands border the Far North; the 'Creatures of the Far North' section (3.3) describes additional cold-adapted beasts (such as winter wolves or northern predators) that can occasionally enter the Ettenmoors and Ettendales, adding to the region’s danger in harsh winters.[file:132]

flora.description: Rhudaur’s vegetation ranges from boulder-strewn, almost barren high fells to evergreen-dominated Pinefells, dense and sinister ancient woods, and wind-scoured grasslands of the Angle. The Flora chapter’s 'Plants of Eriador' provides the broader context: cool-temperate forests, heathlands and riverine vegetation typical of Arnor, with Rhudaur’s specific plant communities shaped by its rough topography and climate.

plants.name: Ettenmoors (Môr Ithvaen) boulder-fields
plants.note: The Ettenmoors, or Môr Ithvaen ('Granite Sea'), are characterized less by vegetation than by rocks: boulders and granite outcrops ranging from child-sized stones to blocks the size of a large villa. Vegetation is sparse; where soil accumulates in cracks and hollows, hardy grasses, mosses and low shrubs cling to life amidst the granite jumble.[file:132]

plants.name: Ettendales bogs and springs
plants.note: South of the Ettenmoors, the Ettendales hold many springs feeding the River Mitheithel. Poor drainage and spring melt produce numerous stagnant bogs in the vales between hills, supporting bog-heath vegetation: sedges, moss, heather and other water-loving plants adapted to cold, wet soils.[file:132]

plants.name: Pinefells / Coldfells evergreen forests
plants.note: The highlands immediately south and east of the Ettendales form the Pinefells, or Coldfells; they are much like the Ettendales but are well-drained and heavily wooded. Most trees are evergreens, taking advantage of the cool, moist climate. The region’s name 'Coldfells' reflects its chilly winds and waters; evergreen conifers dominate, with scattered hardwoods in sheltered spots.[file:132]

plants.name: Trollshaws woodland mosaics
plants.note: The Trollshaws are primarily woodland scattered over rugged hill country. Trees gather in small dense patches with extensive open ground between them; heavy brush grows along grove edges and extends inward wherever trees thin, creating a mosaic of groves, shrub thickets and grassland favored by wolves and browsing herds.[file:132]

plants.name: Coed Drug ('Evilwood') ancient forest
plants.note: The Coed Drug is described as the most significant forest in Rhudaur—an ancient, dense pair of wooded stands above the Great East Road. Little light reaches the forest floor due to the great trees’ canopy, leaving the interior dim and gloomy; stories speak of places never touched by sun or moon. The atmosphere of 'latent evil' and tales of trees capable of malice suggest a dark, old-growth forest with massive trunks, intertwined branches and an understorey of shade-tolerant plants.[file:132]

plants.name: Angle grasslands and scattered trees
plants.note: The Angle consists of rolling grassy hills broken by jagged fells and occasional stands of trees. Constant northeast winds and rain off the fells strip topsoil, making agriculture difficult except in relatively protected, fertile areas along riverbanks. Vegetation is dominated by hardy grasses and low shrubs, with richer alluvial soils near the Mitheithel and Bruinen supporting limited cultivation.[file:132]

plants.name: Riverine vegetation along the Mitheithel
plants.note: The Mitheithel, born of the snows of the Ettenmoors and springs of the Ettendales, runs swift and clear. Along its banks, where topsoil accumulates and winds are buffered, riparian vegetation flourishes: grasses, reeds, shrubs and scattered trees forming narrow green bands through the otherwise wind-scoured Angle.[file:132]

people.description: Rhudaur’s population is sparse and concentrated in hill-country and strategic sites such as Cameth Brin and the Angle. Politically, it is the easternmost and least stable of Arnor’s successor realms, dominated by Hillmen and later influenced or controlled by Angmar. The Arnor book’s detailed cultural and political treatment is split across Arnor: The Land and Arnor: The People; here we focus on the geographic side, noting the Hillmen and Rhudaurrim as the characteristic inhabitants of this harsh landscape.

list.name: Hillmen of Rhudaur (Rhudaurrim / Môr Ithvaen clans)
list.description: The Hillmen are indigenous, short, dark, hardy folk inhabiting the rugged fells and highlands of Rhudaur. They exploit the jagged hills, boggy vales and wooded slopes for hunting, herding and raiding, and their tales emphasize the 'unknown dangers' of Trollshaws and Ettenmoors. Their raiding over the Oiolad ('Endless Plain') into more favored regions is a recurring theme in Arnor’s frontier conflicts.[file:132]

list.name: Rhudaurrim (mixed Dúnedain and Hillmen)
list.description: The term Rhudaurrim encompasses the kingdom’s mixed population: a small, historically ruling Dúnedain element and a larger Hillman base. Geographical descriptions note Rhudaurrim and Hillmen raiding across the Oiolad into neighboring lands, and their presence in central areas like the Trollshaws and Angle, where they share terrain with Trolls, wolves and losrandir.[file:132]

list.name: Trollshaws settlers and garrisons
list.description: Aside from Cameth Brin, few hills in the Trollshaws rise more than 800 feet, and permanent settlements are rare. The main human presence consists of fortified sites (e.g., Cameth Brin) and scattered farmsteads in safer pockets, always under threat from Hill Trolls, Forest Trolls and wolves.[file:132]

land.name: Ettenmoors (Môr Ithvaen, 'Granite Sea')
land.description: Northernmost highlands of Rhudaur, a landscape of large hills and boulder-strewn terrain created by the uplift of the Misty Mountains. Jagged, little-weathered fells hold very little topsoil; boulders from child-sized to villa-sized lie in 'crazy jumbles' through which passage is impossible for anything larger than a rabbit. The region is bleak, treacherous and ideal for Trolls and other large predators.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: cold, rocky highland with minimal soil, scattered hardy vegetation and extreme terrain

land.name: Ettendales
land.description: Hill country south of the Ettenmoors, with smaller hills and less granite. The Ettendales hold many springs feeding the Mitheithel but suffer from poor drainage; vales between hills contain stagnant bogs, especially during spring thaw.
