Despite their water resources, the bogs make travel dangerous and limit settlement.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: cool, boggy hill region with springs, stagnant wetlands and patchy grass/heath

land.name: Pinefells / Coldfells
land.description: Highlands immediately south and east of the Ettendales, similar in structure but better drained and heavily wooded. Dominated by evergreen forests, the region is known as the Pinefells; others call them 'Coldfells' due to the legendary cool winds and waters. It forms a heavily forested, cold highland belt between the Ettendales and Trollshaws.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: cool evergreen forested hills with well-drained soils and persistent cold winds

land.name: Trollshaws
land.description: Central Rhudaur between the Mitheithel and Bruinen, rugged hill country with woodland scattered across it. Trees form small dense patches separated by open ground; heavy brush along grove edges extends inward where canopy thins. Popular with wolves, it is also the winter ground for Rhudaur’s vast herds of losrandir and the favored haunt of Hill Trolls.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate mosaic of wooded groves, brush and open grassland in broken hill terrain

land.name: Coed Drug ('Evilwood')
land.description: The most significant forest in Rhudaur, growing on the southern side of the Trollshaws above the Great East Road. An ancient, dense dual stand of woods, it exudes unease and 'latent evil'. Home to Forest Trolls and huge wolves; its immense trees block most sunlight, leaving the interior perpetually dim. Some inner places are said never to be touched by sun or moonlight, and even Elves and Hillmen fear to enter.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: dark, old-growth forest with closed canopy, thick understorey and a supernatural atmosphere

land.name: The Angle (lowlands between Mitheithel and Bruinen)
land.description: Rolling grassy hills south of the Coed Drug and between the rivers, broken by jagged fells and occasional stands of trees. Constant northeast winds and rain off the fells strip away topsoil, so agriculture is difficult except along relatively protected and fertile riverbanks. It forms Rhudaur’s main lowland and agricultural zone, albeit poor compared to the rest of Eriador.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate grassland and light woodland with strong winds and limited fertile pockets along rivers

land.name: Oiolad ('Endless Plain' of the Eredoriath)
land.description: Flat plain west of the Mitheithel beyond Rhudaur’s lowlands, used as raiding ground by the Rhudaurrim and Hillmen into more favored regions. While technically outside core Rhudaur, it is closely linked to its economy and warfare, providing a contrast to the rugged homeland fells.[file:132]
land.biome_hint: open grassland/plain adjoining Rhudaur, serving as raiding and travel corridor

regions.notes: This object isolates Rhudaur as a region within Arnor: The Land, focusing on geographic subzones (Ettenmoors, Ettendales, Pinefells/Coldfells, Trollshaws, Coed Drug, Angle) and mapping the general Flora & Fauna of Eriador onto these specific landscapes. The key Rhudaur-specific ecological elements are the Pinefells evergreens, the Coed Drug ancient 'Evilwood', the losrandir herds and the concentration of Hill and Forest Trolls. Climate and hydrology are dominated by snow-fed highlands (Ettenmoors/Ettendales) and the swift Mitheithel, with constant winds limiting agriculture to narrow riverine bands.[file:132]
regions.administrative_organization: King by self-proclamation and use of force, Broggha holds the thirteen Hillman tribes together through sheer personal charisma. He appoints family members to most positions of responsibility, distrustful of any others. Tribal leaders retained their legal rights when they swore fealty to Broggha, but they surrendered any claim to land ownership to him.
regions.population: 50,000: 35,000 Hillmen in thirteen tribes and 15,000 Dunlendings in eleven tribes.
regions.military: 4,500 Warriors and Eastern Mercenaries, usually grouped according to tribe.

regions.name: Tol Fuin, Ardolyalinya, Lairiardhon, Lildardhon, Ardquetto, Ascahiriand, Mänoriand, Celanoriand, Apariach, Sandariand, Vinyalas
regions.supplement: Lindon: The Kingdom of the Elves (fan-made Realm #3003)
regions.description: Lindon is the westernmost Elvish realm of Middle-earth, the remnant of Ossiriand that survived the War of Wrath. After the breaking and flooding of Beleriand, the Great Sea breached the Blue Mountains to form the Gulf of Lhûn, dividing Lindon into Forlindon (North Lindon) and Harlindon (South Lindon), with the River Lhûn as the eastern border against Arnor. Throughout the Second and early Third Age it is the principal Elven coastal realm and home of the Grey Havens (Mithlond). Geology consists of folded sedimentary rock (slate and sandstone) with rich iron lodes and abundant coal deposits in forlindon.

climate.description: Lindon has a cool-temperate, oceanic climate strongly moderated by the Elven Sea (Belegaer) and shaped by the Blue Mountains and Gulf of Lhûn. Coastal areas around Mithlond enjoy mild winters and cool summers with abundant rain, while the western and eastern slopes of the Ered Luin have colder winters, moderately warm summers and similar annual precipitation. Mean annual temperature: 50-60° F. Average low: Ninui 15° F. Average high: Úrui 75° F. Average annual precipitation: 40-60 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: The Traveller’s Guide climate table distinguishes three representative bands in western Eriador: (1) Duneriador-type inland zones with average lows around 0° in Ninui (late winter) and highs near 80° in Ivanneth (early autumn), mean 45–60°; (2) Ered Luin with winter lows down to -10° in Narwain and summer highs about 80° in Urui, mean 35–50°; and (3) the 'Elven Sea' coastal strip (Mithlond and nearby shore) with lows about 15° in Ninui and highs around 75° in Urui, mean 50–60°. These values place Lindon squarely in a maritime-temperate regime, with harsher winters restricted to the mountains.[file:99]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Annual precipitation in both Duneriador-like inland zones and the Ered Luin is about 30–40 inches, while the Elven Sea coastal band receives 40–60 inches due to moist sea air and orographic lift on the Blue Mountains. Rain is frequent year-round, with snow common in the Ered Luin during severe winters such as the Long Winter and Fell Winter mentioned in the realm’s history.[file:99]
climate.notes: The climate table is framed for a broader western Eriador context, but for modeling Lindon it is reasonable to map the 'Elven Sea' band to the Forlindon–Harlindon coastal strip and the Ered Luin band to Rhûnlindon and the Dwarven halls in the Blue Mountains. Population and route descriptions also stress that rivers were guarded by carved watch-stones and that coastal storms off Belegaer periodically impacted Lindon’s harbors.[file:99]

fauna.description: The Lindon document focuses almost entirely on history, culture and demographics; it does not provide a dedicated flora/fauna chapter. Nevertheless, the climatic bands and references to shipbuilding, forests and mountain mines imply a fauna typical of cool-temperate coastal and mountain regions of western Eriador.

creatures.name: Coastal marine life of the Elven Sea
creatures.note: The 'Elven Sea' climate band around Mithlond, with mild temperatures (50–60° mean) and high precipitation (40–60 inches), implies rich cold-temperate marine ecosystems supporting Elven fishing and maritime culture at the Grey Havens and secondary ports such as Harlond and Forlond.[file:99]

creatures.name: Forest and mountain wildlife (Ered Luin)
creatures.note: The Ered Luin climatic values (Narwain lows near -10°, Urui highs around 80°, 30–40 inches of precipitation) indicate coniferous and mixed forests on the Blue Mountains, sustaining deer, boar, wolves, bears and other wildlife comparable to other mountain regions in western Eriador, alongside Dwarven mining and Elven refuges.[file:99]

creatures.name: Riverine and estuarine species of the Lhûn
creatures.note: Lindon’s description emphasizes the River Lhûn and Gulf of Lhûn as key geographic and defensive features, with watch-stones guarding river crossings. This context implies migratory fish, waterfowl and estuarine fauna similar to those found in Gondorian havens, forming a basis for Elven fishing and harbor life.[file:99]

flora.description: Although a botanical catalogue is absent, Lindon’s climate and geography support coastal evergreen and mixed forests around the Gulf of Lhûn, mountain forests and heaths in the Ered Luin, and riparian vegetation along the Lhûn and its tributaries. Sections on carpentry, architecture and shipbuilding stress Elven use of specific woods and materials, implying well-managed forests.

plants.name: Coastal evergreen and mixed forests
plants.note: The Elven Sea coastal band’s mild, wet conditions (50–60° mean, 40–60 inches of rain) favor dense evergreen and mixed forests along Lindon’s shores and lower hills, providing timber for shipbuilding and protective woodland belts around Mithlond, Harlond and Forlond.[file:99]

plants.name: Mountain forests of the Ered Luin
plants.note: The Ered Luin’s cooler climate (35–50° mean, frequent winter snow) supports conifer-dominated forests and hardy deciduous trees on both western and eastern slopes, forming the backdrop for Dwarven mines and Elven refuges mentioned in the Traveller’s Guide and appendices.[file:99]

plants.name: Riparian belts and small forests (Taeg Lindon routes)
plants.note: The Wandering Companies’ routes from Imladris to Mithlond cross Cardolan, the South Downs and the borders of the Shire, where the guide notes small forests scattered in grasslands, quiet camps in stands of trees, and rustic refuges with roofs and sometimes walls. These descriptions imply riparian woods along rivers like the Mitheithel and sheltered forest patches across the route corridor leading into Lindon.[file:99]

people.description: Lindon’s population is overwhelmingly Elvish, composed of multiple lineages (Noldor, Sindar, Laiquendi, Nandor, Falathrim) under Gil-galad and later Círdan, with a significant Dwarven presence in the Blue Mountains and peripheral contact with Men and Hobbits via routes and harbors. The Traveller’s Guide provides detailed population tables showing long-term decline through the Third Age.

list.name: Falathrim (Sea-Elves)
list.description: Descendants of the ancient mariners of the Falas, the Falathrim are the primary coastal Elves of Lindon, inhabiting Mithlond, Harlond and Forlond and maintaining shipbuilding and ocean-voyage traditions. They form the backbone of Lindon’s maritime culture and operate the Grey Havens as the main port to Valinor.[file:99]

list.name: Godhellim (Noldor of Lindon)
list.description: Noldorin Elves (Godhellim) under Gil-galad, primarily residing in Forlindon; they provide high leadership, craft and lore. Population tables show their proportion shrinking steadily from the Second to the late Third Age as many depart for Aman, while Sindar and Nandor proportions rise.[file:99]

list.name: Iathrim and Laegrim (Sindar and Laiquendi)
list.description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindon’s woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]

list.name: Lindellim and other Nandor/Mithrim houses
list.description: Nandor Elves (Lindellim) and Mithrim-related northern Sindar houses contribute additional Elven lineages within Lindon. The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and Rhûnlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of Lhûn; Thorin’s Halls (Durin’s Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]

list.name: Hobbits and Men along the Wandering Companies’ route
list.description: Hobbits of the Shire and Men of Cardolan do not form part of Lindon’s resident population, but the Traveller’s Guide describes their lands along the Wandering Companies’ 600-mile route from Imladris to Mithlond via Eregion, the South Downs, Barrow-downs, Old Forest and Brandywine. Tooks in Harlindon and Shire traditions about departures from the Grey Havens illustrate ongoing contacts.[file:99]

land.name: Forlindon (North Lindon)
land.description: The northern part of Lindon west of the Gulf of Lhûn, including coastal plains and the northern Blue Mountain foothills. Historically the primary home of the Noldor (Godhellim) in the realm, it contains harbors and settlements connected to Mithlond and is treated in the government section as a distinct administrative region.[file:99]
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate coastal and lowland zone with maritime influence transitioning to colder mountain foothills

land.name: Harlindon (South Lindon)
land.description: The southern part of Lindon west of the Gulf of Lhûn, associated with Sindar and Laiquendi (Iathrim, Laegrim). It combines coastal stretches and forested lowlands, was once a fief under Celeborn, and is portrayed as more rural and woodland-oriented than Forlindon.[file:99]
land.biome_hint: mild, wet coastal strip with forests and rural Elven settlements around secondary harbors

land.name: Rhûnlindon (east-of-Lhûn / Blue Mountains)
land.description: The part of Lindon’s sphere east of the River Lhûn, including the eastern Blue Mountains with Dwarven halls and some Elven refuges. Population tables assign Rhûnlindon about 10% of Lindon’s population, with climate corresponding to the Ered Luin band (colder winters, moderate summers, 30–40 inches of precipitation).[^file99]
land.biome_hint: cool to cold mountain zone with conifer forests, winter snow and extensive mining

land.name: Mithlond (Grey Havens)
land.description: The principal harbor city at the head of the Gulf of Lhûn, seat of Círdan in the Third Age and the main port for Elves and Ring-bearers departing to Valinor. Population tables show Mithlond comprising 20–25% of Lindon’s inhabitants in the Third Age (and 9–10% in the Second Age), emphasizing its central role in Lindon’s demography and economy.[file:99]
land.biome_hint: maritime urban enclave with sheltered harbor, mild wet climate and intensive shipbuilding

land.name: Taeg Lindon routes and refuges
land.description: The 'Bounds of Lindon' section describes the Wandering Companies’ preferred route from Imladris to Mithlond: across Eregion, the Mitheithel ford south of Fennas Drúnin, the South Downs and Barrow-downs (with stops near Tom Bombadil’s house), then either through the Old Forest and Green Hills or along the Brandywine’s eastern shore before turning west to the Ered Luin. Along roughly 600 miles they maintain rustic camps and more elaborate permanent refuges (glades, caves, small houses) 20–30 miles apart, some shared with Rangers after the fall of Arthedain.[file:99]
land.biome_hint: mixed grassland, forest and hill country between the Misty Mountains and the Ered Luin, with hidden Elven refuges

regions.notes: This Lindon work is an unlicensed, non-commercial fan-made Realm (version 3.2, 2023) rather than an original ICE product, but it is structured like a MERP Realm and synthesizes Tolkien canon with MERP-style material. For your Middle-earth-on-Europe map, Lindon should be treated as one macro-region with internal subregions (Forlindon, Harlindon, Rhûnlindon) sharing a maritime temperate climate, and Mithlond explicitly marked as a major harbor node. Climate numbers (temperature ranges and precipitation) from the Traveller’s Guide can be mapped directly onto your climate system for coastal vs mountain bands, while the Wandering Companies’ route description provides a canonical overland corridor from Imladris to the Grey Havens for your path and distance calculations.[file:99] Eerily beautiful remnants of Beleriand, characterized by rugged coastal hills, fertile valleys, and evergreen forests. A light rain, drizzle, or ethereal fog falls on most days of the year, allowing mosses, ferns, firs, and spruce to thrive.
regions.elevation: average: 500 feet. lowest point: Felaigram (sea level at low tide, 2 fathoms below at high tide). highest point: Amon Draug 1,634 feet.
regions.administrative_organization: Círdan rules as a common Lord over the nobility of Forlindon and Harlindon, and his rule is acknowledged as a wise and peaceful one by all parties. Nobles live in pleasant and serene estates, sharing their bounty with their subjects.
regions.military: 2,000 Warriors and Sailors (Forlindon: 800 Warriors/Sailors, 800 Levy. Harlindon: 1,200 Sailors/Warriors, 800 Militia).
regions.symbol: Círdan's Device: A White Pelannun on a Blue Field

regions.name: North Misty Mountains, Central Misty Mountains, Coldfells, Wilderness of Arnor, Central Anduin
regions.description: The High Pass (Cirith Forn en Andrath, S. 'High-climbing Pass of the North') cuts through the central Misty Mountains connecting Rivendell and Eriador in the west to the upper Anduin Vales and Rhovanion in the east. It encompasses the Orcish stronghold of Goblin-gate, the eyrie of the Great Eagles, the Northman market-town of Maethelburg, the western foothills of Rhudaur, and the broad river plain of the Anduin. Geology consists of limestone and granite with massive igneous basalt intrusions. western foothills consist of red sandstone, while eastern valleys contain oily black shale; rich in copper, iron, silver, gold, and mithril.

source.pdf: MERP 8070 - Goblin Gate and Eagle's Eyrie.pdf

climate.description: The region lies far from the moderating influence of the Great Sea, giving it a continental character: cold winters, mild-to-warm summers, and brief, sudden springs and autumns. The Misty Mountains themselves create a strong climatic divide between the wetter western foothills and the drier eastern rain-shadow of the Anduin Vales. Mean annual temperature: 32-42° F. Average low: 15° F. Average high: 65° F. Average annual precipitation: 80+ inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: Temperature drops at approximately 3.6°F per 1,000 feet of altitude (up to 5.5°F when air moves rapidly upslope). High mountain vales are colder than exposed slopes at the same altitude because cold air sinks and pools there. The lower High Pass (c.3,500 ft) and upper mountain-path (over 5,000 ft) are frigid in winter and subject to sudden cold snaps in any season. The Anduin Vales at lower elevation are mild to warm in summer and cold in winter, with more extreme temperature ranges than the foothills due to rain-shadow dryness.[file:88]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Prevailing moist westerlies from the Great Sea collide with cold northerlies from the Ice Bay of Forochel along the western mountain flanks, producing alternating drizzles, cold driving rains, and heavy snowfall in winter. As air rises up the slopes it cools and condenses, generating the perpetual mist and cloud that names the range (Hithaeglir). The Anduin Vales are spared the worst of this, receiving drier conditions under the rain-shadow, supplemented by cool winds from the Ered Mithrin and channeled westerlies through the Gap of Rohan to the south. Both branches of the High Pass are normally blocked by snow from Hithui to Gwirith (late autumn to early spring).[file:88]
climate.notes: Summer thunderstorms generated by clashing warm and cold air masses are severe and dangerous on the mountain heights. At the lower elevations of the Anduin Vales, winters are cold but navigable; at the pass summit, even experienced mountaineers face life-threatening conditions if caught unprepared.[file:88]

fauna.description: The region supports a full temperate mountain and river-valley fauna, ranging from common prey animals and river birds in the Anduin Vales to apex predators and unique creatures in the high peaks and caves. Underground, a distinct subterranean ecology has developed in the cavern systems beneath the mountains.

creatures.name: Great Eagles
creatures.note: Largest birds in Middle-earth, wingspan 25–30 ft, four times the size of common eagles; descended from divine spirits sent by Manwë to observe the mortal world. Golden-brown plumage, hooked beak and knife-like talons; dive at 200 mph; eyesight eight times keener than a man's. Carnivores ranging over territories of 200+ sq miles. Mate for life; lay one egg per decade; live for centuries. Intelligent, capable of speech in Westron, noble but proud and aloof. Their eyrie is a vast bowl-shaped nest up to 40 ft in diameter on an inaccessible pinnacle. The only natural enemies of Great Eagles are the winged Dragons of the Ered Mithrin.[file:88]

creatures.name: Wargs
creatures.note: An unusually large and intelligent wolf breed, 8–9 ft long including tail, dense grey fur; believed created by Morgoth in the Elder Days. Superior senses of smell, sight and hearing; can run at 30 mph. Fearless carnivores hunting in family packs of ~6, occasionally combining into huge packs. Territorial over hundreds of square miles. Coordinate hunts with a language of howls; use ambush and entrapment in deep snow. Often ally with Orcs in raiding, allowing themselves to be ridden by smaller goblins. Mate for life; litters of 4–14 pups born in spring. Dwell primarily on the eastern side of the Misty Mountains, crossing into Rhudaur when food is scarce.[file:88]

creatures.name: Great Brown Bears
creatures.note: Omnivorous apex predators of the mountain slopes; five-toed with long claws, thick shaggy fur; can rear to 10 ft on hind legs and weigh up to three-quarters of a ton. Omnivores (plants, roots, berries, fish, rodents, large prey); solitary loners without fixed territory; normally shy but extremely dangerous during summer mating season and when cubs are present. Hibernate in winter. One of the most ancient inhabitants of the region, predating even the Stone Giants. The Beorning shape-changers take the form of great bears.[file:88]

creatures.name: Stone Giants (Stan-eotenas)
creatures.note: Enormous beings 10–15 ft tall with greyish, craggy skin resembling stone; of obscure origin, possibly related distantly to Ents or the original stock from which Trolls were bred. Inhabitant of the high mountain vales since the First Age. Only a few dozen scattered throughout the mountains; reclusive and hostile to intruders. During thunderstorms, Giants are seen at their most formidable, hurling boulders and bellowing challenges. Have no real allies or enemies; avoid contact with other races and are equally avoided by Ents and Trolls.[file:88]

creatures.name: Cave Trolls, Hill Trolls, Stone Trolls
creatures.note: Multiple Troll varieties inhabit the region: Cave-trolls lurk in the deep caverns of the mountains; Hill-trolls stalk the lower mountain slopes; Stone-trolls (the most numerous) range through the broken lands of Rhudaur. All are 9–11 ft, immensely strong, fearless in combat, and turned to stone by direct sunlight. Created by Morgoth in mockery of Ents.[file:88]

creatures.name: Red deer, reindeer, bighorn sheep, mountain goats
creatures.note: Herds of swift red deer wander the grassy Anduin plain; reindeer graze the Rhudaur heather highlands; bighorn sheep occupy lower mountain slopes and vales (distinctive thick curling horns, mating contests in autumn); mountain goats inhabit ledges and cliffs above the tree line, covered in thick white fur—extraordinary climbers.[file:88]

creatures.name: Giant Vampire Bats
creatures.note: A unique, noxious bat species of the Misty Mountains; wingspan of a foot or more, black, roosting amidst common bats. Hunts in swarms; known to accompany Orc war parties and feed on the wounded and slain.[file:88]

creatures.name: Common bat colonies
creatures.note: Large colonies of small, harmless insectivore bats roost in the stalactites of mountain caves, navigating by ultrasonic echo; emerge for nocturnal hunting.[file:88]

creatures.name: Subterranean troglodytes
creatures.note: Nameless, ancient creatures in odd corners of the deeper caves—slimy, nosing about in the dark—of uncertain nature (reptile, degenerate mammal, or something older). Pale blind fish swim in underground lakes; cave-adapted rodents also present. Alluded to in The Hobbit: 'other nameless things... slinking and nosing about.'[file:88]

creatures.name: River and wetland birds
creatures.note: Ducks, geese and swans are plentiful along the broad Anduin; kingfishers perch on river banks; great horned owls roost nocturnally in hollow trees of the woodlands.[file:88]

creatures.name: Pheasants, quails, beavers, rabbits
creatures.note: Pheasants roam the high grasses of meadows; quails through forest underbrush; beavers build dams on secluded streams; rabbits, voles and hares are ubiquitous wherever vegetation grows, sustaining larger predators.[file:88]

flora.description: The region spans several distinct vegetation zones from the broad river meadows of the Anduin through the forested foothills and moorland of Rhudaur to the coniferous mountain slopes and the bare, lichen-covered high peaks. Unique cave fungi form their own ecosystem underground.

plants.name: Anduin riverside lush grasses and wildflowers
plants.note: The cultivated and semi-wild Anduin plain supports lush high grasses and wildflower-rich banks along the river and its adjoining marshes; tall reeds grow in the wetland margins.[file:88]

plants.name: Elm and oak groves (Anduin Vales)
plants.note: Groves of broadleaf trees—elms and mighty oaks—rise amid the fair meadows of the Anduin Vales, the most characterful and picturesque vegetation of the eastern lowlands.[file:88]

plants.name: Ancient pines (Rhudaur valleys and hillsides)
plants.note: The sheltered valleys and hillsides of Rhudaur are thickly forested with gloom-shrouded ancient pines; exposed hills and moorland are carpeted in scraggly heather, with rare wildflowers growing from treacherous bogs.[file:88]

plants.name: Coniferous forests (lower mountain slopes)
plants.note: Extensive coniferous forests climb the ridges and slopes along the base of the Misty Mountains, thriving in cooler temperatures and thinner soils where broadleaf trees cannot survive. They give way above the tree line to scrub bushes and grasses, then to lichen, bare rock and permanent snow.[file:88]

plants.name: Alpine meadow flowers (high vales, late spring)
plants.note: Rolling meadows of grasses and wildflowers bloom spectacularly in late spring in the higher mountain vales above the conifer line, creating a brief but vivid carpet of color between snowmelt and the return of frost.[file:88]

plants.name: Cave fungi (Goblin-gate underground)
plants.note: Only fungi can survive in the lightless caverns beneath the mountains; it assumes fantastic forms and frequently attains astounding size. Rumors describe forests of colossal mushrooms, phosphorescent nodules and other growths that defy description. The Orcs harvest these fungi as a food source in the lower cave levels of Goblin-gate.[file:88]

plants.name: Medicinal herbs
plants.note: Both the sheltered river vales and the harsh mountain climate support differing medicinal herb species; they command high prices in market towns but are easily depleted if overharvested.[file:88]

people.description: The region is sparsely populated by several distinct peoples: Northmen (Nenedain) in the Anduin Vales, Beornings on the eastern bank, Hillmen in the rough hills of Rhudaur, and Orcs in the mountain strongholds, alongside rarer presences of Elves, Dwarves, Giants and Eagles.

list.name: Nenedain (Northmen of the Anduin Vales)
list.description: Tall, strong, fair, hardy men descended from the same ancestral stock as the Edain; they form the majority of the settled population of the Anduin Vales, living as farmers, pastoralists and merchants. Culturally influenced by Gondor through trade, they have developed minor urban centers like Maethelburg along the Anduin but remain mostly a scattered rural folk. Decimated by the Great Plague (T.A. 1635–36), which killed about half their number. On good terms with Dúnedain and tolerable terms with Hillmen; deeply hostile to Orcs.[file:88]

list.name: Beornings
list.description: An ancient and unique Northmen clan centered around the Holy Carrock on the eastern Anduin bank; the largest of all Northmen, as tall as Dúnedain and broader. Vegetarians and legendary bakers (honey-cakes of Wilderland fame). Customarily shy and solitary but passionately jovial among kin; berserkers in battle. A rare few are hereditary shape-changers who can assume the form of great bears; the chieftain (Wealdanbera, 'Ruling Bear') is always a shape-changer, presiding over the secretive bear-cult (Beracynn).[file:88]

list.name: Hillmen of Rhudaur (NeDreubhan)
list.description: Short, dark, stocky men of the rugged hills of Rhudaur, distantly related to Dunlendings; superb mountaineers but deeply suspicious of outsiders, prone to treachery and ambush. Worship vengeful petty deities with blood sacrifice. Traditional enemies of both Dúnedain and Northmen; greatly fear Elves. Some served in the hosts of the Witch-king of Angmar alongside Orcs, an uneasy alliance. Survive as hunters, gatherers and occasional brigands in the broken terrain.[file:88]

list.name: Orcs of Goblin-gate (Uruk-tarkhnarb)
list.description: The Orcs of the High Pass, led by the Great Goblin (Durba-hai, 'People-ruler'); an autonomous Orcish tribe paying nominal tribute to Angmar and Mount Gundabad. Composed of common Orcs (3–5 ft) and an elite of Uruk-hai (5–6 ft), who act as leaders and bodyguards. Expert miners, smiths and warriors; operate primarily at night (common Orcs are weakened by daylight; Uruk-hai are unaffected). The tribe holds the mountain passages by force and extorts or destroys all who cross without safe-conduct.[file:88]

list.name: Great Eagles of Eagles' Eyrie
list.description: The Great Eagles under their Lord hold a distinct political power in the region—not mere animals but intelligent beings of near-divine descent, capable of speech in Westron. They intervene selectively in the affairs of the Free Peoples when the cause is just, acting as informal agents of Manwë's will. Proud, aloof and not easily persuaded, they are nevertheless noble at heart and permanent allies to any who earn their gratitude.[file:88]

list.name: Stone Giants
list.description: Ancient, solitary beings of the high mountain vales; present since the First Age, the true indigenous inhabitants of the peaks. They avoid contact with all other races and are equally avoided; dangerous to encounter, especially during mountain thunderstorms when they hurl boulders in play. Not formally allied with or hostile to the Free Peoples, but deeply territorial.[file:88]

land.name: Anduin Vales (eastern lowlands)
land.description: The broad valley plain east of the Misty Mountains, gently sloping eastward and southward; easily traversed open terrain with grassy meadows, river marshes, elm and oak groves, and scattered Northman homesteads and trading towns. Maethelburg is the main settlement, a fortified hill-town at the confluence of the Anduin and Sirros (Foamflood). The Great River here is the main highway, plied by Northman cnearras (shallow-draught clinker-built craft) trading between Maethelburg and Gondor.[file:88]
land.biome_hint: temperate river valley with lush meadows, broadleaf groves, wetland margins and open farmland

land.name: Western Foothills of Rhudaur
land.description: The rugged, forested foothills and moorland west of the Misty Mountains in the territory of Rhudaur; grim stony heights, deep ravines, ancient pine-shrouded valleys and heather-covered moorland, cut by treacherous bogs. Home to Hillmen clans and prowled by Stone-trolls and Orcs at night; nearly impassable without a guide except along the Great East Road (which ends at the Ford of Bruinen).[file:88]
land.biome_hint: cold Atlantic-type rugged moorland and forested ravines with bog, heather and ancient conifer cover

land.name: High Pass (Cirith Forn en Andrath)
land.description: A double-branched pass through the Misty Mountains connecting Rivendell to the Anduin: the lower branch (Pass of Imladris, c.3,500 ft) permits horses and wagons but is controlled by Goblin-gate at its summit; the upper mountain-path (over 5,000 ft) is passable only on foot or by mule/pony, steep and exposed to storms but less Orc-infested. Both branches are snow-blocked from Hithui to Gwirith. The pass is riddled with scree, avalanche risk, tumbling boulders and the constant threat of Giants above and Orcs below.[file:88]
land.biome_hint: subarctic alpine pass with permanent snowcaps, glaciers, scree slopes and fierce storm exposure

land.name: Goblin-Gate (Goblin-town underground)
land.description: A vast network of natural limestone caverns expanded and connected by Orcish mining over millennia, stretching up to 40 miles between the Wolf Gate (main western entrance at the pass summit) and the Back Door (eastern exit to the Anduin slopes), with further passages to the Fungi Caves, Mine levels, and reputed deep shafts connecting to the Underdeeps of Moria. At T.A. 1640: the Great Goblin rules from the City Level; Gollum has not yet arrived (that happens c.T.A. 2470). Underground lakes, underground rivers, stalactites, ice caves (upper levels), massive fungi forests (lower levels) and deep mine shafts all present in the complex.[file:88]
land.biome_hint: karst limestone cave system with ice caves (upper), inhabited city levels, fungi caves, and deep mine shafts

land.name: Eagles' Eyrie (Amon Thyrir pinnacle)
land.description: A solitary rock pinnacle rising above the mountain crags east of the High Pass, inaccessible except by flight; crowned by the vast bowl-shaped nest (up to 40 ft diameter, several tons) of the Lord of the Eagles. The surrounding high peaks and thermal columns of warm rising air are the domain of a small population of Great Eagles, each controlling a territory of 200+ sq miles of mountain terrain.[file:88]
land.biome_hint: bare alpine rock pinnacle above the tree line, surrounded by high-altitude thermals and glaciated peaks

land.name: Giants' Isle (mountain vale)
land.description: A secluded area of high mountain vales where Stone Giants are concentrated; largely avoided by all other peoples. The Giants hurl boulders during thunderstorms and have occupied these heights since the First Age. No settlement is possible here.[file:88]
land.biome_hint: remote high-altitude vale with rock, scree, glacial streams and no human habitation

regions.notes: This module is centered on T.A. 1640 (primary time period), with additional adventures set in T.A. 2940 (time of The Hobbit, when Gollum is already in the caves and Thorin's company passes through) and F.A. 1. The fauna and flora data reflect the T.A. 1640 baseline. Goblin-gate's internal geography is partially procedurally generated (Random Features Table in the module) and not fully fixed on maps—only key route passages are mapped. The region connects westward to Rivendell/Eriador and eastward to northern Mirkwood via Maethelburg and the Old Ford, making it a critical chokepoint on the Misty Mountains.[file:88] The longest and highest mountain chain in the west, serving as a formidable glacial barrier between Eriador and Rhovanion. Heavy snows and glaciers cover peaks above 11,500 feet year-round. It is riddled with Orc-holds, Dwarven caverns, and the under-deeps.
regions.elevation: average peak elevation: 10,000-12,000 feet. lowest peak: Fëaras 7,983 feet. highest peak: Caradhras 16,002 feet.
regions.political_organization: Independent settlements, each ruled by an elite warrior lord.
regions.administrative_organization: Each clan strives for selfreliance, meeting with the others only for occasional trade and in times of crisis. Within the clan, all power rests with the clan chief, a warrior of noble lineage (frequently the descendant of the previous leader) chosen by all adult males.
regions.military: 100 Beornings. 60 Burgwards of Maethelburg. 900 Militia (3-4 weeks to raise, organized by clan). 135 Eagles (allies).
regions.products: Grain, hides, cattle, honey, amber, tin.

regions.name: Harondor
regions.description: Harondor is the arid, contested plateau between the Poros river and the Vale of Harnen, bounded by the Ephel Dûath to the east and the Belfalas coast to the west; a perpetually disputed borderland between Gondor and the Haruze/Umbarean powers, drier and hotter than the Gondorian heartland. Geology consists of igneous bedrock, with basalt common near the coast and granite further inland. valuable coastal salt panning is a major industry.

climate.description: Harondor has a semi-arid to arid climate—hot and dry in summer, mild to cool in winter—transitional between the temperate Gondorian coast and the true desert of Harad to the south. Mean annual temperature: 60-70° F. Average low: Narwain 40° F. Average high: Ivanneth 105° F. Average annual precipitation: 20-40 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: Summers are notably hotter and drier than coastal Gondor, with temperatures frequently exceeding 90°F on the Aegardh plateau; winters are mild but can bring cold nights, and the Ephel Dûath blocks moisture from the east.[file:73]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Northward winds from the Bay of Belfalas bring occasional rainfall to Harithilien on the western Ephel Dûath face, but the Aegardh plateau and most of Harondor is dry steppe; the southern zone near the Harnen grades into sandy scrub desert with scattered dunes (bursk country).[file:73]
climate.notes: Volcanic soils from the Ephel Dûath make the Vale of Harnen and Harithilien relatively arable despite aridity, an exception to the otherwise impoverished upland geology; the Ephel Dûath contain some placer gold streams.[file:73]

fauna.description: Harondor's fauna is typical of hot, open steppe and semi-desert: gazelles and antelope in the Aegardh, lions in the Harnen gorge, nomadic flocks, and migratory wildlife moving between Harad and Gondor through the Harnen corridor.

creatures.name: Gazelle and antelope
creatures.note: Gazelles and antelopes enter the Aegardh plateau from the Vale of Harnen, attracting hunters—including Gondorian nobles seeking game from Harondor's wilder districts.[file:73]

creatures.name: Lions
creatures.note: Lions are confirmed inhabitants of the upper Harnen gorge, preying on the seasonal flocks of Haruze nomads who migrate into Harondor; they are the primary large predator of the region.[file:73]

creatures.name: Nomadic livestock (flocks, camels)
creatures.note: Haruze nomads' sheep and goat flocks migrate seasonally between Nurn and Harondor through the Harnen valley; domesticated animals dominate much of Harondor's visible animal life by the Third Age.[file:73]

creatures.name: Steppe and desert fauna
creatures.note: The bursk (scrub desert) areas in southern Harondor support typical arid-zone animals: reptiles, small rodents, birds of prey and scavengers moving between the steppe and the nearby Haruze lands.[file:73]

flora.description: Harondor's vegetation shifts from thin, grassy steppe on the Aegardh plateau to sandy scrub desert in the south, with the notable exception of the Harnen and Poros valleys which carry richer volcanic-soil agriculture.

plants.name: Steppe grasses (Aegardh)
plants.note: The upland Aegardh plateau is covered by flat, grassy steppes cut by canyons and wadis; natural grass cover supports the seasonal migrations of gazelle, antelope and nomadic flocks.[file:73]

plants.name: Scrub desert vegetation (bursk country)
plants.note: Southern Harondor grades into sandy scrub desert (bursk) with sparse, drought-adapted shrubs, succulents and dune grasses similar to the Haruze lands beyond the Harnen.[file:73]

plants.name: Date palms and sorghum (Nan Harnen)
plants.note: The Vale of Harnen, with its volcanic soils, supports date palms and sorghum domesticated by the Haruze, the most productive agricultural zone in an otherwise arid region.[file:73]

plants.name: Riparian vegetation (Harnen, Poros, Carnen)
plants.note: Gallery vegetation—tamarisks, willows and reeds—lines the banks of the Harnen, Poros and Carnen rivers, providing the main cover and shade in an otherwise open landscape.[file:73]

people.description: Harondor is a contested borderland with no stable dominant power in T.A. 1640; Gondorian border rangers, Haruze nomads, Eraguk steppe-folk and Umbarean agents all claim influence at different times.

list.name: Eraguk (steppe nomads)
list.description: Fierce steppe nomads of mixed Haruze and Nórniag lineage, the Eraguk roamed the Aegardh since ancient times as the original inhabitants; after centuries of Gondorian pressure, most were driven east or assimilated, surviving in small, often bandit-style groups by T.A. 1640.[file:73]

list.name: Haruze migrants (Nan Harnen)
list.description: Haruze farmers and nomads who have occupied the Vale of Harnen, growing sorghum and herding flocks, moving seasonally between Harad/Nurn and the southern Harondor plateau; a persistent cultural presence even when Gondor claims political control.[file:73]

list.name: Gondorian border rangers and garrisons
list.description: Gondor maintains border fortresses like Amon Eithel and patrols the Hyarmenti road to keep the Aegardh nominally under control; these rangers are thin on the ground and frequently harassed by nomads, Haruze and Umbarean agents.[file:73]

list.name: Umbarean agents
list.description: After the Kin-strife, Umbarean and Haruze captains encouraged nomadic clans to migrate into the Aegardh, using Harondor as a staging ground against Gondor and the Hyarmenti trade road.[file:73]

land.name: Aegardh (Fell Region)
land.description: The dry, flat upland plateau of central and eastern Harondor, cut by canyons and narrow wadis (Emyn Gonngaran in the northeast); historically home to the Eraguk nomads and now a contested zone of Gondorian border control and periodic banditry.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: hot dry steppe with thin grasses, canyon systems and sparse nomadic pasture

land.name: Bursk country (southern Harondor)
land.description: Sandy scrub desert with dunes in southern Harondor, transitional between the Aegardh steppe and the true Great Desert of Harad; dry and inhospitable, crossed only by the Harnen corridor.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: semi-arid to arid scrub desert with scattered dunes, sparse shrubs and heat-adapted wildlife

land.name: Vale of Harnen (Nan Harnen)
land.description: The valley of the Harnen river running east to west across southern Harondor, enriched by volcanic Ephel Dûath soils and supporting date palm orchards and sorghum fields; the main cultural corridor between Gondor and Harad.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: semi-arid river valley with volcanic soils, irrigated agriculture and riparian gallery woodland

land.name: Harithilien
land.description: A distinct zone on the western face of the Ephel Dûath north of the Harnen, watered by northward bay winds and enriched by volcanic soils; relatively arable compared to the Aegardh and historically contested between Gondor and the powers of Mordor.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: semi-arid to dry upland with volcanic-soil fields and scrub, moderated by orographic rainfall

regions.notes: Harondor is treated as a separate region from the Gondorian heartland due to its clearly distinct biome (steppe-desert vs. temperate coastal), political instability and frontier character; it is the transitional zone to Near Harad and should be linked with that region for route and climate continuity.[file:73] Best described in three zones: a fertile coastal strip receiving sufficient rain to support cedar, oak, juniper, and palm with domestic crops (olives, dates, grapes, pomegranates); vegetated dune-fields, badlands, and hilly shrublands inland; and an arid desert interior of active dunes and salt flats.
regions.elevation: average: 600 feet. lowest point: sea level. highest point: Amon Halitan 5,602 feet.

regions.name: Andrast, Hairavercien, Anfalas, Lamedon
regions.description: Southern Gondor comprises the fertile river valleys, coastal plains, highland vales and mountain flanks between the White Mountains (Ered Nimrais) and the Bay of Belfalas, encompassing the provinces of Lebennin, Belfalas/Dor-en-Ernil, Lamedon, Anfalas and the Pinnath Gelin—the heartland of Númenórean civilization in Middle-earth.

climate.description: Southern Gondor has a temperate maritime climate—warm, moderately wet summers and mild, rainy winters—very close to a Mediterranean pattern, with slightly harsher conditions in mountain vales and a drier, hotter character toward Harondor in the southeast.
climate.temperature_pattern: Coastlands range roughly 35–50°F in midwinter (Narwain) up to 70–85°F in midsummer (Cerveth–Urui); inland vales are similar but slightly cooler in winter; mountain habitable zones (1,000–3,000 ft) run about 5–15°F cooler at all seasons, with snow common in winter.[file:73]
climate.precipitation_pattern: The coast and inland vales receive moderate to normal rain year-round, heaviest in autumn (Narbeleth) and throughout winter and spring (Hithui–Gwirith); summer months (Cerveth–Urui) are the driest with only moderate rain; mountains follow the same pattern but with heavier precipitation and snowfall in winter.[file:73]
climate.notes: The Bay of Belfalas generates highly variable sea winds—from dead calm to hurricane-force—and autumn is statistically the most dangerous season for mariners; coastal storms driven by Bay and ocean systems can bring heavy rain at any time of year.[file:73]

fauna.description: The natural fauna of Southern Gondor has been heavily modified by millennia of Númenórean settlement and agriculture, surviving mainly in upland preserves, river mouths and the sea; domesticated animals dominate the lowlands and farmland.

creatures.name: Wolves and black bears
creatures.note: Wolf packs prowl highland vales of the Ered Nimrais, preying on wild mountain goats; black bears wander the timberline, though hunted almost to extinction by the Orodbedhron clans of the mountains.[file:73]

creatures.name: Lions
creatures.note: Lions have been sighted in the Ephel Dûath foothills and are confirmed in the upper Harnen gorge, where they prey on nomadic flocks; their presence in the Ered Nimrais is rumored but unconfirmed, possibly a story spread to protect sacred Daen sites.[file:73]

creatures.name: Cathfiar (feline hunter)
creatures.note: A large, described forest cat native to the woodland preserves of Southern Gondor, illustrated in the module as a significant wild predator of remaining forested areas.[file:73]

creatures.name: Mountain goats
creatures.note: Wild mountain goats are the primary prey of wolves and bears in the upland vales of the Ered Nimrais, inhabiting ledges and cliffs above the timberline.[file:73]

creatures.name: Elk (Caru) and deer
creatures.note: Herds of elk and caru from Enedhwaith winter in the remote western vales of Anfalas and Andrast after migrating through the Cirith Nimrais; deer and roe are hunted throughout protected woodlands.[file:73]

creatures.name: Wild boar and pheasant
creatures.note: Wild boar and pheasant live in remaining forest preserves; hunting them is a privilege of the nobility, with commoners largely excluded except in the most remote western districts.[file:73]

creatures.name: Aurochs
creatures.note: A species of aurochs native to Anfalas was hunted nearly to extinction by the Dúnedain during the second millennium of the Third Age; by T.A. 1640 it survives only in very remote areas.[file:73]

creatures.name: Domesticated animals (sheep, cattle, horses)
creatures.note: Multiple breeds of Númenórean sheep are grazed in Lamedon and the Pinnath Gelin; cattle, goats and pigs occupy farmland valleys; horses are common but mostly imported from Rhovanion or Khand, not native to the region.[file:73]

creatures.name: Kirinki (crimson songbirds of Númenor)
creatures.note: Small crimson songbirds brought from Númenor by the Faithful before the Downfall, they survive in the Ered Torthonion range of Belfalas—the only place in Middle-earth they can be found.[file:73]

creatures.name: Swans and sea otters
creatures.note: Swans, sacred to Ulmo and Ossë, glide the Morthond-Ringló estuary; sea otters inhabit the outflow of the Gilnduin-Serni estuary.[file:73]

creatures.name: Marsh adders, crocodiles and Tûmlaship (fishing cat)
creatures.note: The swampy Ethir Anduin delta holds deadly marsh adders and crocodiles (locally called 'swamp dragons'), plus the tûmlaship—a semi-intelligent, human-friendly fishing cat unique to the delta.[file:73]

creatures.name: Marine life of the Bay of Belfalas
creatures.note: The Bay of Belfalas sits on the continental shelf and teems with life: seals, dolphins (friends of Uinen), gulls and seabirds on cliff-faces, pearl-bearing mollusks in the shallows of Methed Lebennin, annual whale migrations along the western Anfalas coast, and sea turtles nesting on Tolfalas; rarely, deep-sea creatures including krakens may be encountered.[file:73]

flora.description: Most native vegetation in Southern Gondor has been replaced by farmland after millennia of Númenórean settlement; remaining wild flora is concentrated in mountain vales, protected woodlands and estuaries, with key species of great cultural and economic significance.

plants.name: Oak, beech and birch forest remnants
plants.note: The ancient oak, beech and birch forests that once covered Southern Gondor survive as protected hunting preserves, mostly in upland vales of the Ered Nimrais where the land was unsuitable for agriculture.[file:73]

plants.name: Stone pines and mast-pines
plants.note: A mixture of hardwoods and stone pines occurs in lower mountain valleys of the Ered Nimrais; the tall mast-pines of the Ered Torthonion and upper Lefnui valley provided timber for the Ship-kings' fleets, carefully replanted under royal mandate to sustain the resource.[file:73]

plants.name: Lebethron
plants.note: A dark, dense hardwood found in the higher mountain forests of the Ered Nimrais in Lebennin, prized above all others for making ship-masts for the great vessels of Southern Gondor and the lances of Belfalas knights.[file:73]

plants.name: Firs and conifers (upper vales)
plants.note: Higher mountain valleys are covered mostly with fir and conifer forest; above the timberline, large rolling meadows of grass and wildflowers bloom spectacularly in late spring.[file:73]

plants.name: Athelas (Kingsfoil)
plants.note: The healing herb athelas, brought from Númenor, grows in the western mountain valleys of Lebennin, planted by the Dúnedain near all their settlements; it is most commonly found in the valley glens alongside other herbs and flowers.[file:73]

plants.name: Gwingyrn (Elven ship-timber)
plants.note: Sacred timber trees growing in Eryn Laegol near Edhellond, given to the Elves of that haven specifically for ship-building; the Elves take only trees gifted by Ossë for this purpose, treating all others as protected beings.[file:73]

plants.name: Oiolair of Tolfalas
plants.note: A sacred, rare tree that grows only on the volcanic island of Tolfalas and is revered by the Dúnedain; none may touch it without leave of its guardians, the Princes of Belfalas.[file:73]

plants.name: Mallos and alfirin (white lilies, golden bells)
plants.note: The famous flowers of Lebennin—white mallorn-lilies (mallos) and golden-belled alfirin—sway in the coastal wind from the Sea, growing in the green fields watered by the rivers Celos and Erui.[file:73]

plants.name: Agricultural crops (cereals, pulses, fruit trees)
plants.note: Lebennin and the central plains produce the full range of Númenórean agriculture: cereals, pulses, oil-producing plants, fruit trees, vegetables and tubers, most introduced from Númenor or cultivated with Dúnedain guidance.[file:73]

plants.name: Date palms and sorghum (southern coast)
plants.note: Date palms from the Haruze and sorghum domesticated in Nan Harnen appear along the southern coasts of Belfalas and in the Vale of Harnen, crops encountered as Gondor expanded south in the Third Age.[file:73]

plants.name: Salt-tolerant plants and marsh reeds (Ethir Anduin)
plants.note: The broad estuarine delta of the Ethir Anduin supports marsh vegetation—reeds, sedges and water plants—forming an important ecosystem for the delta's distinctive wildlife.[file:73]

people.description: Southern Gondor is the most populous and civilized part of Gondor, dominated by Dúnedain nobility and the Daen-Dúnedain mixed population of the river plains, with Daen highlanders (Orodbedhrim) in the upland vales, swamp peoples in the Ethir Anduin, and Grey-elves at Edhellond.

list.name: Dúnedain of the Faithful
list.description: The Númenórean descendants of the Faithful who settled Lebennin, Belfalas and the river valleys, governing the realm from Pelargir, Dol Amroth and other citadels; tall, long-lived and sea-faring, they form the nobility and officer class.[file:73]

list.name: Danan Lin / Daen folk (lowlands)
list.description: The indigenous pre-Númenórean people of the coastlands and plains, shorter and stockier than the Dúnedain, who adopted Westron and much of Númenórean culture while retaining their oral traditions and ballads; they form the majority of the common population of Lebennin and Anfalas.[file:73]

list.name: Orodbedhrim / Daen Coentis (highlanders)
list.description: Mountain clans of the White Mountains living above the timberline in primitive groupings; semi-nomadic goat shepherds and fisherfolk on the coasts (Dunir of Andrast), they have largely avoided cultural contact with Gondor and still honor ancient Daen traditions.[file:73]

list.name: Sakalai (Ethir swamp peoples)
list.description: Small, dark-haired, independent tribes in the Loeg Balimur and remote Ethir Anduin, believed to be of mixed Daen and Southron stock; they live as swamp fishermen largely outside Gondorian authority.[file:73]

list.name: Grey-elves of Edhellond
list.description: A remnant Sindarin community dwelling at the fabled haven of white swan-ships near the mouth of the Morthond, closely allied with the Princes of Belfalas; guardians of the Eryn Laegol and builders of the most beautiful ships in Southern Gondor.[file:73]

land.name: Lebennin (Land of Five Rivers)
land.description: The heartland of Southern Gondor, a fertile central plain watered by the Gilnduin, Celos, Serni, Erui and their tributaries; the most populous and productive province, dominated by farmland with boggy bottomlands near the rivers and conifer-covered mountain valleys to the north.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: temperate fertile lowlands with mixed agriculture, river floodplains, boggy bottomland and mountain-fringe conifer forests

land.name: Dor-en-Ernil / Belfalas
land.description: The rocky peninsula and coastal province of the Prince of Belfalas, containing the Ered Torthonion and the citadel of Dol Amroth; silver-rich mountains, the sacred kirinki birds and the Elven haven Edhellond define its character.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: rocky Mediterranean-type coast with mountain mineral wealth, pine forests and maritime influence

land.name: Anfalas and Pinnath Gelin
land.description: A long, thin coastal land west of the Morthond with flat, waterlogged plains, fens and rich fishing grounds; the Pinnath Gelin highlands to the north support wool-producing sheep and a timber-mining economy.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: low coastal plain with fens, salt marshes, fishing grounds and rolling highland pasture

land.name: Lamedon
land.description: Mountain province of the White Mountains rich in iron, copper and silver deposits; a militarized zone in early Third Age history, later transformed into a province supporting mining communities and highland farming.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: forested mountain vales and upland pastures with mineral-rich geology

land.name: Cape of Andrast
land.description: The rugged, rain-battered westernmost cape of Southern Gondor where the White Mountains bend into the sea; steep-sided green valleys and bare peaks home to the Dunir (Orodbedhrim), essentially untouched by Gondorian civilization.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: wet Atlantic-type rocky cape with steep valleys, heavy rainfall and primitive settlement

land.name: Ethir Anduin (Great Delta)
land.description: The vast, meandering delta of the Anduin where it meets the Bay of Belfalas, created by the Númenórean Downfall's Great Wave; a unique ecosystem of swamps, channels, marsh and tidal flats with crocodiles, fishing cats and swamp tribes.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: subtropical-type estuarine delta with marshes, swamps, tidal channels and rich aquatic biodiversity

land.name: Tolfalas
land.description: A volcanic island in the Bay of Belfalas with dramatic topography, hot springs, active minor eruptions and the sacred oiolair trees; settlement has always been marginal due to geological instability.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: geologically active island with volcanic soils, hot springs, endemic sacred trees and nesting sea turtles

land.name: Bay of Belfalas
land.description: The great bay forming the southern coast of Gondor, sitting on the continental shelf and rich in marine life; critical for trade, fishing and naval power but plagued by unpredictable storms and Corsair threats.[file:73]
land.biome_hint: warm temperate sea with rich coastal fisheries, marine mammals, whale migration routes and volatile weather

regions.notes: Southern Gondor's natural environment has been extensively reshaped by Númenórean colonization; the biomes encoded here reflect the Third Age T.A. 1640 state, not a pristine natural landscape; the most 'wild' zones are the Cape of Andrast, Ethir Anduin delta, upper mountain vales and Tolfalas. Harondor (the arid plateau south of the Poros) is treated as a separate region.[file:73]

regions.name: Talath Uichel
regions.description: Talath Uichel, the Plain of Everlasting Cold, is the vast tundra interior east of Forochel, structured into green belts and herding grounds, while beyond its northern edge lies the Landless Land, the polar ice-cap desert at the very end of Middle-earth.

climate.description: Talath Uichel has a classic sub-arctic tundra climate with short summers and long winters, while the Landless Land is a true polar desert where mean monthly temperatures never rise above freezing and light alternates between months of day and months of night.[file:39]
climate.temperature_pattern: Across Talath Uichel, average summer highs range around 40–60°F with lows near freezing, while winters run from about 10°F down to −40°F; in the Landless Land temperatures remain below 32°F all year, with severe wind-chill making perceived temperatures much lower.[file:39]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Talath Uichel receives moderate snowfall, especially where moist air from Forochel and Eriador meets cold polar air; bogs and fens in the Lakeland and Wash Tundra remain waterlogged in summer. The Landless Land gets very little precipitation, mainly as dry, sand-like snow particles blown by constant winds.[file:39]
climate.notes: Angmar’s meddling with weather and the changed global circulation after Númenor’s fall create highly unstable storm tracks and jet-stream behaviour, leading to sudden blizzards, deadly cold snaps and unusual oscillations between polar easterlies and mid-latitude westerlies.[file:39]

fauna.description: Talath Uichel is the main land of herds and predators—reindeer, elk, musk ox, wolves, bears and smaller mammals—while the Landless Land supports only a few highly adapted species and supernatural beings, mainly Snow-elves and Morgoth’s legacies.[file:39]

creatures.name: Reindeer (wild and domestic)
creatures.note: Immense reindeer herds dominate the Herd Tundra and migrate through Wash Tundra and Lakeland, with Ystvät Talven domesticizing some for meat, milk, hides and traction.[file:39]

creatures.name: Elk, moose and deer
creatures.note: Elk and deer inhabit forested belts along rivers and the Eriadorian frontier, while moose favor boggy forests and wetlands of the Lakeland and Fen Tundra.[file:39]

creatures.name: Musk oxen
creatures.note: Musk oxen occupy open tundra in Talath Uichel, grazing lichens and coarse grasses even in winter and serving as key prey for wolves and human hunters.[file:39]

creatures.name: Small mammals and birds
creatures.note: Lemmings, mice and rabbits live under the snowpack year-round; spring and summer bring vast numbers of mosquitoes and gnats plus migratory ducks, geese and other waterfowl to lakes and fens.[file:39]

creatures.name: Wolves and Wargs
creatures.note: Common wolves, larger dire wolves, grey wolves and white northern wolves range across Talath Uichel, some packs allied with Angmar and Wargs near its borders; they are primary predators on herds and travelers.[file:39]

creatures.name: Bears (black, snow and cave)
creatures.note: Black bears haunt wooded rivers and the frontier; snow bears roam open tundra and ice-fields; rare cave bears lurk in remote highlands and cavern systems.[file:39]

creatures.name: Snow leopards, lynx and foxes
creatures.note: Snow leopards stalk high ground near ranges like the Bleak Mountains, while lynx and foxes spread throughout Wash and Herd Tundras taking small game and scavenging.[file:39]

creatures.name: Snow Trolls
creatures.note: Snow Trolls, a northern Troll variant, appear in the Bleak Mountains, Stone Tundra and Landless Land; they are strong, cold-resistant and only vulnerably inert under continuous summer daylight.[file:39]

creatures.name: Legacies of Morgoth and Angmar
creatures.note: Dragons, Orcs, undead and unique horrors like Jnainen the Siren of the Ice and Eloeklo the Demon of the North Wind prowl parts of Talath Uichel and the polar regions, posing threats beyond mere climate.[file:39]

creatures.name: Polar-adapted species
creatures.note: The Landless Land itself supports very limited wildlife: some Snow Trolls, occasional polar predators and Snow-elves’ mounts, with most life concentrated along edges and rare oases like Helloth.[file:39]

flora.description: Talath Uichel carries the classic progression from frontier forest to open tundra and fire-heated enclaves, with herbs and fungi of great value to healers and locals; the Landless Land has virtually no vegetation beyond microscopic life and rare lichens near its margins.[file:39]

plants.name: Frontier forests (spruce, fir, birch, willow)
plants.note: On the Eriadorian frontier and Wash Tundra, glaciated hills slowly softened into soils anchored by grasses, then shrubs and firs, and finally mixed conifer and birch forest along valleys and streams.[file:39]

plants.name: Tundra grasses, sedges and wildflowers
plants.note: Across Herd Tundra and Lakeland, short, hardy grasses and sedges dominate, with patches of thistle, sage and seasonal wildflowers that briefly turn the plains into colourful carpets in spring and summer.[file:39]

plants.name: Lichen heaths
plants.note: Extensive lichen mats cover higher, drier ground, especially toward the Forsaken Sea and near the highlands, forming vital winter forage for reindeer and musk ox.[file:39]

plants.name: River moss (Jokisammal / Edram)
plants.note: Dark-green moss along rivers of the Wash Tundra, Everhir and Lakeland; eaten in pastes to aid healing of fractures and internal injuries.[file:39]

plants.name: Spearleaf (Keihänlehti / Neithlas)
plants.note: Silver-green bushes found in Wash Tundra, Lakeland, Evermist and Ered Rhívamar valleys; their leaves in poultice accelerate healing of bruises and minor wounds.[file:39]

plants.name: Keltakukka (Yellow Flower / Arfandas)
plants.note: Yellow blossoms of Wash Tundra and rare spots near the Everhir; used in medicine with other plants to double healing speed for broken bones.[file:39]

plants.name: Mustakuu fungus
plants.note: A dark, peculiar fungus unique to Talath Uichel and eastern tundras; when prepared correctly, it repels or distracts the swarming tundra insects that torment summer travelers.[file:39]

plants.name: Fire Tundra plants (Unikukka, Sinithti)
plants.note: Around Morgoth’s Well, geothermal warmth supports heat-loving species like Unikukka and Sinithti that would otherwise die in the far north, creating a pocket of high botanical interest.[file:39]

people.description: Talath Uichel and the Landless Land are the main domains of the Ystvät Talven tundra folk, Snow-elves in their icy city, Angmar’s sled-hordes and scattered frontier groups from Eriador; very few others can endure the conditions.[file:39]

list.name: Lumimiehet (Lossoth tundra clans)
list.description: The archetypal Snowmen of southern lore, the Lumimiehet are nomadic Ystvät Talven who range the Wash, Herd and Lakeland Tundras, following herds and living in small family bands bound into animal-totem clans.[file:39]

list.name: Jämiëhet
list.description: A land-focused Ystvät Talven sub-culture relying heavily on herd management and inland hunting, occupying central Talath Uichel and interacting with both Lossoth and Angmar’s frontier forces.[file:39]

list.name: Ystvät Talven hunter–herders (general)
list.description: Across Talath Uichel, Ystvät Talven communities time their movements to cycles of herd migration, fur hunting and fishing, organizing life around kinship, totems and seasonal camps.[file:39]

list.name: Snow-elves of Helloth
list.description: The Lossidil Snow-elves maintain Helloth, an enchanted ice city on the Landless Land, and patrol the polar wastes and Talath Uichel’s far north, guarding Helecthil’s radiance and intervening occasionally in mortal affairs.[file:39]

list.name: Umli
list.description: Bearded folk of mixed Mannish–Dwarvish appearance who have migrated from eastern lands into parts of Talath Uichel, where they adapt to tundra life and trade with Ystvät Talven and Elves.[file:39]

list.name: Berninga frontier folk
list.description: Bear-cult Men associated with the Beornings on the Eriadorian frontier, occupying the Rammas Forod and Northern Marches, grazing sheep, trapping and acting as transition communities between forest and tundra.[file:39]

list.name: Angmarim and the Sled-horde
list.description: The Witch-king’s forces in Talath Uichel include Urdic Sled-horde warriors, Trolls, Wargs and Orcs, raiding Lossoth and guarding Angmar’s northern approaches while remaining limited in number by the harsh land.[file:39]

land.name: Talath Uichel (Plain of Everlasting Cold)
land.description: The core tundra plain formed from the shattered bedrock of the Iron Mountains, structured into the Wash Tundra near Eriador, the central Herd Tundra ‘Land of Good Hunting’ and the northern Lakeland of rivers and lakes near the Forsaken Sea.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: vast sub-arctic tundra with permafrost, bogs, heaths and large herbivore herds

land.name: Wash Tundra
land.description: The southern green belt bordering Eriador, with more forest, wetlands and human contact than other parts of Talath Uichel, serving as staging ground for traders and frontier folk.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: mixed forest–tundra mosaic with rivers, bogs and relatively rich game

land.name: Herd Tundra (Hyvämetsästykseen Maa)
land.description: The central, long plain reaching from Forochel’s shores to the watershed of Angmar, drained by the Everhir and Lhîchir, and underlain by frozen soil without underground drainage, riddled with bogs and shallow streams.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: open grass–lichen tundra with permafrost, seasonal waterlogging and massive herd migrations

land.name: Lakeland
land.description: The northern green belt near the Forsaken Sea and southeast of the Bleak Mountains, rich in lakes, fens and forests that support diverse wildlife and Ystvät Talven settlements.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: tundra with dense lake and wetland networks, conifer stands and heavy insect/fowl activity in summer

land.name: Eriadorian Frontier (Rammas Forod, Northern Marches)
land.description: The jumbled hills and scarped downs where the old Iron Mountains descended into Eriador, later softened by soil, forests and herds; now a hard frontier zone with abandoned Angmar-haunted valleys and hardy Rivermen using northbound canoe routes.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: cold steppe and forested hills transitioning into tundra and hosting mixed Eriador–Forodwaith flora and fauna

land.name: Landless Land (polar ice-cap)
land.description: The polar ice-cap covering Ekkaia’s waters, with permanent and multi-year pack-ice that thickens and thins seasonally; average monthly temperatures stay below freezing, snowfall is scant and distinctive light–dark cycles dominate the year.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: true polar desert with thick sea-ice, minimal life, extreme cold and unique psychological/visibility hazards

land.name: Bleak Mountains and Stone Tundra
land.description: Raised and fractured when the Stone Tundra sank at Angband’s fall, these ranges and plateaus form harsh, wind-scoured highlands between Talath Uichel and the Landless Land, shelters for Snow Trolls and other monsters.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: elevated, rock-dominated cold deserts with thin vegetation and extreme exposure

land.name: Fire Tundra and Morgoth’s Well
land.description: A geothermal anomaly within Talath Uichel where hot springs, geysers and volcanic remnants create warm ground and exotic vegetation around the crater of Morgoth’s Well.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: local warm enclave with unusual plant communities amid otherwise frigid tundra

regions.notes: Use this region for large-scale herd migrations, overland routes, Angmar–Arthedain maneuvering and polar expeditions; coastal and marine gameplay, iceberg travel and bay whaling belong primarily to the Forochel region.[file:39]

regions.name: Forochel
regions.description: The Cape and Bay of Forochel are a rough, cold and storm-wracked peninsula and bay thrusting into Belegaer and the Encircling Sea, the last remnant of lands once lying beyond the Iron Mountains, scarred by Morgoth’s malice but rich in marine life, whaling, furs and treacherous ice. Geology consists of barren shelf of granite worn smooth by the passage of great ice sheets, with little accessible mineral wealth.

climate.description: Forochel’s climate is only slightly harsher than Talath Uichel’s, moderated by the surrounding seas but subject to bitter natural storms from the Landless Land; to southerners its summers feel cold and its winters lethal.[file:39] Mean annual temperature: 30-45° F. Average low: Nínui -20° F. Average high: Ivanneth 75° F. Average annual precipitation: 40-55 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: Summer temperatures along the bay commonly hover between about 40–50°F by day and around freezing at night, while winter averages drop toward 10°F down to −40°F in severe spells, with rapid swings driven by shifting sea storms and cold air masses off the polar ice.[file:39]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Coastal storms from the Encircling Sea, Bay of Forochel and Belegaer can bring heavy snow, sleet and occasional rain; pack-ice forms in autumn along the northern coasts, making travel and navigation hazardous until it thickens in deep winter, then breaks up dangerously again in spring.[file:39]
climate.notes: Weather in the bays and coastal areas is extremely unpredictable; even Ystvät Talven find it hard to read the sky, and non-natives face very hard penalties to forecast daily conditions, especially the onset of storms and ice break-up.[file:39]

fauna.description: Forochel is dominated by marine and coastal life—seals, walruses, whales and seabirds—plus tundra herbivores and predators moving between the cape and Talath Uichel, with Morgoth’s monsters and Angmar’s minions adding further danger.[file:39]

creatures.name: Seals and walruses
creatures.note: Huge populations of seals and walruses haul out on pack-ice and rocky shores, providing meat, oil and ivory to the Ystvät Talven and southern whalers, and drawing sharks, demon whales and other predators to the bays.[file:39]

creatures.name: Great whales
creatures.note: Humpback, killer, pilot and sperm whales frequent the Bay of Forochel and adjacent seas, hunted by Merimetsästäjät berg-dwellers and Cardolanian whalers for oil, meat and bone.[file:39]

creatures.name: Coastal and pelagic monsters
creatures.note: Fell turtles, ice-drakes, water-drakes and demonic whales appear as rare but devastating encounters in Forochel’s icy waters, threatening ships and berg-delvings alike.[file:39]

creatures.name: Reindeer, elk and musk ox
creatures.note: Reindeer herds migrate between Forochel’s tundra fringes and Talath Uichel’s plains, elk haunt sheltered coastal forests and musk oxen browse lichens across open inland tundra.[file:39]

creatures.name: Wolves and bears
creatures.note: Packs of wolves—common and white northern varieties—range the shoreline and tundra, hunting reindeer and seals on the ice, while black bears prowl forested valleys and snow bears patrol the cape’s interior and nearby ice-fields.[file:39]

creatures.name: Snow leopards and other predators
creatures.note: Snow leopards inhabit highlands such as the Ered Rhívamar bordering Forochel, hunting wild sheep and goats; foxes, lynx and weasels round out the predator communities.[file:39]

creatures.name: Birdlife and fish
creatures.note: Forochel’s coasts host dense colonies of seabirds feeding on abundant fish, while migratory waterfowl crowd estuaries and bays in the brief summer.[file:39]

creatures.name: Snow Trolls and Morgoth’s legacies
creatures.note: Snow Trolls, Orcs, dragons and other legacies of Morgoth sometimes appear around Forochel’s edges and ancient ruins, though they are more common inland and to the east.[file:39]

flora.description: Vegetation in Forochel is sparse but varied: stunted conifers and birch in sheltered valleys, tundra grasses and lichens inland, and rare herbs adapted to the salty, cold coastal climate.[file:39]

plants.name: Coastal conifers
plants.note: Spruces, firs and larches grow along river valleys and sheltered fjords, especially on south-facing slopes, providing timber and fuel for coastal Ystvät Talven settlements.[file:39]

plants.name: Birch and willow belts
plants.note: Paper birch and willow thickets line streams and wetlands in more protected parts of the cape, marking a transition between forest patches and open tundra.[file:39]

plants.name: Tundra grasses and sedges
plants.note: Short grasses and sedges blanket drier parts of the cape and form the basis of inland grazing for reindeer and musk ox.[file:39]

plants.name: Lichens and salt-tolerant plants
plants.note: Lichen mats and tough coastal plants cling to rocky headlands and spray-swept bluffs, surviving intense cold, wind and salt exposure.[file:39]

plants.name: Local herbs (shared with Talath Uichel)
plants.note: Some medicinal herbs listed for Talath Uichel, such as spearleaf and river moss, also occur in Forochel’s river valleys and warm refuges like Evermist, though they are rarer along exposed coasts.[file:39]

people.description: Forochel is the heartland of the Ystvät Talven sea-hunters and tundra folk, but also hosts Snow-elves, Noldorin mystics at Evermist and southern whaling expeditions, all under the distant shadow of Angmar.[file:39]

list.name: Merimetsästäjät (Sea-hunters)
list.description: Ystvät Talven who dwell on moving icebergs and along the Bay of Forochel’s shores, hunting whales, seals and walruses; they are stocky, short, fair-haired and remarkably resistant to cold and immersion, but suffer high mortality from the hazards of the sea.[file:39]

list.name: Helechoth / Aerfaroth (Lossoth of the Cape and Bay)
list.description: The Lossoth of the cape and bay—called Icemen and Sea-hunters in Sindarin—build snow houses, run on ice with bone skates and use wheel-less sled-carts; they camp along Forochel’s shores at the feet of the Blue Mountains and maintain a harsh, proud independence.[file:39]

list.name: Snow-elves of Helloth
list.description: Snow-elves travel between the Landless Land, Helloth and Forochel’s hinterlands; some venture to the bay to trade, while others remain in the enchanted city of ice guarding Helecthil’s radiance.[file:39]

list.name: Lodge of Awakening (Noldorin mystics)
list.description: An obscure order of Noldorin mystics maintains Evermist, a hidden valley on the Cape of Forochel that serves as a haven for the Free Peoples and base for resistance to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:39]

list.name: Southern whalers and traders
list.description: Whaling ships and camps from Cardolan and Gondor exploit the rich marine resources of Forochel’s bay, trading with or competing against local sea-hunters.[file:39]

land.name: Cape of Forochel
land.description: The glaciated peninsula jutting into Belegaer and Ekkaia, once part of Morgoth’s northern realm beyond the Iron Mountains, now a jagged landscape of cliffs, inlets and ice-scoured rock.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: sub-arctic maritime peninsula with cliffs, tundra, glaciers and storm-exposed coasts

land.name: Bay of Forochel
land.description: An immense, ice-prone bay whose currents concentrate marine life but whose storms and shifting pack-ice routinely wreck ships and bergs.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: cold sea with seasonal pack-ice, rich marine megafauna and highly variable weather

land.name: Ered Muil and Ered Rhívamar
land.description: Forlorn mountain ranges dividing Forochel from the rest of Forodwaith and providing some of its more sheltered valleys, forests and predator habitats.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: cold mountains with mixed conifer valleys, bare ridges and avalanche-prone slopes

land.name: Evermist valley
land.description: A hidden, relatively mild valley on the cape maintained by Elvish and spiritual power as a refuge and sanctuary, with richer vegetation than the surrounding land.[file:39]
land.biome_hint: local micro-climate with denser forest, herbs and running water amid an otherwise harsh region

regions.notes: Use this region for coastal, maritime and whaling routes, iceberg travel and interactions with Lossoth sea-hunters, Snow-elves and southern whalers; inland tundra and the broader herd-migration ecology are handled under Talath Uichel.[file:39] Bleak, barren arctic wastes where only the Lossoth live in snow-houses, hunting seals and whales. No trees grow in Forochel; only stubbly grasses and scrawny shrubs exist to feed the herds of Losrandir.
regions.elevation: average: 300 feet. lowest point: sea level. highest point: Amon Losthoron 2,983 feet.

regions.name: Iron Hills, Dale
regions.description: The Grey Mountains (Ered Mithrin) form a long, cold northern range between Mirkwood and the Forodwaith, with abrupt cliffs above the Wilderland, gentler northern slopes fading into tundra and the Talath Oiohelka, and the dragon-haunted basin of the Withered Heath between the eastern forks of the range.

climate.description: The region has a mild arctic climate with short, cool summers and long, dark winters, moderated slightly in the Narrows and intensified on the northern tundra and Withered Heath by blizzards and frequent snow.
