climate.temperature_pattern: Springs and summers last less than four months; temperatures in the Narrows may briefly reach 75–90°F in high summer, while winter lows across the mountains and Northern Waste often drop to −25 to −50°F, with little or no daylight at midwinter in the far north.[file:38]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Precipitation is plentiful through most of the year, falling mainly as snow; rain occurs regularly only during the warmer months, with the Narrows receiving moderate to very heavy monthly precipitation in spring and summer and the Grey Mountains and Northern Waste receiving frequent snow, hail and sleet.[file:38]
climate.notes: Blinding blizzards, violent windstorms with driving snow and intense cold are the most dangerous feature of the weather, turning tundra travel into a lethal gamble; by contrast, the Narrows are sheltered by the mountains and relatively hospitable, with arable land despite their short growing season.[file:38]

fauna.description: The Grey Mountains support abundant wildlife for such a harsh climate: large herds of reindeer and elk on the tundra, mountain goats in the high crags, wolves, snow lions and bears as apex mammalian predators, and numerous ‘dark creatures’—Trolls, Orcs, Fell Beasts, Undead and above all Dragons—centered on the Withered Heath.[file:38]

creatures.name: Grey Wolves
creatures.note: The most common predator of the southern foothills, Narrows and south-eastern Grey Mountains, living in adult packs of 12–20 and sometimes in larger bands of younger wolves that may raid Beorning settlements when local game cannot support them.[file:38]

creatures.name: White-wolves (North-wolves)
creatures.note: Larger, pale northern wolves that rarely come south of the mountains, preying chiefly on Losrandir herds and sometimes attacking Lossoth in lean years; domesticated by Ice-orcs as riding mounts.[file:38]

creatures.name: Wargs
creatures.note: Unnatural, undead-descended wolf-breeds created by Morgoth, abnormally large and sometimes leading wolf packs as tools of Sauron; when slain, their bodies soon vanish.[file:38]

creatures.name: Snow Lions
creatures.note: Highland hunters of the Grey Mountains’ upland vales with thick white coats, feeding mainly on mountain goats and occasionally Caru or deer; heavily targeted by furriers and feared nearly extinct by Beornings.[file:38]

creatures.name: North Bears
creatures.note: Immense pale grey or white bears roaming from the Encircling Sea to north of the Grey Mountains, preying chiefly on Losrandir; they are relentless trackers that will follow a scent for days and can break a reindeer’s back with a single blow.[file:38]

creatures.name: Losrandir (Reindeer)
creatures.note: Gregarious northern reindeer whose herds can reach many thousands, migrating across the north in regular seasonal patterns and serving as staple prey for predators, Lossoth and Ice-orcs.[file:38]

creatures.name: Caru (Elk)
creatures.note: Large elk forming herds of 500–5000 animals, wintering in the Narrows and lands east of the mountains and migrating north through Cirith Feadin to tundra summer ranges; an important food source for northern predators.[file:38]

creatures.name: Mountain Goats
creatures.note: Small, tough, agile climbers in upland areas, living in groups of 10–15 over several square miles and serving as nearly exclusive prey for Snow Lions.[file:38]

creatures.name: Trolls
creatures.note: Stone, Hill, Cave and Snow Trolls inhabit the region: Stone and Hill Trolls are common, brutal plunderers that turn to stone in sunlight, Cave Trolls are huge, blind, subterranean hunters, and rare Snow Trolls in the far north become vulnerable slag-ice pillars during continuous summer daylight.[file:38]

creatures.name: Orcs and Ice-orcs
creatures.note: Several Orc tribes—Lor-uruk-shab, Uruk-erag and Asharag—hold passes and foothills, while Ice-orcs, adapted to cold and sunlight, dwell in Kala Dulakurth and northern Ered Mithrin outposts, raiding Lossoth and Lotan and riding White-wolves.[file:38]

creatures.name: Fell Beasts
creatures.note: A small colony of winged, drake-like Fell Beasts lives in the central upland valleys, serving as aerial terrors allied to Sauron’s servants.[file:38]

creatures.name: Dragons of the Withered Heath
creatures.note: Numerous Cold-drakes and Fire-drakes, greater and lesser, use the Withered Heath as their breeding and war ground; named dragons such as Scatha, Smaug, Ando-anca, Ruingurth, Angurth and others have devastated Dwarven colonies and Beorning lands over the centuries.[file:38]

creatures.name: Undead in ruins
creatures.note: Undead inhabitants—ghosts, wights and similar beings—haunt some Dwarven ruins and defiled Adan burial grounds in and around the mountains.[file:38]

flora.description: Vegetation is relatively sparse but characteristic: grass species dominate in the Narrows and tundra, conifer forests cling to the foothills and lower slopes, and the Withered Heath and Northern Waste bear only hardy grasses, mosses, lichens and a few scattered pines and firs; several notable herbs used by northern peoples, Ice-orcs and Elves grow in this environment.[file:38]

plants.name: Dragon Grass (Lóke salquë)
plants.note: A dry, reed-like grass flourishing in pockets on the basin of the Withered Heath, with long hollow stalks 8–10 feet tall that grow densely and rustle loudly in the wind; it germinates in early spring, seeds in autumn and dies back slowly over winter without immediately collapsing.[file:38]

plants.name: Silver Firs
plants.note: Tough conifers forming scattered stands in the northern foothills, reaching about 30–40 feet tall and growing in some of the most barren soils; their needles are tinged silver and glow eerily in moonlight.[file:38]

plants.name: North Pines
plants.note: Hardy, stocky, long-lived pines found mainly on southern foothills and highland valleys, closely related to Misty Mountain pines but shorter and sturdier, often living up to four hundred years.[file:38]

plants.name: Tundra mosses and lichens
plants.note: Multiple moss and lichen species clinging to rocks on the barren tundra between the mountains and Talath Oiohelka, forming the primary plant cover in an otherwise rock- and ice-dominated landscape.[file:38]

plants.name: Telek
plants.note: A small, stunted bush common on the rocky tundra, valued by Ice-orcs, Lossoth and others because its spring fruit and berry juice rapidly reverse frostbite effects and grant 3–30 ‘hits’ of recovery for cold injuries when ingested.[file:38]

plants.name: Kalmog’s Spoor
plants.note: A delicate shrub growing on northern aspects of the Ered Mithrin, cultivated by Ice-orcs for a potent contact poison; pulped leaves cause burning pain, sores and severe movement penalties unless treated with its secret antidote.[file:38]

plants.name: Chebkuile
plants.note: An extremely rare moss-like growth on high peaks, apparently known only to Mirkwood Elves; in its safe green state it can revive the near-dead and ‘keep life’, though most patients suffer permanent amnesia, while the blue-green form is instantly lethal if eaten.[file:38]

people.description: The Grey Mountains region is sparsely populated but contested by Beornings in the Narrows, Dwarves of Durin’s folk in mining colonies, Ice-orcs in northern fortresses and outposts, and several Orc tribes in passes and foothills, with Dragons, Undead and agents of Angmar and Gundabad influencing politics and warfare.[file:38]

list.name: Beornings / Beijabar (T.A. 1640)
list.description: Tall, heavy-boned, fur-clad Northmen practicing the cult of the Bear and shape-changing, living in scattered family steads in the Narrows and highlands near the mountains; governed informally by household elders and spiritually led by a High Shape-changer (Waetan) who can call the Faird Levy in times of crisis.[file:38]

list.name: Dwarves of Durin’s folk
list.description: Durin’s heirs and their people who, after the loss of Moria, established mining colonies at Silverplunge, Thunderclift, Silverpit, Long Peak and Norr-dum, and later reconnected with Erebor; ruled by a king advised by Dwarf-lords and by appointed Wardens in outlying colonies.[file:38]

list.name: Beornings (later period c. 2580)
list.description: By the time Dragons press hardest, Beornings in the Narrows are a people under siege, losing numbers to wolves, Orcs, Angmarim and Dragons, with many migrating south to their kin in the Anduin vales as settlements fail.[file:38]

list.name: Ice-orcs of Kala Dulakurth
list.description: Tall, gaunt, pale, sun-tolerant Orcs descended from Morgoth’s northern guards, ruling the Forodwaith from the Dark-Ice Fortress and mining outposts like Thollakar; they raid neighboring Lotan and Lossoth, worship Morgoth in gruesome festivals, and now answer uneasily to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:38]

list.name: Orc tribes of the Grey Mountains
list.description: Three main tribes—Lor-uruk-shab, Uruk-erag and Asharag—hold strategic passes and foothills, nominally independent but increasingly forced to send tribute and soldiers to Mount Gundabad under Angmar’s pressure; each ruled absolutely by a warlord.[file:38]

list.name: Lossoth and northern Mannish tribes
list.description: Human groups in the Forodwaith and tundra who hunt Losrandir and Caru and are frequent targets of Ice-orc raids and dragon harassment, especially from nomad drake Klyaxar and others roaming the Northern Waste.[file:38]

land.name: Grey Mountains (Ered Mithrin)
land.description: A long mountain chain north of Mirkwood with peaks mostly around 5000 feet, Mount Gondmaeglom reaching 10,720 feet and more than 50 miles in diameter; southern faces rise abruptly in sheer cliffs over the Wilderland, while northern slopes grade into the Talath Oiohelka.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: high cold mountains with snowfields, glaciers, conifer slopes and sheer southern cliffs

land.name: The Narrows (Q. Arka)
land.description: A thin strip of land between Mirkwood and the Grey Mountains where forest and mountains almost meet, centered on the broad, flat-bottomed valley of the young Forest River (Taurduin); relatively sheltered, arable and rich in wildlife despite a short growing season.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: cool temperate valley with river, fields, scattered woods and frequent snow

land.name: Talath Oiohelka (Plain of Ever-ice)
land.description: The broken, ice-covered plain north of the Grey Mountains, dotted with tortured hills and deep crevasses—remnants of the Iron Mountains—and permafrost polygons and patterned ground formed by freeze–thaw cycles.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: arctic plain with permafrost, ice fissures, patterned ground and minimal vegetation

land.name: Withered Heath
land.description: A vast, desolate basin between the two eastern forks of the Grey Mountains, pitted and scarred by dragon battles, underlain by former swampland and now full of treacherous mud pockets and seasonal slush when snowmelt partially reverts it to swamp; primary breeding and lairing ground for the region’s dragons.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: cold, partially frozen basin with dragon-scarred ground, seasonal bogs and limited grass cover

land.name: Northern Waste (Forodwaith fringe)
land.description: The tundra zone between the northern foothills and Talath Oiohelka, composed of barren rocky ground with mosses, lichens and hardy grasses, punctuated by ancient Morgothic Orc-hold ruins that offer rare shelter in an otherwise deadly land.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: rocky tundra with moss/lichen cover, sparse grasses and extreme weather

land.name: Forest River (Taurduin) headwaters
land.description: The beginnings of the Forest River issuing from under the mountains, cutting a deep rift and forming features such as Steel Fall, a several-hundred-foot cascading waterfall, before flowing into the Narrows.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: steep mountain river gorge with waterfalls and conifer-lined banks

land.name: Mithlin forks and Langwell confluence
land.description: The two forks of the Mithlin rise in the Grey Mountains and join the Langwell to form the upper Anduin, creating a critical hydrological link between the Ered Mithrin and the greater river system of Middle-earth.[file:38]
land.biome_hint: cold headwaters and confluences in mountainous, glacially carved terrain

regions.notes: This object treats the Grey Mountains, Narrows, Northern Waste fringe and Withered Heath as a single climatic and ecological unit centered on the Ered Mithrin and its dragon-haunted basin; specific Dwarven holds (Silverplunge, Thunderclift, Norr-dum, Silverpit), Beorning manors, Ice-orc Kala Dulakurth and named dragon lairs such as Steel Fall or Anvilmount can be modeled as separate site objects where finer resolution is needed.[file:38]
regions.political_organization: Dale: Hereditary Lordship. Esgaroth: Republic with an elected Master.
regions.administrative_organization: Appointed advisors in Dale, elected officials in both towns; land held by aristocracy, granted to smallholders in return for military service.
regions.military: 150 Professional Warriors, many mounted. 150-200 drilled militia troops. 300-500 raw levy, used only in emergencies and takes 2-5 weeks to raise.

regions.name: Dead Marshes & Nindalf
regions.description: The Dead Marshes and Nindalf form a chain of wetlands stretching south and east from the Emyn Muil and the Anduin toward Dagorlad: Nindalf as a rich, living Wetwang of fens and islets, and the Dead Marshes as a blighted extension where stagnant waters, ghosts and Undead now creep over the graves from the Battle of Dagorlad.

climate.description: The marshlands share the region’s inland extremes, but constant moisture moderates actual temperatures while making summers feel oppressively hot and winters raw and wet; local microclimates differ between the thriving Nindalf and the foul Dead Marshes.
climate.temperature_pattern: In both Nindalf and the Dead Marshes summers are warm to hot and steamy, with Marshmen feeling ‘cooked’ in mid-summer; winters bring icy sleet and penetrating damp that makes the cold seem worse than on the dry plain.[file:37]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Rain and runoff from the Emyn Muil and the Entwash (Onodló) maintain high water levels; in Nindalf this supports a healthy mosaic of open water and wet meadow, while in the Dead Marshes diminished inflow and poor drainage have produced stagnant, scummy pools and choking fog.[file:37]
climate.notes: The eastern Dead Marshes appear to be slowly tilting downward while the western margin rises, creating a fertile but mosquito-plagued strip between Nindalf and the Dead Marshes and causing the Dead Marshes to advance over the old burial fields; this geomorphology coincides with Sauron’s malign influence, which many locals blame for the corruption.[file:37]

fauna.description: The Dead Marshes now support mainly snails, leeches, insects and a few specialized predators, while Nindalf remains rich in fish, amphibians, waterfowl and larger mammals; unique creatures such as Ninevet hellbenders, pied swans, green-winged crows, marsh adders, black mink and fishing cats define the living Wetwang, even as ghosts and Orcs turn the Dead Marshes into a haunted killing ground.

creatures.name: Snails, leeches and marsh insects
creatures.note: Primary inhabitants of the Dead Marshes’ stagnant pools, where insect numbers have increased even as higher plants and animals have died off, making the area disease-ridden and unpleasant.[file:37]

creatures.name: Gorcrows
creatures.note: Scavenging crows that feed on insects and carrion across the wetlands, particularly in the Dead Marshes where other birds have fled.[file:37]

creatures.name: Ninevet (Giant Hellbender)
creatures.note: A large, loathsome aquatic amphibian five to eight feet long that waits beneath muddy surfaces to erupt under prey such as ducks, deer, beavers or Men, wrestling them to the bottom and swallowing them whole; reported in both Nindalf and stagnant Dead Marshes pools.[file:37]

creatures.name: Pied Swan (Elph Tithen)
creatures.note: Creamy white swans splotched with black and grey, unusually intelligent herbivores that may guide lost travellers to safety; revered by Marshmen as servants of Yavanna and protected by taboo, but hunted by poachers for feathers and meat.[file:37]

creatures.name: Green-winged Crow (Crebain Gelremen)
creatures.note: Large, clever crows with iridescent green shoulder patches, often tamed as messengers able to return home from up to 75–100 miles in any direction, but notorious thieves of shiny objects.[file:37]

creatures.name: Marsh Adder (Lyg Loeg)
creatures.note: A mottled grey-brown aquatic snake whose highly potent venom (10th-level in MERP terms) causes swift respiratory paralysis or flaccid paralysis in a wide range of creatures; easily startled and prone to bite with little warning.[file:37]

creatures.name: Black Mink
creatures.note: Large, semi-aquatic carnivores up to four feet long and eighty pounds, vicious pack hunters in spring that will attack man-sized beings en masse and fight to the death; their thick, waterproof black pelts are extremely valuable.[file:37]

creatures.name: Fishing Cat
creatures.note: Stealthy wildcats adapted to marsh life, using their paws to flip fish from the water and also preying on birds and frogs; tamable only from kittens and then loyal to a single person.[file:37]

creatures.name: Typical Nindalf fauna
creatures.note: Abundant fish, frogs, newts, turtles, songbirds, waterfowl, deer, beavers, wild boars and black bears, making Nindalf a stark contrast to the Dead Marshes’ depauperate life.[file:37]

creatures.name: Ghosts and Undead of the Dead Marshes
creatures.note: Restless spirits, ghouls, wraiths and ghosts associated with flooded graves and battlefields, and eerie lights and faces in the pools that lure travellers to drown; Sauron’s will is suspected in their proliferation.[file:37]

creatures.name: Orcs in the Marshes
creatures.note: Orc bands that have begun using the Dead Marshes’ cover for movement and lairs again after some 1600 years, despite the area’s meagre food.[file:37]

creatures.name: Trolls near southeastern Dead Marshes
creatures.note: Likely Olog-hai Trolls reported in broad daylight in the southeastern marshes, suggesting a new, more dangerous breed aligned with Sauron.[file:37]

flora.description: Plant life ranges from almost-absent in the Dead Marshes to luxuriant in Nindalf; the Dead Marshes retain only stunted reeds, poisonous weeds and a few tenacious trees and carnivorous plants, while Nindalf boasts a full suite of reeds, wild rice, waterlilies, herbs, shrubs and trees, along with notable medicinal and hazardous species used by Marshmen and others.

plants.name: Stunted reeds of the Dead Marshes
plants.note: Sparse, weakened reeds surviving in the stagnant pools where nutrient flow has ceased and many other aquatic plants have died.[file:37]

plants.name: Water Hemlock
plants.note: An extremely poisonous plant resembling parsley, with feathery foliage and white flowers; its tuberlike roots are deadly and can be processed into ingested ‘Hemlock’ or weapon-coating ‘Blade Hemlock’ poisons.[file:37]

plants.name: Pangwood
plants.note: A low shrubby tree forming dense thickets on islets and channels, whose fine hairs pierce skin and cause burning rashes, spasms and severe debilitation; a dangerous contact poison that can render victims helpless for days.[file:37]

plants.name: Daynet (Water-weed)
plants.note: A floating weed that rises by day to form an impassable mat over channels and sinks at night, forcing Marshmen to continually clear it from their waterways.[file:37]

plants.name: Grabwort
plants.note: A giant carnivorous plant akin to an oversized sundew, with sticky, tough fronds capable of trapping and digesting medium-sized animals or even humans; destroyed by Marshmen with burning oil when discovered.[file:37]

plants.name: Mudbean
plants.note: A hardy legume whose pods and seeds are edible and highly nutritious though hard to digest; grows at the edges of the Dead Marshes and in Marshmen gardens and can sustain a person for days.[file:37]

plants.name: Red Willow
plants.note: A small tree whose leaves turn from silvery green to brilliant scarlet; leaf extracts are a potent anti-fever medicine and, combined with Thurl, produce ‘Caranan’ or Marsh Wine, a powerful healing drink.[file:37]

plants.name: Curulot (Cunning Blossom)
plants.note: A rare small pale-blue flower used by animists and mages to fix herb essences so they can be applied as salves or injections instead of ingested, enabling more flexible use of many herb properties.[file:37]

plants.name: Typical Nindalf plants
plants.note: Various ferns, cattails, reeds, wild rice, scented rushes, pink waterlilies, wild carrot and parsnip, plus maples, larches, beeches, pines and spruces on drier islets and banks, forming a rich wetland flora.[file:37]

people.description: The wetlands are home primarily to Marshmen in Nindalf and Gondorian and Northman settlers along the drier margins, with Asdriags, Orcs, Trolls and Undead increasingly intruding from Dagorlad and the east; healers, animists and hermits such as Camlan exploit the marsh’s herbs and isolation.

list.name: Marshmen (Ninniden)
list.description: Eldest human inhabitants of Nindalf and the marshes, small but hardy folk of Edain descent who live in reed-and-mud huts or island villages, hunt, fish, herd goats and garden, and organize annual councils on Tel Belarin under high priest Envir and high priestess Kerit.[file:37]

list.name: Marsh healers and priests
list.description: Animists, herbalists and priests of Eremi and Jevinn among the Marshmen who collect herbs like Mudbean, Red Willow and Curulot and guard the spiritual and physical health of their scattered communities.[file:37]

list.name: Men of Gondor in Wetlands corridor
list.description: Gondorian soldiers and settlers at Tir Nindor, Caras Gwindor, Eithel Celebrin and scattered estates who patrol the marsh edges, trade with Marshmen and use Nindalf’s herbs and routes to support frontier operations.[file:37]

list.name: Asdriag Easterlings
list.description: Easterling nomads who range close to the western edges of the wetlands, sometimes trading, sometimes raiding, but mostly keeping to drier ground and avoiding the Dead Marshes’ worst corruption.[file:37]

list.name: Camlan the healer (Radagast’s agent)
list.description: A hermit healer living on the southeastern edge of the marsh, secretly a trusted agent of Radagast who gathers news, talks with animals and shelters a Marshman apprentice and a traumatized Elf named Meglin.[file:37]

list.name: Orcs and Trolls of the Dead Marshes
list.description: New arrivals and experiments of Sauron, using the Dead Marshes as cover for movement and sites like Thuringwathost as strongholds, threatening both Marshmen and Gondorian outposts.[file:37]

land.name: Dead Marshes (Loeg Firn)
land.description: A foul swamp stretching south and east from the edge of the Emyn Muil, once akin to Nindalf but now a region of stagnant, nutrient-starved pools, choking fog, dead trees and ghostly lights creeping over the graves of Dagorlad.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: stagnant, nutrient-poor wetland with dead trees, scum-covered pools, heavy mists and strong supernatural taint

land.name: Nindalf (Wetwang)
land.description: The great marsh south of the Emyn Muil and east of the Anduin, fed by local runoff and the Entwash, rich in healthy wetlands, islets and channels and still largely spared the worst effects of the Great Plague.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: large healthy wetland with fens, open water, islets, rich flora and fauna

land.name: Fertile raised strip between Nindalf and Dead Marshes
land.description: An ever-widening strip of raised, dried-out former marshland between Nindalf and the Dead Marshes, fertile but mosquito-infested, likely created by the tilting of the marsh ‘slab’ and now used for new settlement and agriculture.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: recently uplifted, fertile but waterlogged ground with grass and crops between marsh zones

land.name: Islands and islets of Nindalf
land.description: Numerous small islands and islet clusters used by Marshmen for villages, gardens and goat pens, linked by boats and reed rafts.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: slightly raised, drier patches within wetland supporting trees, huts and gardens

land.name: Emyn Muil scarps and feeders
land.description: Western scarps and cliffs of the Emyn Muil that shed water and sediments into Nindalf and the Dead Marshes, controlling inflow patterns and local hydrology.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: rocky hill and cliff margins grading into marshy lowlands

regions.notes: This object models the Dead Marshes and Nindalf together as a hydrologically linked wetland system: Nindalf as the living Wetwang and the Dead Marshes as its cursed, sinking eastern extension over Dagorlad’s graves; local cultures, monsters and Undead all respond to this gradient in water quality, fertility and Sauron’s influence.[file:37]

regions.name: Dagorlad
regions.description: Dagorlad is the wide, flat battle-plain north and northeast of Udûn and the main pass into Mordor, once covered with wild grains and grasses and now a bare, declining expanse dotted with cairns and barrows where the Last Alliance fought Sauron at the end of the Second Age.

climate.description: Dagorlad has an inland continental climate tending toward extremes, with hot, dry-feeling summers and cold, raw winters accentuated by the plain’s vast flatness and lack of vegetation.
climate.temperature_pattern: Seasonal temperature swings are large; summers on the treeless plain are felt as very hot under direct sun, while winters are bitterly cold with icy winds sweeping across the open ground.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Rain and sleet arrive in irregular spells rather than steady gentle weather; the plain receives enough moisture to support sparse grass and shrubs, but far less than the richer wetlands of Nindalf, and recent centuries have seen a steady decline in fertility.
climate.notes: Compared to the adjacent marshes, Dagorlad’s dryness and exposure exaggerate the sense of heat in summer and cold in winter; the Great Plague of T.A. 1636 was only the last in a series of blows that, together with Sauron’s influence, have left the soil impoverished and the vegetation stunted.[file:37]

fauna.description: Modern Dagorlad is almost depopulated of larger wildlife; where once wild cattle and grouse grazed and nested among tall grasses, now only a few hardy small animals and scavengers inhabit the bare plain, alongside Men, Easterlings, Orcs, Trolls and ever-increasing Undead stirred by Sauron.

creatures.name: Scrawny hares
creatures.note: Thin, wary hares that are among the few remaining game animals on the plain; even a skilled hunter may watch all day to spot a single specimen.[file:37]

creatures.name: Small goats
creatures.note: Goats smaller and warier than those of the Talath Harroch, eking out a living on scarce forage and serving as livestock for Asdriag herders in western Dagorlad.[file:37]

creatures.name: Stunted grouse
creatures.note: Grouse that once pecked among wild grains; now they appear rarely as undersized, nervous birds dashing over the exposed ground.[file:37]

creatures.name: Feral dogs
creatures.note: Half-wild dogs scavenging on carrion and scraps, sometimes preying on the few remaining small animals of the plain.[file:37]

creatures.name: Red-legged Hawk
creatures.note: A carrion-hunting hawk that subsists mostly on the remains of other beasts, but will opportunistically attack living prey on the plain.[file:37]

creatures.name: Orcs of the Dead Marshes region
creatures.note: Orc bands have reappeared along the eastern edge of the Dead Marshes and Dagorlad after long absence, stalking the plain by night as Sauron rebuilds his strength.[file:37]

creatures.name: Trolls (Olog-hai type)
creatures.note: Trolls or similar creatures, likely Olog-hai able to endure sunlight, have been sighted in the southeastern Dead Marshes and may roam onto the eastern Dagorlad under cover of darkness.[file:37]

creatures.name: Undead of Dagorlad
creatures.note: Ghouls, wraiths, ghosts and other Undead raised or twisted by Sauron haunt barrows and flooded graves, drawing life from the living and turning the battlefield into a cursed land bridge between Dol Guldur and Mordor.[file:37]

flora.description: Dagorlad’s vegetation has collapsed from once-fruitful wild grains and grasses to a sparse, thorny cover; only scattered tufts of hardy grass, gorse-like shrubs and a few healing herbs survive on the impoverished soil.

plants.name: Sparse rye grass
plants.note: Thin tufts of rye-like grass scattered across the plain, replacing the former rich stands of wild grain and providing meagre forage for goats and game.[file:37]

plants.name: Thorny gorse-like shrubs
plants.note: Low, spiny shrubs reminiscent of gorse that cling to the harsher parts of the battlefield, particularly as one approaches the Morannon where other greenery fails.[file:37]

plants.name: Thurl (dryland form)
plants.note: A common healing herb whose greyish dryland variety appears in occasional patches on Dagorlad, one of the few plants of medicinal value remaining.[file:37]

people.description: Dagorlad in T.A. 1640 lies between Gondor’s waning eastern power and the encroaching Shadow; it hosts Gondorian soldiers and settlers, Asdriag Easterling herders, urban Northmen merchants, Marshmen refugees at the margins, Orc warbands and Trolls, with Elves and Dwarves as rare visitors and Undead as a growing, unnatural presence.

list.name: Marshmen (Ninniden)
list.description: Small, long-lived descendants of the Edain dwelling in Nindalf and western marshes, living by hunting, fishing, goat herding and gardening, worshipping Eru (Iroi) and the Valar as Jevinn and Eremi, and organizing themselves through yearly councils on the holy island of Tel Belarin; some have been driven westward by the Dead Marshes’ corruption.[file:37]

list.name: Men of Gondor
list.description: Soldiers, officers and settlers who built fortresses like Tir Nindor and towns such as Caras Gwindor and Eithel Celebrin to guard roads and trade; much diminished by the Great Plague, but still the principal organized power with forts, laws and minted coin.[file:37]

list.name: Asdriags (Easterling nomads)
list.description: Dark, stocky Easterling herders of cattle and goats on western Dagorlad, organized into small tribes like Ashkira and Vethrig, living in felt-and-hide huts and sustained by a religion that frames their present suffering as a prelude to a future Great Age of the Asdriag People.[file:37]

list.name: Urban Northmen
list.description: Merchants and settlers from the Nan Celduin valley, speaking Rhovanion and Westron, who came to share in eastern trade and now inhabit fortified towns, culturally close to Gondor but proud of their Northman identity.[file:37]

list.name: Orcs (Yrch)
list.description: Morgoth’s corrupted offshoots of Elves, now Sauron’s servants; strong, tough and clannish, forging good weapons and fortresses, and recently seen again marching along the eastern Dead Marshes and across Dagorlad after centuries of absence.[file:37]

list.name: Trolls (Tereg, including Olog-hai)
list.description: Large, powerful but usually stupid mockeries of Ents made by Morgoth; common trolls turn to stone in sunlight, but the superior Olog-hai bred by Sauron can endure daylight and have been sighted in the southeast Dead Marshes region.[file:37]

list.name: Bandits and deserters
list.description: Disaffected soldiers, plague-maddened peasants and local rogues haunting the roads and ruins, turning the depopulated frontier into a patchwork of robber bands unconcerned with Gondor or Sauron beyond what they can steal.[file:37]

land.name: Dagorlad (Battle-plain)
land.description: A wide, flat, grassy plain north and northeast of Udûn and the main pass into Mordor, once covered with wild grains and now a bare field of cairns, barrows and shallow graves where the Last Alliance broke Sauron’s armies.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: dry temperate battle-plain with sparse grasses, thorny shrubs and burial mounds

land.name: Haudh-en-Ohtarrin Morin (Mound of the Black Warriors)
land.description: A long, low barrow on the plain said to hold Black Númenórean captains loyal to Sauron, its doors trapped and its crypts filled with ghoul-animated remains.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: low grassy mound on arid plain with subterranean burial chambers

land.name: Eastern Dagorlad estates and ruins
land.description: Former Gondorian estates like Parth Mallin that once tried to use the plain as granary and grazing land, now largely abandoned, flooded or converted into strongholds by bandits.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: ruined farms and manors on marginal grassland, encroached by marsh

land.name: Tir Nindor and Caras Gwindor corridor
land.description: The crossroads fortress of Tir Nindor and its nearby town Caras Gwindor and waystation Eithel Celebrin, forming Gondor’s main bastion and service hub on the approach between the Anduin, Dagorlad and Mordor.[file:37]
land.biome_hint: fortified stone and timber structures amid lightly settled plains and wetlands

regions.notes: This object treats Dagorlad primarily as the battle-plain and Gondorian frontier immediately north of Mordor; the Dead Marshes, Nindalf and associated wetlands are modeled separately to preserve their distinct flora, fauna and hydrology while still acknowledging that the marshes now overlap and partially drown the eastern Dagorlad graves.[file:37]

regions.name: Dor Guldur, Mirkwood Wilds, Woodmen
regions.supplement: Southern Mirkwood – Haunt of the Necromancer
regions.description: Southern Mirkwood and Southern Rhovanion form a wild, embattled region stretching from the Old Forest Road down to the Dagorlad and the Emyn Muil, dominated by the dark forest around Dol Guldur, the horse-plains of Talath Harroch, and the Anduin vales and Brown Lands that lie between Mirkwood and Gondor. Geology consists of primarily sedimentary strata of limestone and shale, with significant granite projections such as the cinder cone of amon lanc and the mountains of mirkwood.

climate.description: A temperate but increasingly harsh climate with dense, shadowed forest on the slopes of Mirkwood, cold, windy winters on the open plains of Talath Harroch, and relatively mild, well-watered conditions in the Anduin vales and Dagorlad. Mean annual temperature: 45-55° F. Average low: Nínui 10° F. Average high: Úrui 90° F. Average annual precipitation: 20-40 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: In Southern Mirkwood itself and Nan Anduin, mean annual temperatures are about 45–55°F; the southern Rhovanion plain shares similar means (45–55°F), while Dagorlad averages 50–60°F; winters on the plains are snow-blown and freezing, and since the 11th century T.A. winter snows have begun to linger in the forest around Dol Guldur.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Annual precipitation within Mirkwood and on the southern Rhovanion plain is about 20–40 inches, somewhat higher in Nan Anduin (25–45 inches) and lower on Dagorlad (15–35 inches); the Talath Harroch sees long dry spells punctuated by intense spring downpours called ‘fonwindar’, while the forest and river-vales receive more evenly distributed rain.
climate.notes: The Great Plague of 1635–1636 coincided with unusually heavy snow and prolonged cold in Southern Mirkwood, and repeated harsh winters and erratic summers have followed; the Anduin’s moderating influence makes Nan Anduin more stable than the exposed plains, while Dol Guldur’s presence appears to suppress vegetation and alter local conditions on its slopes.

fauna.description: Southern Mirkwood retains many gentle woodland creatures but is increasingly dominated by predators and corrupted beasts—spiders descended from Shelob’s line, fell beasts in the Emyn Guldur and wolves and Orcs under the Necromancer—while the Talath Harroch supports wild horse herds, goats, culcarnix and dangerous vipers and jackals; birds are especially numerous around Rhosgobel.

creatures.name: Woodland birds of Rhosgobel
creatures.note: Migratory and resident birds in vast numbers around Radagast’s forest home, nesting in trees and in wizard-crafted birdhouses, with great northward spring migrations and autumn dispersals across Middle-earth.

creatures.name: Beijabar animal companions
creatures.note: Bears, snow-lion like great cats and other beasts living among the Beijabar as part of the household, defending their homes and sometimes ranging as far as the Undeeps and inner East Bight.

creatures.name: Southern giant spiders
creatures.note: Two main spider populations: large black spiders expanding south from Northern Mirkwood, and the white broods of Enna San Sarab, Shelob’s eldest daughter, hatching every thirty-six days from egg-sacs in Dol Guldur and spreading through the southwestern forest.

creatures.name: Fell beasts of the Emyn Guldur
creatures.note: Winged, drake-like monsters up to 30 feet long with 30–35 foot wingspans, gliding silently from caves and ledges, armed with huge claws and fangs and bearing the taint of Morgoth’s ancient cold-drakes.

creatures.name: Wild horses of Talath Harroch
creatures.note: Free-ranging herds roaming the plains, providing the breeding stock from which the plains Northmen derive their strong war- and work-horses.

creatures.name: Wild goats
creatures.note: Herds that move between forest margins and open grassland, even chewing through thorny rose-tree hedges that stop most other creatures.

creatures.name: Culcarnix
creatures.note: Large, docile herd-beasts native to the Talath Harroch and unknown elsewhere, forming a distinctive part of the plains ecology and Northmen economy.

creatures.name: Kine of Araw
creatures.note: White wild oxen from the lands around the Sea of Rhûn that sometimes wander west into the eastern Talath Harroch.

creatures.name: Egils Viper
creatures.note: A dangerous venomous snake of the plains and lower slopes, feared by travellers and herdsmen alike.

creatures.name: Undarfalf jackals
creatures.note: Foul jackals that first appeared during the Plague winter, now haunting hills and scavenging battlefields and plague sites.

flora.description: Southern Mirkwood’s flora is defined by dense stands of furry oak and chap-beech under constant shadow, pockets of broadleaf evergreens such as grape-leaf magnolia, and Elven-planted rose-trees, while the Talath Harroch plains and Nan Anduin vales support grasses, crops and useful herbs; Dol Guldur itself is ringed by dead trees and scrub where healthy forest once grew.

plants.name: Furry oak and chap-beech
plants.note: Tall, closely spaced hardwoods with branchless trunks and umbrella crowns about forty feet up, resilient to insect blights and chestnut diseases, creating the dark, moist, foggy character of Southern Mirkwood.

plants.name: Grape-leaf magnolia
plants.note: An abundant broadleaf evergreen with large holly-like leaves and downward-hanging blood-red flowers, especially in mid-summer; its nectar produces dark red honey that can be mildly intoxicating and is used by the Beijabar to brew magnolia mead.

plants.name: Mirkwood rose-trees
plants.note: High hedging shrubs or small trees bearing dense masses of rose, burgundy and white blossoms from May to September; their needle-sharp thorns make them formidable living walls, first planted by Elves as boundary markers and later prized by Lórien’s healers for their nectar.

plants.name: Milk-white trumpet (Giant Datura)
plants.note: A late-arriving shrub that grows up to twelve feet tall, exuding a nauseating odor when bruised; its huge white flowers explosively shed pollen that can cause temporary blindness and nausea, while its black seeds are strong psychoactive poisons—one seed intoxicates, several kill most races, though Elves are immune.

plants.name: Din Fainen (death-moss)
plants.note: A deadly moss associated with dark, damp spots in the forest, used in poisons and feared by cautious travellers.

plants.name: Loth-nu-Fuin (Glorious Lichen)
plants.note: A striking lichen growing on rocks and trees in particularly shadowed areas, serving as a visual marker of deep shadow and sometimes used in lore and alchemy.

plants.name: Touch-grass and plains grasses
plants.note: Characteristic grasses of the Talath Harroch, including touch-grass, bog-land grass and other hardy species that survive snow-laden winters and sudden spring floods.

plants.name: Vales of Anduin trees and herbs
plants.note: Oak–maple, beech–maple and willow–elm stands along the Anduin, accompanied by wild persimmons, ‘carefree’ mustard, splayfoot goodwort and athelas, making the river-vales rich in useful plants.

plants.name: Brown Lands prairie cover
plants.note: Short, dry prairie grass overlying the old raised garden beds of the Entwives, suggesting a buried legacy beneath the otherwise barren hills.

people.description: Southern Rhovanion is a country under siege: Beijabar and Wood-men cling to the western eaves, horse-lords and Gramuz Northmen fight for survival on the Talath Harroch, Gondor projects power along the Anduin and into Dor Rhûnen, Easterling tribes press from the east, and above all Dol Guldur and its Necromancer darken the forest and shape the fate of all inhabitants after the Great Plague.

list.name: Wood-men of Southern Mirkwood
list.description: Clans of woodsmen based around Woodmen-town and smaller camps in the western eaves of the forest, living by woodcraft, hunting and small-scale farming and maintaining palisades and tactics suited to defending against Orcs and beasts.

list.name: Beijabar of Nan Anduin
list.description: Bear-folk and related Northmen dwelling in the Anduin vales south of the Old Forest Road, living in close symbiosis with animals and regarding certain sites along the river as holy, with reputations for hospitality but fierce defense of their lands.

list.name: Northmen of the Talath Harroch
list.description: Descendants of Vidugavia’s horse-lords in the plains and East Bight, formerly prosperous farmers and horse-breeders, reduced by the Plague to hard-edged defenders of isolated homesteads and fortified camps.

list.name: Eothraim and Gramuz tribes
list.description: Roving horse-tribes in the rolling hills and scattered woodlands, hunting great stags, herding horses and moving between seasonal camps with strong warrior traditions.

list.name: Gondorian outposts and settlers
list.description: Gondorians of Calenardhron and Dor Rhûnen who garrison forts like Thorontir and farm pockets such as Tharlorien and the Undeeps, guarding the fords and asserting Gondor’s claimed dominion.

list.name: Asdriags and Sagath (Easterlings)
list.description: Eastern tribes with favored camps east of the plains, periodically raiding and trading, and deeply entangled in Gondor’s campaigns and rivalries across Southern Rhovanion.

list.name: Elves of Southern Mirkwood
list.description: Silvan and Sindarin Elves in the northern reaches of the southern forest, heirs to Greenwood the Great’s earlier glory, maintaining hidden dwellings and circles in Angaladadh and along the forest roads.

list.name: Radagast the Brown
list.description: The Wizard dwelling at Rhosgobel on the western edge of Mirkwood, devoting his power and attention to birds and beasts rather than politics, and providing a rare point of light and refuge in the shadowed woods.

list.name: The Necromancer of Dol Guldur
list.description: Sauron in his guise as the Necromancer, occupying Dol Guldur since the 12th century T.A., radiating fear and corruption, breeding Orcs, trolls, spiders and fell beasts, and acting as the dark center of Southern Mirkwood.

list.name: Orcs and Tereg trolls of Dol Guldur
list.description: Sauron’s garrison in Dol Guldur and the Emyn Guldur, using the hill as a fortress, prison, armory and staging point for raids into Mirkwood, Nan Anduin and the plains.

land.name: Angaladadh (Northwest quadrant)
land.description: The deep northern heart of Southern Mirkwood’s forest, north-west of Dol Guldur, where the canopy remains thick, the old rose-tree circles still stand and Elven influence is strongest.
land.biome_hint: dense temperate hardwood–evergreen forest with heavy undergrowth

land.name: Aur Esgalabar / Romeniaur (Northeast quadrant)
land.description: The northeastern wedge where the forest thins and gives way to grassland and the East Bight, mixing woodland, scrub and open country.
land.biome_hint: mosaic of forest edge, scrub and plains

land.name: Talath Harroch (The Plains)
land.description: The broad plains southeast of Mirkwood, home to horse-herding Northmen, wild herds and culcarnix, with long snowy winters and violent spring rains.
land.biome_hint: cool temperate grassland and farmland with scattered homesteads

land.name: Nan Lothanduin / Nan Anduin
land.description: The Anduin vales south of the Old Forest Road and opposite Dol Guldur, containing fertile pockets such as Tharlorien, the North and South Undeeps and Beijabar holdings.
land.biome_hint: broad river valley with alluvial fields, groves and settlements

land.name: Dol Guldur (Amon Lanc)
land.description: A stark, 3649‑foot volcanic cone on the southern eaves of Mirkwood, crowned and ringed by fortress walls and towers, once called the Naked Hill and now the hill of sorcery under Sauron’s control.
land.biome_hint: rocky volcanic hill with dead trees and corrupted scrub

land.name: Emyn Guldur (Hills of Sorcery)
land.description: Rugged hills and broken country around Dol Guldur, full of caves and shelves where spiders and fell beasts lair, and criss-crossed by secret Orc-paths.
land.biome_hint: rocky hills with dark forest patches, caves and cliffs

land.name: Old Forest Road (southern sector)
land.description: The east–west road along the northern edge of Southern Rhovanion, carrying refugees and trade and linking the western vales with the eastern plains and forest entries.
land.biome_hint: forest-margin road through mixed woodland and clearings

land.name: Road to the Morannon
land.description: The main north–south route from Nan Anduin across the Brown Lands toward the Black Gate, used increasingly by Sauron’s forces shifting from Dol Guldur to Mordor.
land.biome_hint: long overland route across river-vales and dry hills

land.name: North and South Undeep
land.description: Shallow fords of the Anduin where the river spreads over rock shelves, forming strategic crossing-points guarded and contested by Gondor and its enemies.
land.biome_hint: wide, shallow river reaches with rocky beds and down-like hills

land.name: Brown Lands
land.description: Bleak, brown hills east of the Anduin whose ridges were once raised and irrigated garden beds tended by the Entwives, now covered in short dry grass and scarred by frost and neglect.
land.biome_hint: dry temperate steppe on old terraced hills

regions.notes: This object treats Southern Mirkwood and Southern Rhovanion as a single strategic and ecological unit in T.A. 1640: the haunted southern forest around Dol Guldur, the Talath Harroch horse-plains, the Anduin vales and Brown Lands, and the human and inhuman powers contesting them; individual sites such as Woodmen-town, Rhosgobel, Thorontir and Dol Guldur’s detailed layouts can be represented as separate site objects if needed. The largest forest in western Endor, dominated by oaks, maples, elms, and conifers. It has fallen under a stagnant, heavy shadow since the Necromancer established Dol Guldur, making southern reaches twisted, dark, and home to giant spiders.
regions.elevation: average: 1,012 feet. lowest point: Tauraelin 617 feet. highest point: Amon Lanc 5,266 feet.
regions.administrative_organization: Each tribe is independent, usually electing its own chieftain based on merit. For issues effecting the entire Wood, the chieftains seek counsel with the Althyn, the most influential of their number. Land is held in common by the tribe, its use dictated by the chief.
regions.military: 650 Warriors, led by local chieftains.
regions.products: Lumber, hides, nuts, carved wooden boxes.

regions.name: Woodland Realm, Esgaroth
regions.description: Northern Mirkwood is the northern half of the great forest once known as Greenwood the Great, stretching between the Anduin, the Grey Mountains, the Withered Heath and the upper River Running, and including Thranduil’s woodland realm, the Long Lake, the Lonely Mountain and the Iron Hills.

climate.description: A cool to mild temperate climate shaped by the Grey Mountains, deep forests and major rivers, with pleasant summers and cold but generally not polar winters south of the Grey Mountains.
climate.temperature_pattern: South of the Grey Mountains, mean annual temperatures lie around 40–50°F, with colder conditions in the mountains (25–35°F) and even colder north of the range (15–25°F); winters bring snow to the Grey Mountains, while the forest interior has cold but survivable winters and warm summers.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Mean annual precipitation is roughly 20–40 inches south of the Grey Mountains, 20–40 inches in the mountains themselves, and 10–20 inches north of the range; rainfall and snow are spread across the year with heavier moisture in spring and early summer.
climate.notes: Glaciation has heavily shaped the land between Erebor and the Grey Mountains, producing tumbled hills, Long Marshes and the basin of Long Lake; prevailing westerlies and the presence of the Anduin and other rivers moderate climatic extremes and make the region suitable for Northmen, Wood-elves and Dwarves.

fauna.description: Northern Mirkwood hosts a layered ecosystem from high mountains to deep forest and marsh, dominated by Great Eagles and dragons in the heights, giant spiders and bears in the woods, and a wide variety of birds, insects and smaller animals in rivers and wetlands; the Great Plague reduced numbers of large animals but left insects and many lesser creatures largely intact.

creatures.name: Great Eagles
creatures.note: Princely, divinely-linked raptors of the Grey Mountains, able to see a running rabbit a mile away even by moonlight and acting as scouts, rescuers and allies of the Free Peoples.

creatures.name: Dragons of the Ered Mithrin
creatures.note: Cold-drakes and fire-drakes lairing in the Grey Mountains and breeding in the Withered Heath, including Smaug the Golden and other named dragons who terrorize the surrounding lands.

creatures.name: Giant Spiders of Mirkwood
creatures.note: Colonies of huge spiders, descendants of Ungoliant and kin to Shelob, that spin vast webs between trees, paralyze victims with venom and liquefy their insides before feeding.

creatures.name: Great Bears and black bears
creatures.note: Large omnivorous bears that fish, raid beehives, forage nuts and roots, and hibernate through winter; their solitary habits and winter denning help them survive the Great Plague.

creatures.name: Wolves and Warg-allies
creatures.note: Communal wolf packs with complex social hierarchies, some large enough and closely allied to Wargs and Orcs to be used as mounts and battle-companions.

creatures.name: Marsh and river fauna
creatures.note: Rich assemblages of insects, fish, amphibians, birds and small mammals in the Long Marshes and along the Anduin, Forest River and River Running, with insect populations barely affected by the Plague.

creatures.name: Plague vector fleas
creatures.note: Common fleas acting as the chief transmitters of the Great Plague, leaping vast distances relative to their size and surviving long periods without feeding.

flora.description: Northern Mirkwood combines ancient conifer–hardwood forest, glacial wetlands and fertile river-vales; after the Great Plague the forest floor is quieter and more overgrown, with twisted oaks, strangling ivy and deep thickets under a dense canopy, while the Long Marshes and upland dales remain hotspots of plant diversity.

plants.name: Mixed pine–oak–beech forest
plants.note: The dominant forest type, with dense stands of pine, oak and beech covering much of the interior, providing cover for Elves, spiders, bears and wolves.

plants.name: Twisted oaks and ivy
plants.note: Oaks with gnarled trunks and pain-twisted branches, often darkened and lengthened leaves, choked by serpentine ivy that trails to the ground.

plants.name: Long Marshes vegetation
plants.note: Brackish pools, reeds, sedges and swamp grasses forming a highly productive wetland along the Forest River near Long Lake, largely untouched by the Plague.

plants.name: Upland berries and nuts
plants.note: Nuts, blackberries, hawthorn berries and other shrubs in the hills and lower mountains, now less abundant than before the Plague but still important for bears and men.

plants.name: Meadow herbs and clover
plants.note: Clover patches, thyme, sage, marjoram and other herbs in oak-lined dales and open slopes, supporting bees and livestock.

plants.name: Deep-forest fungi and herbs
plants.note: Pale medicinal herbs with strong odors and fungi of varied kinds in the darkest parts of the forest, used by healers and offering food for woodland creatures.

people.description: Northern Mirkwood in T.A. 1640 is a sparsely civilized frontier containing Thranduil’s Wood-elves, Dwarves of the Iron Hills and Erebor, multiple Northmen cultures in valleys and plains, and scattered Beijabar and other folk in the hills, all living under the growing long-range shadow of Dol Guldur and the aftermath of the Great Plague.

list.name: Thranduil’s Wood-elves
list.description: Silvan Elves under Thranduil living in caverns and halls near the eastern eaves of the forest, lovers of feasting, song and languages and largely indifferent to neighboring Men and Dwarves unless threatened.

list.name: Dwarves of the Iron Hills
list.description: Durin’s Folk who mine and smelt rich iron deposits in the Iron Hills east of Mirkwood, providing metalwork, weapons and armor and maintaining trade links with Dalemen and Lake-men.

list.name: Dwarves of Erebor
list.description: The Dwarves of the Lonely Mountain, dwelling in a great isolated peak north of Long Lake and using its caverns as halls, mines and treasure vaults; tightly connected to trade along the River Running.

list.name: Northmen of the Anduin and Rhovanion
list.description: Tall, strong, fair-haired Men living along the Anduin and on the eastern plains, farming, herding and trading while maintaining distant kinship ties to Gondor’s rulers.

list.name: Mountain men of the Iron Hills and Grey Mountains
list.description: Fierce Northmen hunters and trappers wrapped in furs, dwelling in the uplands north of the Forest River and within earshot of dragon roars, living in scattered clans and answering to no overlord.

list.name: Beijabar (Beornings)
list.description: A rarefied Northman branch with a strong cultic link to the Carrock, famed for hospitality toward friends and for their breads and honeys; some members may be skin-changers in later Ages.

list.name: Orcs and other servants of Darkness
list.description: Orc tribes, trolls and allied creatures occupying lairs, caverns and ruins around the fringes of the forest and mountains, raiding human settlements and sometimes operating under Dol Guldur’s influence.

land.name: Northern Mirkwood proper
land.description: The northern half of Mirkwood, north of the Men-i-Naugrim and between the Anduin, the Grey Mountains, the Withered Heath and the upper River Running, including Thranduil’s realm and vast tracts of deep forest.
land.biome_hint: large temperate conifer–broadleaf forest with hills, marshes and river-valleys

land.name: Ered Mithrin (Grey Mountains)
land.description: A long, jagged range north of Mirkwood with peaks over 6000 feet, snowfields in winter and numerous lairs of dragons, Great Eagles, giants and hardy mountain folk.
land.biome_hint: cold temperate to subarctic mountains with rock, snow and conifer bands

land.name: Withered Heath
land.description: The desolate, frost-scarred upland east of the Grey Mountains and north of Erebor that has long served as the breeding ground and hunting territory of dragons.
land.biome_hint: cold, rocky heath with sparse vegetation and dragon-haunted cliffs

land.name: Long Lake
land.description: A long, narrow lake east of Mirkwood, essentially a glacial trench filled to the brim by the Forest River, feeding the River Running and supporting Lake-town and its trade.
land.biome_hint: cold temperate lake with marshy margins and nearby low hills

land.name: Forest River (Taurduin)
land.description: River flowing from the Grey Mountains through the northern forest into Long Lake, cutting gorges with stone cliffs and shingle banks and forming the Long Marshes near its mouth.
land.biome_hint: mountain-fed river with gorges, marshes and mixed forest banks

land.name: Anduin (upper course)
land.description: The upper Anduin flowing south along the western edge of Mirkwood, defining the forest’s western boundary and offering fertile vales for Northmen and Beijabar.
land.biome_hint: large temperate river with floodplains, bluffs and mixed woodland

land.name: Long Marshes
land.description: Expansive marshlands of brackish pools and islets along the lower Forest River near Long Lake, teeming with insects, birds, amphibians and small mammals.
land.biome_hint: cool-temperate marsh with reeds, shallow pools and islets

land.name: Mountains of Mirkwood
land.description: Lower, older limestone mountains within the forest that extend to its eastern edge, riddled with caverns ideal for caches, lairs and hidden strongholds.
land.biome_hint: low to mid-elevation karst hills with forested slopes and caves

land.name: Rhovanion plains (northern fringe)
land.description: The open grasslands and gentle hills east of the forest and south of the Iron Hills, used by Northmen as grazing land and farmland.
land.biome_hint: cool temperate steppe and farmland with scattered groves

regions.notes: This object treats Northern Mirkwood as the combined forest, mountain and river system north of the Men-i-Naugrim in T.A. 1640, including Thranduil’s woodland realm, the Grey Mountains, Long Lake, Erebor’s immediate environs and the Iron Hills; specific cities such as Lake-town and Dale can be modeled as separate site objects.
regions.administrative_organization: King has direct authority over entire realm, but rules by consent with a council of eleven nobles. Land is held by King, but all subjects are free to use it within the parameters of the law.
regions.military: 1,900 Elite Warriors and Rangers. 2,100 Warriors. 200 Raft-elf Warriors.
regions.symbol: Dark Green Beech Leaf on a Grass Green Field

regions.name: Dunland, Enedhwaith
regions.supplement: Dunland and the Southern Misty Mountains
regions.description: Dunland is the rugged foothill and plateau country south of the Glanduin, east of the Gwathló and north of the Isen, a land of highlands and plains ruled by fractious Dunlending clans who herd, raid and scheme along the vital Old South Road and the Isen Gap.

climate.description: Dunland has a milder but still challenging cool‑temperate climate, with cold wet winters, stormy transitional seasons and warm, often humid summers, harsher in the Highland zone and gentler in the Plains and Gwathló basin.
climate.temperature_pattern: In the Highlands, winters are cold and wet with regular heavy snows from late autumn into early spring, while early summer brings warm days and pleasantly cool nights; the Plains and Gwathló basin see somewhat warmer winters with frequent melt‑offs and warm to hot, humid early summers that become more comfortable later in the season.[file:32]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Highland winters receive regular heavy snow, followed in spring by snowmelt‑driven flash floods and fog; summer has occasional gentle rains, while autumn brings massive thunderstorms and dense fogs that can turn to sleet or hail as temperatures drop; the Plains mirror this pattern but with less severe storms, and the Gwathló margins become marshy in spring due to combined rain and mountain runoff.[file:32]
climate.notes: Fogs are common, especially in spring and autumn, sometimes lying as a ground‑hugging layer only knee‑deep to a man; the weather is close kin to Rhudaur’s but warmer overall, and microclimates differ sharply between high ridges, plateaus, river valleys and the low Gwathló wetlands.[file:32]

fauna.description: Dunland’s fauna is rich and varied: the Highlands and adjacent mountains host herds of deer, horn‑sheep and other game with numerous predators, while the Lowlands hold pronghorn, rabbits, crocodiles in the Gwathló swamps and a wide range of birds, snakes and notable special creatures.

creatures.name: Fiara, Caru and Goral
creatures.note: Herds of Fiara (deer of several kinds), Caru (elk in the northern hills) and golden‑colored Goral horn‑sheep range across the Highlands and higher hills, migrating upward in warmer months.[file:32]

creatures.name: Grey wolves (Moctire)
creatures.note: Packs of ten to fifteen grey wolves called Moctire by Dunlendings roam the Highlands, their numbers reduced by hunting for valuable pelts but still a serious threat to livestock and travelers.[file:32]

creatures.name: Bears and felines
creatures.note: Bears once common in the Highlands have retreated mostly into the mountains, while Highland Lynx hunt in prides of five to eight and occasional Snow Leopards range down from the higher slopes.[file:32]

creatures.name: Pronghorn (Astabanhe) and lowland game
creatures.note: Pronghorn herds roam the Lowlands though much diminished by hunting, with some now kept in captivity along with Fiara by lowland clans; rabbits are extremely plentiful on plateaus and low hills, including an unusually large species the size of a medium dog.[file:32]

creatures.name: Gwathló crocodiles and marsh life
creatures.note: The swamps of the Gwathló are one of the few northern Middle‑earth regions with indigenous crocodiles, alongside reeds, cattails, lily pads, fish and amphibians in the marsh channels.[file:32]

creatures.name: Birds of Dunland
creatures.note: Highland woods are noisy with songbirds in spring; lowlands and marshes host cranes, bitterns and other wading birds, while Golden Eagles and falcons hunt in the high hills, Goshawks raid game in lower Highlands, and owls—viewed as ancestors’ spirits—occupy barns and forests.[file:32]

creatures.name: Notable special creatures
creatures.note: Distinctive beasts include Jatewoone ‘Swordbeak’ marsh birds, Highland Nathair and Plains Nathair venomous snakes, aquatic Nathrach, extremely poisonous Coireal snakes, Glutan wolverines, Vereut eagles and Gorbet ‘trail lizards’ with a nerve‑toxin skin secretion.[file:32]

flora.description: Dunland was once part of the same vast forest that covered much of Middle‑earth, but Númenórean logging and Sauron’s wars left it almost treeless; now the Highlands bear light to medium coniferous forests and scrub with older forest patches, while the Plains and Gwathló basin are mostly grassland and marsh.

plants.name: Highland conifer and mixed woods
plants.note: Highland slopes are covered by light to medium coniferous forests interspersed with surviving patches of older mixed forest from the Second Age, plus isolated broadleaf stands.[file:32]

plants.name: Scrub, brambles and heather
plants.note: Low scrub bushes, brambles, heather, thistles and other low ground cover form dense, sometimes impassable thickets in certain valleys and on treeless hilltops.[file:32]

plants.name: Lowland grasses and marsh plants
plants.note: The Lowlands and Gwathló basin are almost devoid of large trees except near settlements; most of the area carries short grass that greens in spring and summer and yellows in fall and winter, with reeds, cattails and lily pads in marshy areas and open water.[file:32]

plants.name: Eorna and other crops
plants.note: Eorna, a hardy grain akin to barley, is one of the few crops grown in large volume by Dunlendings; they also cultivate herbs and maintain gardens near clan‑holds and towns.[file:32]

plants.name: Avhail
plants.note: A sweet, yellowish wild fruit in the Gwathló basin, resembling cherries and tasting like port; eaten fresh as a hangover remedy and used as the main ingredient in the popular fermented drink Biotaille.[file:32]

people.description: Dunland is dominated by Dunlendings—Daen Lintis highlanders descended from the Daen Coentis—living in extended family bands within clans, but also includes Dúnedain estates, Stoors, Drúedain in Caerdh Wood, organized traders, bards, healers and a powerful Dark‑influenced Temple hierarchy.

list.name: Dunlendings (Daen Lintis)
list.description: Stocky, medium‑height Common Men with sparse brown hair and ruddy complexions, organized into clans of extended family bands; isolationist, superstitious and proud, they survive largely by hunting, gathering and herding, with strong raiding traditions and loose, often violent politics.[file:32]

list.name: Daen Iontis faction
list.description: Militant Dunnish faction exemplified by clan Arailt and their allies, bitterly hating Dúnedain, closely linked to the Temple of Justice, and pushing for war and a harsh, sacrificial religion that twists old Daen beliefs.[file:32]

list.name: Daen Coentis faction
list.description: Clans such as Treforn, Roth and Ewen who consciously revive the ‘Skilled People’ heritage of the White Mountains, value craft and old gods, and have invited Drúedain back to Caerdh Wood to rebuild temple‑observatories and shared religious life.[file:32]

list.name: Dúnedain landowners
list.description: Scattered Dúnadan manors and estates, often old Númenórean outposts like Palomir’s estate or the Healers’ Hall at Cillien, occupied by families who farm and herd with fortified houses and uneasy relations with surrounding clans.[file:32]

list.name: Stoors of New Maresh
list.description: A small Stoor Hobbit community living in burrows on hills rising from a marsh near the Dunstrem, traveling by pole‑boats, trading herbs and fish, and protected by the treacherous wetlands and their own wariness of outsiders.[file:32]

list.name: Drúedain of Caerdh Wood
list.description: Three villages of Drúedain invited by Daen Coentis clans into Caerdh Wood, who build stone villages and act as priests and spiritual guides, re‑establishing ancient Daen‑Drúedain religious partnerships.[file:32]

list.name: Traders and smugglers
list.description: Merchants based in Larach Duhnnan and other sites move goods between clans and to Tharbad, while a hidden Smugglers’ Hold grows gort and tartella herbs for Corsairs of Umbar using Dunnish and Dúnadan frontmen.[file:32]

land.name: Dunland Highlands
land.description: Eastern highland zone backed by the southern Misty Mountains, with hills and ridges up to about twelve hundred feet, running in orderly lines and breaking westward into mesa‑like hills with flat tops and steep sides.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: cool hills and ridges with mixed forest and pasture

land.name: Dunland Plains and Gwathló basin edge
land.description: Western plateaus and low rolling hills that step down over fifty miles into the flat plains of the wide Gwathló basin, mostly grassy and nearly treeless except near settlements and stream corridors.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: temperate grasslands with scattered trees and marsh fringes

land.name: Old South Road (Tiar Hariar)
land.description: The major highway skirting Dunland’s southern border, running east–west along the plateau edge from Tharbad toward the Isen Gap, carrying trade and travelers through Larach Duhnnan and clan territories.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: engineered road across plateau edge and river valleys

land.name: Larach Duhnnan
land.description: Largest town of Dunland, built on a plateau overlooking the Dunstrem and the Old South Road, surrounded by an embankment with clan Feargan’s fortified hold on a raised hill; serves as main trade hub and neutral meeting‑ground for multiple clans.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: market town in upland valley with river and road access

land.name: Caerdh Wood
land.description: One of only two true forests in Dunland, a remnant of the primordial woods, considered mysterious and possibly haunted; hosts Daen Coentis temple‑observatories and three Drúedain villages.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: cool mixed old forest in foothill basin

land.name: New Maresh marshes
land.description: Marshy area draining into the Dunstrem near its junction with the Gwathló, with hills rising above the wetlands to hold Stoor burrows and channels navigated by small boats among patches of quicksand.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: freshwater marsh with raised hillocks and slow streams

land.name: Temple of Justice valley
land.description: A long dead‑end wooded valley about twenty‑five miles east‑south‑east of Cillien where the dark granite Temple of Justice stands on a low hill, with a stream and bridge at its mouth and steep walls guarded by lookout posts.[file:32]
land.biome_hint: forested valley with religious complex and defensive works

regions.notes: Keep this Dunland object strictly for the foothills, plateaus, plains and valleys north of the Old South Road and below the alpine zone; the Southern Misty Mountains object above covers the high peaks and explicitly mountain-only flora, fauna and powers.[file:32]
regions.administrative_organization: Fifteen hereditary and merit-based chiefdoms, all lesser offices appointed; tribal land held in common.
regions.military: Each Chief keeps from 100-400 Warriors as a permanent retinue. Each Clan can also raise 1,400-3,200 Warriors given 1-4 weeks. Tribes favor light armor and carry spears and melee weapons predominately.

regions.name: Minhiriath, Mintyrnath, South Downs, Rast Vorn
regions.description: Cardolan, the Land of Red Hills, is the southern successor-kingdom of Arnor between the Baranduin and the Gwathló–Mitheithel, a windswept land of treeless plains, red downs, marshy river basins and the barrow‑haunted hills whose shattered Dúnedain realm never fully recovered from war, civil strife and plague.

climate.description: Cardolan has a humid mid‑latitude climate: generally mild on average but capable of some of the most severe weather in Endor, with cool wet springs, hot dry summers, cool wet autumns and cold winters, slightly cooler in the downs than in the lowlands.
climate.temperature_pattern: Both Minhiriath and Mintyrnath share cool, wet springs, moderately hot summers, cool wet autumns and cold winters, with Mintyrnath running about five degrees cooler; strong daily swings are common, with differences of up to thirty‑five degrees between day and night, and a slow long‑term cooling trend makes late frosts and shortened growing seasons increasingly problematic.[file:31]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Warm, wet air from the western sea collides with cold, dry air from the Misty Mountains and Forochel to produce frequent rain and violent storms, especially in late spring and early summer, including severe thunderstorms, hail and tornadoes in central Cardolan, plus annual flooding along the Gwathló basin that both enriches fields and periodically turns Tharbad into an island.[file:31]
climate.notes: The destruction of the primordial forest and subsequent erosion left Cardolan with generally poor soils except where rivers like the Gwathló and Baranduin regularly renew floodplains; weather is highly variable and exposed, with strong westerlies complicating river navigation and long‑term cooling associated in local superstition with the malice of the Witch‑king until Angmar’s fall.[file:31]

fauna.description: The module focuses on history and politics rather than detailed bestiaries, but it presents Cardolan as an ecologically fragile, largely deforested landscape where sheep, wolves and reptiles dominate, with richer bird and fish life along rivers and coasts and a growing return of wild animals as Dúnedain control fades.

creatures.name: Wolves of Cardolan
creatures.note: The primary predator in Cardolan, grey (and occasional black) wolves hunt in intelligent packs that prey chiefly on sheep but are not afraid of Men and will attack lone travelers; under Angmar’s influence they sometimes kill simply for sport.[file:31]

creatures.name: Sheep and livestock
creatures.note: Sheep introduced and bred on a large scale by the Dúnedain are the defining domestic animals of the plains and downs, with cattle and horses also present, especially around the Gwathló basin and richer farmlands.[file:31]

creatures.name: Reptiles of Swanfleet and marshes
creatures.note: The Nîn‑in‑Eilph (Swanfleet Marshes) and other wetlands harbor numerous poisonous reptiles and amphibians alongside lordly swans, making them both valuable and dangerous hunting grounds.[file:31]

creatures.name: River and coastal life
creatures.note: Fish and waterfowl occupy the Baranduin, Gwathló and coastal waters near Rast Vorn, where shellfish and small whales are important resources for coastal folk.[file:31]

creatures.name: Increasing wild fauna in ruins
creatures.note: As forts fall and farms are abandoned after the Kingdom’s collapse, more wild beasts return to barrow‑lands, forests and ruins, making exploration riskier for adventurers.[file:31]

flora.description: Cardolan was once part of a single primordial forest that covered southern Eriador, but Númenórean logging, war and fire destroyed almost all of it; today it is dominated by treeless, windswept plains of hardy grasses, scattered new beech‑and‑pine woods, thorn hedges and limited but extremely fertile river‑basin farmlands.

plants.name: Ablas grass
plants.note: A plains grass that dominates Minhiriath’s exposed lowlands, forming the basic sward of the semi‑arid, sheep‑grazed central plains.[file:31]

plants.name: Deblas moss‑grass
plants.note: A short, mossy grass that replaces ablas across most of Mintyrnath and the downs, covering the red‑tinted hills and making them suitable mainly for grazing.[file:31]

plants.name: Hagavorl (Hedge‑wall)
plants.note: A vast hawthorn hedge planted originally by Pharconatar in S.A. 1777 and completed by King Valandil in T.A. 1126 as a living barrier against Orcs and Hillmen, stretching from Mitheithel to Baranduin above the Old Forest; now broken by many gaps but still impressive and lethal with blossoms and thorns.[file:31]

plants.name: New beech and pine woods
plants.note: Scattered secondary forests of beech with some pines have sprung up where land has been left fallow, forming patches of woodland amid thistles and brambles.[file:31]

plants.name: River‑basin crops
plants.note: Along the Gwathló farmers grow wheat and other grains, peas, cassona (a small potato‑like tuber) and in uplands eorna, a hardy barley‑like grain, making the basin the breadbasket of Eriador.[file:31]

people.description: Cardolan’s population mixes Dúnedain princes and warriors, Eriadoran Northmen, Dunlendings, Beffraen and later southern refugees; after the Kingdom’s fall the land fragments into rival principalities, mercenary companies, peasant rebels, Gondorian garrisons and eventually bandit‑ridden ruins under the shadow of Angmar.

list.name: Dúnedain of Cardolan
list.description: High Men descended from Númenor who form the royal house, barons and professional soldiers of the Kingdom; they reach a peak of prosperity under King Tarandil but are later shattered by disasters like Cameth Brin and the Witch‑king’s wars, with the last Prince Argil assassinated and survivors drifting to Arthedain and Gondor.[file:31]

list.name: Eriadoran Northmen and mercenaries
list.description: Tall blond horsemen from Rhovanion and other Northman stocks who settle as mercenaries and burh‑folk, especially in Feotar and the eastern marches, later revolting against cruel lords and sometimes forming self‑governing communities.[file:31]

list.name: Dunlendings and Dunnish migrants
list.description: Descendants of the Daen Lintis who migrate into Cardolan from Enedhwaith and Dunland, forming hill‑loving herders, Saralainn clans and later the Kingdom of Saralainn south of the Gwathló after Gordaigh Trollbane’s rise.[file:31]

list.name: Beffraen of Rast Vorn
list.description: A relatively primitive folk akin to Drúedain, inhabiting the dark woods of the Rast Vorn peninsula; they are secretive, use the treacherous forest and coasts to remain largely outside Dúnadan control and maintain their own customs.[file:31]

list.name: Urban folk and guilds of Tharbad
list.description: Merchants, craftsmen, guildsmen, smugglers and refugees crowd the fen‑ringed city of Tharbad, whose power shifts from Cardolan’s kings to Gondor’s Canotar and then to powerful guilds as war, flood and plague erode royal authority.[file:31]

list.name: Saralainn clans and southern tribes
list.description: Rowdy, semi‑independent clans of Saralainn and the Gwathló mouthlands whose culture blends Dunnish traditions and refugee influences, often providing both raiders and hardy defenders in the south.[file:31]

land.name: Cardolan proper
land.description: All lands between Baranduin and Gwathló–Mitheithel as far north as the Great East Road, roughly six hundred miles by one hundred and fifty, divided by the Old North Road into Minhiriath (Region of the Streams) and Mintyrnath (Region of the Downs). [file:31]
land.biome_hint: treeless temperate plains with river basins and red downs

land.name: Minhiriath
land.description: The southwestern half of Cardolan, a nearly featureless, gently seaward‑sloping plain cut by countless small rivers and streams toward Baranduin, Gwathló and the sea, with generally poor soils suited mainly to sheep, except along streams and in the richly silted Gwathló basin.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: semi‑arid temperate plain with many streams and floodplain pockets

land.name: Mintyrnath
land.description: The northeastern half, a complex of red downs, highlands and basins including the Pinnath Ceren, Barrow‑downs, South Downs, Girithlin highlands and parts of the Gwathló basin; windswept, infertile ridges alternate with richer floodplains.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: windswept hills and downs with thin turf and scattered woods

land.name: Barrow-downs (Tyrn Gorthad) and South Downs
land.description: A line of ridges running northeast with steep southwestern faces and long gentle slopes, largely treeless and infertile, famed for ancient Adan passage‑graves, royal barrows and later the Wight‑haunted mounds of Cardolan’s princes.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: open chalk and limestone ridges with short turf and barrow mounds

land.name: Old Forest (Taur Iaur)
land.description: Dark, dangerous woodland west of the Barrow‑downs between them and Baranduin, where resentful ancient trees remain from the primordial forest and house Tom Bombadil’s domain; little inhabited and largely unexplored by Cardolani.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: dense temperate old‑growth forest with hostile, uneven ground

land.name: Pinnath Ceren and Girithlin highlands
land.description: Red‑tinted ridges atop a small plateau and the higher Girithlin highlands, rich in iron ores and marble, historically centers of resistance and mining, but exposed to erosion and only marginally fertile beyond scattered farming pockets.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: rocky red and white ridges, thin soils, cool and windy

land.name: Gwathló basin and Nîn‑in‑Eilph
land.description: The wide, flood‑prone basin of the Gwathló, narrow near the sea but spreading to almost eighty miles near Tharbad, with extremely fertile silted farmlands on the floodplain and the four‑hundred‑square‑mile Swanfleet Marshes of alkali bogs and swans where Mitheithel and Glanduin join.[file:31]
