land.biome_hint: rich alluvial farmland and extensive freshwater marshes

land.name: Rast Vorn and Eryn Vorn
land.description: A dark, mostly unexplored peninsula south of the Baranduin’s mouth with treacherous shoal‑ridden, fog‑shrouded coasts and the ancient Dark Woods inhabited by Beffraen; considered one of the most dangerous coasts known to Dúnadan mariners.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: coastal dark forest on broken, foggy, reef‑strewn shores

land.name: Tharbad and environs
land.description: The great river‑port city raised above surrounding fenland at the Gwathló crossing, protected by immense dikes and causeways and straddling the river with wondrous bridges; later reduced by siege, plague and flood to a decaying but still strategic free city in the marshes.[file:31]
land.biome_hint: urban island in broad, seasonally flooded river marsh

regions.notes: This Cardolan object treats the realm at its mid‑Third Age extent around T.A. 1640 as a single climatic and cultural region node, combining Minhiriath and Mintyrnath into one macro‑region for your model; more detailed political units like Saralainn, Tharbad, individual principalities or the Warlord’s domain can be spun out later as separate objects if finer resolution is needed.[file:31]
regions.administrative_organization: Hereditary Princes, appointed Canotar, appointed lesser officials, elected Guildmasters; all land nominally held by Princes, given to smallholders in fief.
regions.population: 48,000 Cardolani (not including populations of Saralainn or the Cantons).
regions.military: Each Prince and Lord commands about 100 warriors in a personal retinue and can raise 200-800 levy troops in 2-3 weeks. Most often, however, the Lords rely upon mercenary bands and the Dunnish tribes in the region for military muscle. Gondor and Arthedain also maintain garrisons in Cardolan to ensure the safety of the trade routes.

regions.name: Lebennin, Dor-en-Emyl, Tolfalas
regions.description: Lebennin, the Place of Five Waters, is the rich, river-laced heartland of Gondor between the White Mountains and the Anduin, a fertile province of rolling fields, busy ports and Sea-lords whose fleets fight Corsairs and smugglers across the Bay of Belfalas.

climate.description: Lebennin enjoys a mild, maritime-influenced temperate climate: cool, river-fed air off the White Mountains and the Bay of Belfalas keeps the province green, with long growing seasons in the lowlands and cooler, windier conditions in the highland vales.
climate.temperature_pattern: Lowlands along the five rivers and the Anduin see warm to hot summers and cool but generally not severe winters, while the upland vales of the White Mountains are cooler year-round and can turn harsh in winter storms blowing down from the snow peaks.[file:30]
climate.precipitation_pattern: Moist winds from the Bay of Belfalas and uplift along the White Mountains bring regular rain and mountain runoff, feeding the five rivers and creating lush fields, marshy river mouths and the foggy, shifting channels of the Ethir Anduin; snow is common on the Ered Nimrais but only occasional and short-lived on the coastal plain.[file:30]
climate.notes: Lebennin’s climate is defined by the interaction of the White Mountains, which trap and release meltwater into five cool rivers, and the open Bay of Belfalas and Anduin delta, which provide humidity, sea breezes and occasional storms that shape both agriculture and seafaring.[file:30]

fauna.description: The module focuses on history, peoples and ships more than detailed wildlife, but Lebennin clearly supports abundant river and coastal life, marsh creatures in the Ethir Anduin, mountain and highland fauna in the Ered Nimrais vales, and domestic herds and horses in its farm country.

creatures.name: River and marsh life of the five waters
creatures.note: Fish, waterfowl and wetland animals thrive along the Gilrain, Erui and the other rivers, and especially in the wide, marshy Ethir Anduin where smugglers and Marshmen hide among reeds and shifting channels.[file:30]

creatures.name: Coastal and bay creatures
creatures.note: Sea birds, dolphins and other marine life accompany the startling variety of ships that crowd the Bay of Belfalas off Lebennin’s coasts, feeding in rich coastal waters frequented by fishermen and privateers.[file:30]

creatures.name: Highland wildlife of the White Mountains
creatures.note: Goats, hardy mountain game and predators haunt the gleaming White Mountain vales above Lebennin, sharing the rugged uplands with reclusive Drúedain and Dunnish clans.[file:30]

creatures.name: Domestic animals of the farms and roads
creatures.note: Cattle, sheep, horses and pack animals are widespread in the rolling fields and along the Gondor Road, supporting Lebennin’s role as a breadbasket and its constant caravan and naval logistics.[file:30]

flora.description: Lebennin is explicitly described as a land of green fields and lilies, with five cool rivers watering fertile plains, orchards and farms, flower-rich meadows and marshes, backed by forested and rocky slopes of the White Mountains.

plants.name: Green fields of Lebennin
plants.note: Wide, cultivated fields of grain and pasture form the core landscape, the ‘green fields of Lebennin’ celebrated in song, supplying much of Gondor’s food.[file:30]

plants.name: White lilies and golden bells
plants.note: White lilies and the golden bells of mallos and alfirin sway in the sea-wind across Lebennin’s meadows and river-vales, marking it as one of Gondor’s most floral provinces.[file:30]

plants.name: River and marsh vegetation
plants.note: Reeds, rushes and other wetland plants grow thickly along the Ethir Anduin and other marshy reaches, providing cover for smugglers and wildlife alike.[file:30]

plants.name: Mountain woods and scrub
plants.note: Forested hills and scrubby slopes in the northern vales of the White Mountains frame the province, with pine and mixed woodland around the older Daen Coentis and Drúedain refuges.[file:30]

people.description: Lebennin’s population is a layered mix: Númenórean-descended Dúnedain and urban Gondorians in the cities and ports, rural folk and Dunnish highlanders in the uplands, Drúedain in remote mountain valleys, Marshmen in the Ethir Anduin, and a constant presence of Sea-lords, sailors and smugglers bound up with the Bay of Belfalas.

list.name: Dúnedain and Sea-lords of Lebennin
list.description: High Men of Gondor, especially the Sea-lords whose power is rooted in Pelargir and the coastal towns; they command fleets, administer the province and lead the struggle against Corsairs and Haradan privateers.[file:30]

list.name: Urban folk of Pelargir and river towns
list.description: Merchants, artisans, sailors and laborers crowd Pelargir—the largest and oldest city of Gondor—and the lesser ports, living from shipbuilding, trade and supplying the Royal Fleet and merchant traffic.[file:30]

list.name: Dunnish highlanders
list.description: Descendants of the Daen Coentis who inhabit isolated highland villages in the White Mountains; some cling to old ways and occasional cultural nationalism, sometimes clashing with Gondorian authority.[file:30]

list.name: Drúedain (Drughu, Woses)
list.description: Secretive, reclusive clans of Drúedain in remote White Mountain valleys, preserving ancient customs, mysterious stoneworks and semi-magical lore, largely independent of Gondor.[file:30]

list.name: Marshmen of the Ethir Anduin
list.description: Hardy folk of the Anduin delta and coastal marshes who know the shifting channels and shallows; some act as guides or smugglers in the Ethir Anduin labyrinth.[file:30]

list.name: Smugglers, privateers and pirates
list.description: Semi-legal privateers, outright pirates and smugglers operate from coves, swamps and hidden harbors along the coasts and the Ethir Anduin, raiding trade and dodging royal duties despite the Sea-lords’ efforts.[file:30]

list.name: Haradrim and Corsairs (external foes)
list.description: Haradrim traders, privateers and Corsairs of Umbar are ever-present enemies and rivals on the Bay of Belfalas, raiding Gondor’s shipping and coasts and shaping Lebennin’s militarized seafaring culture.[file:30]

land.name: Lebennin proper – Place of Five Waters
land.description: The central province of Gondor bounded by the White Mountains to the north, Gilrain and Dor-en-Ernil to the west, the Anduin and South Ithilien to the east, and the Bay of Belfalas and Ethir Anduin to the south, defined by five cool rivers rising in the gleaming Ered Nimrais.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: fertile temperate lowlands cut by multiple rivers

land.name: White Mountains (Ered Nimrais) above Lebennin
land.description: Snow-capped mountains forming the northern boundary of Lebennin, with gleaming peaks, remote vales, Daen and Drúedain sites, and passes leading toward Dunharrow and other ancient highland centers.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: alpine highlands with snow peaks and forested lower vales

land.name: Bay of Belfalas and coastal fringe
land.description: Lebennin’s southern edge meets the Bay of Belfalas, where numerous ships crowd the waters off its ports, smugglers and Corsairs prowl, and storms and reefs challenge even skilled Gondorian captains.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: temperate sea-coast with bays, shoals and busy shipping lanes

land.name: Ethir Anduin
land.description: The swampy delta and marshy flanks at the mouth of the Anduin, a maze of shifting channels and shoals where smugglers avoid royal duties and Haradrim and Corsairs hunt among unpredictable waters.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: large river delta with brackish marsh and sandbars

land.name: Pelargir
land.description: The great port city on the Anduin founded in S.A. 2350 by the Faithful, oldest city of Gondor, capital of Lebennin and home of the Royal Fleet, the finest harbor in the kingdom and a focal point in the war against Corsairs.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: major river-port city on broad temperate river

land.name: Balimur Swamps and Balibogach
land.description: Marshy region associated with smugglers’ stronghold Balibogach in southern Lebennin, where Balimur Runner boats with shallow draft slip along hidden waterways.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: freshwater marsh with narrow channels and smuggler paths

land.name: Road network – Gondor Road and coastal routes
land.description: The Gondor Road runs through Lebennin to Linhir and points west, with busy inns along the routes to Dol Amroth and Lamedon; traffic remains brisk despite raids and war.[file:30]
land.biome_hint: well-travelled roads across rolling farmland and river crossings

regions.notes: This Lebennin object treats the province as the central Gondorian coastal heartland described in Sea-Lords of Gondor, suitable as a single climatic and cultural region node: sub-areas like Pelargir’s urban districts, Balimur Swamps or individual mountain valleys can be broken out later as separate region or site objects if you want finer resolution.[file:30]

regions.name: Bree, Shire, Weather Hills, South Arthedain, South Downs
regions.description: Arthedain is the northern Dúnedain kingdom in Eriador, cradled between the Blue Mountains and the Misty Mountains and bounded by the rivers Lhûn and Baranduin, a cool, sparsely populated realm of rocky hills, downs, marshes, and the Shire, ruled from Fornost as the last Númenórean realm in the North in T.A. 1640.

climate.description: Arthedain has a generally cool, windy climate with rocky soils, ample rivers, and enough rainfall to support mixed woodland, downs, and the fertile fields of the Shire, but winters are long and increasingly harsh while summers can be brief and intense.
climate.temperature_pattern: Historically cool but not brutal, with fresh summers and cold winters moderated by sea winds from the Gulf of Lhûn; after the Great Plague winters lengthen and hold snow into late season while summers arrive suddenly and can scorch the land for weeks.[file:25]
climate.precipitation_pattern: The kingdom is well-watered by the Lhûn, Baranduin, and many streams, with regular rain and occasional snow in most regions, heavier snow and ice in the far north toward Forochel, and persistent mist and dampness in places like the Midgewater Marshes and the Barrow-downs.[file:25]
climate.notes: Arthedain’s geography—nestled between two great mountain chains and bounded by major rivers—gives it a sheltered yet wind-swept character; despite its northern latitude, the combination of maritime influence from Lindon and extensive river systems keeps the realm habitable and relatively green compared to the wastes beyond Forochel or the high Misty Mountains.[file:25]

fauna.description: The text emphasizes politics, history and settlement more than detailed animal lists but implies a northern mix of big game, predators, marsh life, and domesticated animals, with harsh northern variants in Forochel and more tamed fauna in the Shire.

creatures.name: Northern big game
creatures.note: Reindeer, elk, deer and similar large herbivores in the hills and northern reaches near Forochel and the North Downs, providing hunting for Dúnedain and Lossoth.[file:25]

creatures.name: Wolves
creatures.note: Wolf packs roam the wild uplands and borders, especially in the north and around the downs; in severe winters they become more numerous and dangerous.[file:25]

creatures.name: Bears and hill-trolls
creatures.note: Northern bears and hill-trolls appear in wilder hill-country and along the approaches from the Ettenmoors and Angmar, threatening frontier forts and travelers.[file:25]

creatures.name: Marsh and fen creatures
creatures.note: In the Midgewater Marshes and other low, wet areas, swarms of biting midges, insects (neekerbreekers), amphibians and marsh birds thrive in stagnant pools and bogs.[file:25]

creatures.name: Domestic livestock of the Shire
creatures.note: Sheep, cattle, pigs, poultry and working ponies are widespread in the hobbit farmlands, along with typical small mammals and birds of hedgerows and fields.[file:25]

creatures.name: River life of the Baranduin and Lhûn
creatures.note: Fish, waterfowl, otters and other riverine animals occur along the Baranduin and Lhûn, particularly around Lake Evendim and the estuary of the Lhûn.[file:25]

flora.description: Arthedain spans several biomes: tundra-like ice plains in the far north around Forochel, rocky downs and hills in the interior, chalk downs and tower-crowned hills in the west, barren limestone Weather Hills and boggy marshes in the southeast, and the richly farmed, hedged fields of the Shire in the south.

plants.name: Tundra mosses and grasses of Forochel
plants.note: Around the Ice-bay of Forochel the land supports hardy mosses, lichens and low grasses, with few trees except in sheltered spots near the roots of the Blue Mountains.[file:25]

plants.name: Heaths and rocky hill-grass
plants.note: In the North Downs and other upland areas, thin soils and exposed rock carry rough grasses, heather-like low plants and scattered shrubs, with trees mostly restricted to folds and sheltered valleys.[file:25]

plants.name: Chalk and limestone downs vegetation
plants.note: The White Downs, Far Downs and similar chalk hills bear short turf, scrub and sparse trees, forming open pastoral country west of the Shire.[file:25]

plants.name: Marsh plants of Midgewater and lowlands
plants.note: Reeds, rushes and bog grasses dominate the Midgewater Marshes and other soggy lowlands below the Weather Hills, creating insect-ridden, treacherous ground.[file:25]

plants.name: Riverbank woods
plants.note: Poplar and willow line the banks of the Baranduin, with scattered birch, spruce and pine, especially along slower, meandering stretches near the Shire.[file:25]

plants.name: Shire fields and hedgerows
plants.note: The Shire is described as fertile and orderly, with grain fields, gardens, orchards, grassy hills and hedgerows, especially in the Green Hill Country and around settled hobbit townships.[file:25]

people.description: Arthedain’s population is dominated by the high Men of the Dúnedain, with important minorities including the hobbits of the Shire, the Lossoth of Forochel, rivermen on the northern waters, Bree-landers at the crossroads, and scattered Northmen and Dunlendish groups on the borders.

list.name: Dúnedain of Arthedain
list.description: High Men descended from the Faithful of Númenor, forming a relatively uniform but small population with long lifespans, great physical and mental strength, and a contemplative, Elven-influenced culture; they rule the kingdom from Fornost and maintain the line of kings.[file:25]

list.name: Lossoth of Forochel
list.description: The Snowmen of the far north, a poor, primitive, nomadic folk who roam the ice-plains north of Eriador and Angmar, hunting big game and living off the cold sea in a harsh environment.[file:25]

list.name: Bree-landers
list.description: Mixed folk of Bree and its environs, including men of various stocks and hobbits, living in and around a trenched, hedged village at the junction of the Greenway and Great East Road, once a vibrant trade-town but fading by T.A. 1640.[file:25]

list.name: Rivermen of the northern waters
list.description: River-folk who ply the Baranduin and other northern rivers, claiming the waters as their own and serving trade and communication between Arthedain, Lindon and other regions.[file:25]

list.name: Hobbits of the Shire
list.description: Small, hearth-loving folk who settled the Shire in T.A. 1601 under grant from King Argeleb II; they farm the rich southeastern lands of Arthedain and maintain a semi-autonomous, peaceful society that survives even the downfall of the kingdom.[file:25]

list.name: Elves of Lindon (nearby)
list.description: Though outside Arthedain proper, the Elves of Lindon and the Grey Havens are close neighbors across the Lhûn, providing crucial aid against Angmar and cultural and political support to the North-kingdom.[file:25]

land.name: Forochel and the Icy North
land.description: The far northern portion of Arthedain reaches toward the Ice-bay of Forochel and the Forodwaith, a realm of ice-plains, tundra and bitter cold, inhabited mainly by the Lossoth and visited by Dúnedain only in times of dire need.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: arctic coastal tundra and sea-ice

land.name: North Downs
land.description: High rolling hills east of Lake Evendim, forming rocky ridges that march across northeastern Arthedain like massive natural altars; they shelter and overlook Fornost and serve as a defensive barrier toward Angmar.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: rocky downs with thin soils and open grass-heath

land.name: Lake Nenuial (Lake Evendim)
land.description: A large blue lake in north-central Arthedain, sheltered on three sides by the Hills of Evendim, with the ruined first capital of Annúminas on its southern shore.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: cool upland lake with stony shores and reeds

land.name: Annúminas
land.description: Elendil’s original capital of Arnor on the southern shore of Lake Evendim, once a glittering Númenórean city and now a crumbling ruin of marble and stone razed in T.A. 1409.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: ruins on windy lake-hills

land.name: Fornost Erain
land.description: The Northern Fortress of the Kings, built on the southern slopes of the North Downs, ringed by dikes and walls, now the capital of Arthedain and seat of its Royal Court and Seers in T.A. 1640.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: fortified hill-city in rocky uplands

land.name: Baranduin / Brandywine River
land.description: A swift yet meandering river flowing over 500 miles from Lake Evendim to the Sea south of Harlindon, forming a major barrier and artery, with key crossings at the Brandywine Bridge and Sarn Ford and providing the Shire with water and fertile valley lands.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: temperate river valley with mixed woodland and farmland

land.name: Southern Arthedain and the Shire
land.description: The fertile southern part of the kingdom, once neglected by Dúnedain concerned with war, now intensively farmed and settled by hobbits of the Shire, with rolling hills, rich fields and the Green Hill Country.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: temperate lowland farmland and hedged hills

land.name: Weather Hills and Amon Sûl
land.description: Barren rocky ridges in the southeast forming a natural wall along the border, culminating at Weathertop (Amon Sûl), a thousand-foot hill that once bore the chief northern palantír tower and still dominates the Great East Road.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: treeless limestone ridges and exposed summits

land.name: Midgewater Marshes
land.description: Buzzing bogs below the Weather Hills, carved in glacial times and now collecting stagnant water in treacherous pools swarming with midges, making overland travel slow and unpleasant.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: cool, insect-ridden freshwater bog

land.name: White Downs, Far Downs and Tower Hills
land.description: Chalk cliffs and downs in the west of the Shire leading up to the Emyn Beraid (Tower Hills), where three ancient white towers overlook the estuary of the Lhûn and house a westward-looking palantír.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: chalk downs with short turf and coastal influence

land.name: Bree-land and the Greenway
land.description: Region around Bree-hill where the Great East Road crosses the old North–South road (Greenway); Bree is surrounded by ditches, dikes and hedges rather than walls, and serves as a weathered but still significant stop for travelers and traders.[file:25]
land.biome_hint: temperate hill-country with fields and woodland patches

regions.notes: This Arthedain object is meant to capture the realm as described around T.A. 1640 in Rangers of the North, with climate and landforms usable as a base layer for pathfinding, biome assignment and climate modeling; more focused regions like Angmar, Cardolan and Rhudaur are handled in their own supplements.[file:25]
rulers: Marl Tarma, Captain of the Rangers
regions.administrative_organization: Hereditary monarchy, administrative positions held by appointed officers and hereditary nobility; land divided into 63 noble and 24 royal fiefs; smallholders rent land from the nobility, or from the King in exchange for service.
regions.population: 50,000 Dúnedain (not including the Shire and Bree-land).
regions.military: Royal Forces: 540 Men at Arms. 370 Cavalry. 1,000 Rangers. Noble Forces: 2,150 Men at Arms. 620 Cavalry. 3,000 Levy.

regions.name: Eregion
regions.supplement: Lórien & The Halls of the Elven Smiths
regions.description: Eregion is a Noldorin-founded realm on the western side of the Misty Mountains, centered on the great city of Ost-in-Edhil and famed for its jewel-smiths, close friendship with Khazad-dûm, and the forging of the Rings of Power.

climate.description: Mild temperate climate on the western side of the Misty Mountains, with warm summers, cool but not severe winters, and enough rainfall to support extensive agriculture and holly woods.
climate.temperature_pattern: Cool to mild winters and warm summers, moderated by proximity to the Glanduin and the western lowlands rather than high mountain plateaus.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Regular rain spread across the year, sufficient to sustain grain fields, vineyards, orchards, and scattered woodlands; heavy snows are more typical in the nearby mountains than in the cultivated lowlands around Ost-in-Edhil.
climate.notes: The realm lies between the Sirannon, Glanduin, and the western foothills of the Misty Mountains; it is drier and more open than Lórien proper but greener than the lands further west.

fauna.description: The text focuses on cities, crafts, and politics rather than animal life. Eregion around Ost-in-Edhil appears as a tamed landscape of farms and estates, so naturally occurring fauna is largely displaced or subdued near the city, with typical temperate woodland and river species persisting in the more rural hills.

flora.description: Eregion combines carefully planned agriculture and ornamental planting with symbolic holly trees; the lands around Ost-in-Edhil are divided into triangular fields of grain, vineyards, orchards, and pasture, with the holly serving as an emblem of the realm and appearing at gates and gardens.

plants.name: Holly trees of Eregion
plants.note: Large hollies that are the symbol of the realm; a pair stands in monumental planters near Ost-in-Edhil’s western approach, and holly appears in gardens and markers.

plants.name: Vineyards
plants.note: Extensive vineyards around Ost-in-Edhil, producing wines described as the finest in Middle-earth, surpassing even later Dorwinion vintages.

plants.name: Wheat fields
plants.note: Triangular fields of wheat forming a major staple crop in the countryside supporting Ost-in-Edhil and, indirectly, Khazad-dûm.

plants.name: Barley fields
plants.note: Large barley fields interspersed with wheat and pasture, part of the structured agricultural mosaic around the city.

plants.name: Orchards
plants.note: Fruit orchards scattered among fields and estates, providing fresh produce to the city and complementing the vineyards.

plants.name: Gardens and ornamental trees
plants.note: Formal and semi-formal gardens, tree-lined courts, and planted terraces within Ost-in-Edhil and around noble houses, used both for beauty and for elite recreation.

people.description: Eregion is a Noldorin-founded realm ruled initially by Galadriel and Celeborn, with Celebrimbor and the Gwaith-i-Mírdain dominating its crafts and politics. The population includes Noldor, Sindar, and Silvan Elves, with close ties to the Dwarves of Khazad-dûm and scattered rustic Silvan shepherds in the less-settled triangle between the Sirannon and the Glanduin.

list.name: Noldor of Eregion
list.description: Exiled High Elves who founded the realm after the War of the Jewels; they are master architects, jewel-smiths, and scholars, concentrated in Ost-in-Edhil and in estates on the rolling hills to the north.

list.name: Gwaith-i-Mírdain
list.description: The Brotherhood of Jewel-smiths, headquartered at the Mirdainthron near Ost-in-Edhil. They are a tightly knit order of smiths and crafters, strictly organized with ranks such as Lord Smiths, Master Smiths, Hall Masters, and Smiths, and they ultimately create the Rings of Power.

list.name: Sindar of Eregion
list.description: Grey Elves who dwell in and around Ost-in-Edhil, some bearing memories and grudges from Doriath’s sack by Dwarves of Nogrod, making them more distrustful of Dwarves even while living beside Khazad-dûm.

list.name: Silvan Elves (rural shepherds)
list.description: Rustic Silvan Elves in the sparsely inhabited triangle between the Sirannon and the Glanduin, living as shepherds and smallholders far from the dense Noldorin urban culture.

list.name: Dwarves of Khazad-dûm
list.description: Allied Dwarves who trade extensively with the Noldor of Eregion, exchanging ores and craft for Elven goods and knowledge; their friendship is largely driven by shared interest in craft and learning.

list.name: Galadriel and Celeborn
list.description: Queen and King of Eregion in its early years, residing in a modest but elegant city house and a country estate; Galadriel is the political and prophetic mind pushing for alliance against Morgoth’s lingering evil.

list.name: Celebrimbor
list.description: Master Smith, founder of the Gwaith-i-Mírdain and de facto leader of the craft community; arguably the second-greatest Elven smith after Fëanor, and the maker of the Three Rings.

land.name: Eregion proper
land.description: All lands north of the river Glanduin and south of the Mitheithel/Bruinen, with Ost-in-Edhil on its southern edge and most Elven settlements north of the Glanduin.
land.biome_hint: temperate rolling uplands with rivers, cultivated fields, and scattered woodland

land.name: Ost-in-Edhil
land.description: The great Noldorin fortress-city built on a granite bluff above the Glanduin, planned on an intricate triangular grid and resembling a gigantic stone ship facing west over Lake Estelin, with multiple districts at different elevations.
land.biome_hint: walled stone city on a river bluff with ornamental gardens

land.name: Estelin
land.description: Small lake west of Ost-in-Edhil formed by a natural rock dam later enhanced by Noldorin engineers; serves as a placid foreground to the city’s ‘ship’ prow.
land.biome_hint: temperate river-lake on a broadening of the Glanduin

land.name: Glanduin
land.description: Main river flowing below Ost-in-Edhil’s bluff, varying between swift and foaming and wide and sluggish, carrying trade and water past the city.
land.biome_hint: temperate river with alternating rapids and slow reaches

land.name: Sirannon
land.description: Tributary joining the Glanduin just upriver from the city; its valley and the narrow water tunnels provide access toward Khazad-dûm and to the Mirdainthron’s water entry.
land.biome_hint: mountain-fed river descending through foothills

land.name: Lantalaith
land.description: Small, swift river called “tumbling laughter” that drains the hills north of Ost-in-Edhil and guards the northwest edge of the city bluff.
land.biome_hint: small fast-flowing hill stream

land.name: Triangle between Sirannon and Glanduin
land.description: Sparsely inhabited wedge of land between the two rivers, used mainly by rustic Silvan shepherds rather than Noldorin estates.
land.biome_hint: open pasture and scrub in a river angle

land.name: Fields and estates north of Ost-in-Edhil
land.description: Rolling hills covered in triangular plots of wheat, barley, vineyards, and pasture land, punctuated by Noldorin country houses belonging to prominent families.
land.biome_hint: intensively cultivated temperate uplands

land.name: Tharbad Road and western approach
land.description: Paved road running east from the west along the Glanduin’s steep bank, marked thirty miles from the city by twin holly-pillared obelisks welcoming kindred to ‘The Fortress of the Eldar’.
land.biome_hint: riverbank road along rocky slopes

land.name: Ost-in-Edhil districts
land.description: Tiered districts of the city (Tirannon, Ardhlarem, Medhlarem, Monde, Pennahithri, Gaiadhurm, High Citadel, Tirmmen) forming a multi-level urban layout with noble residences, commercial areas, harbors, misty terraces, wooded quarters, and a military citadel.
land.biome_hint: stone terraces with mixed ornamental vegetation at several elevations

regions.notes: This object treats Eregion as the larger political and geographic region surrounding Ost-in-Edhil in the Second Age. Lórien, specific city sites, and Khazad-dûm are to be modeled as separate region or site objects.

regions.name: Lórien
regions.supplement: Lórien & The Halls of The Elven Smiths
regions.description: Lórien, the Golden Wood, is a secluded Elven forest-realm east of the Misty Mountains, ruled by Galadriel and Celeborn and defined by its golden mallorn trees, timeless beauty, and potent yet subtle Ring-lore.

climate.description: Temperate, sheltered valley climate east of the Misty Mountains, with relatively mild winters and long, gentle springs that support the characteristic golden mallorn woods.
climate.temperature_pattern: Cool winters and warm but not scorching summers, moderated by the surrounding mountains and river system.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Reliable rainfall across most of the year, enough to maintain dense deciduous and mallorn forests and a lush understorey; fogs and mists are common near the river.
climate.notes: The land is under Elven and Ring-influenced stewardship, so the perceived climate is more gentle and timeless than raw geography alone would suggest.

fauna.description: The supplement emphasizes Elven culture and landscape more than specific animals; Lórien can be assumed to host typical temperate forest fauna—deer, small mammals, birds—largely kept in balance by Elven guardianship.

flora.description: Lórien is defined by its great golden mallorn trees, silver trunks, and seasonal golden leaves, alongside more typical temperate river-forest species; the understorey includes ferns, soft grasses, and carefully tended clearings.

plants.name: Mallorn trees
plants.note: Iconic tall trees with smooth silver-grey bark and golden leaves that define the visual character of Lórien’s forests.

plants.name: Riverside willows and alders
plants.note: Trees along the banks of the Celebrant and other streams, supporting a humid microclimate and stabilizing the banks.

plants.name: Forest ferns and mosses
plants.note: Thick ferns and carpets of moss that cover the forest floor and tree roots in the shaded parts of the Golden Wood.

plants.name: Elven gardens and clearings
plants.note: Carefully curated glades with grasses and flowering plants used for paths, gathering places, and vantage points beneath the mallorns.

people.description: Lórien is inhabited mainly by Silvan and Sindarin Elves under the rule of Galadriel and Celeborn, with a culture focused on preservation, song, subtle magic, and cautious isolation from the outside world.

list.name: Galadhrim
list.description: The Silvan and Sindarin Elves of Lórien, known for their skill in woodcraft, stealth, and the construction of flets among the mallorn trees.

list.name: Galadriel
list.description: Lady of Lórien, a Noldorin princess and Ring-bearer, whose wisdom and foresight shape the realm’s defenses and long-term stance against Sauron.

list.name: Celeborn
list.description: Lord of Lórien and consort of Galadriel, providing military and diplomatic leadership and embodying Sindarin traditions within the Golden Wood.

land.name: Lórien proper (Golden Wood)
land.description: Forested realm on the east side of the Misty Mountains, centered on the mallorn woods along the river Celebrant, bounded by rivers and fields that separate it from neighboring lands.
land.biome_hint: dense temperate deciduous forest with magical mallorn stands along a major river

land.name: Celebrant (Silverlode)
land.description: Main river flowing through the heart of Lórien, providing water, fog, and reflective vistas beneath the mallorns.
land.biome_hint: clear, cold forest river with wooded banks

regions.notes: This object treats Lórien as the Golden Wood region as a whole. Tightly defined sites like Caras Galadhon can be modeled as separate site objects if you want finer granularity.
regions.administrative_organization: Lórien functions as a set of interdependent units called Glades, each responsible for one aspect of the Elves' life. The King coordinates the actions of the Glades and appoints the Masters from among his most skillful subjects. Land is held in common, but the Elves claim their homes and their movable goods as their own.
regions.military: 100 Wardens. 1,300 Guardians.
regions.symbol: A Golden Mallorn Tree on a Green Field

regions.name: Angmar
regions.description: Angmar is a cold, militarized realm north of Arnor, ruled by the Witch-king of the Nazgûl; it exists primarily as a war machine, built on harsh land, hardened frontier peasants, and armies of men, orcs, and monsters. Geology consists of deeply eroded limestone that robs the surface soil of water, with igneous and metamorphic outcrops near the mountains. carn dûm is built on a massive block of dull red granite.

climate.description: Cold, harsh land north of Arnor, with strong winds, poor soils, and a rigorous environment that forces even the nobility to work the land. Mean annual temperature: 22-32° F. Average low: Gwaeron -20° F. Average high: Ivanneth 77° F. Average annual precipitation: 10-20 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: Long, severe winters with frequent frosts and snow, short cool summers; tundra and windswept highlands dominate much of the region.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Irregular rain and snow over poor soils, with moisture concentrated in mountain valleys and along streams; blizzards and freezing rain are common in winter.
climate.notes: The land is infertile and the climate severe; only hardened peasants can maintain themselves here, which suits Angmar’s role as a militarized frontier rather than a prosperous agrarian kingdom.

fauna.description: Angmar’s fauna is dominated by large predators and dangerous creatures such as trolls, giants, dragons, wolves, wargs, bears, and orcs; brown bears directly compete with wolves in Angmar and Rhudaur’s forests.

creatures.name: Brown bears
creatures.note: Weigh 400–800 pounds; rarely attack humans, are omnivorous with fish as a main food source, and become inactive in winter, often sleeping for much of the season.

creatures.name: Wolves
creatures.note: Direct competitors with bears for prey in the forests of Angmar and Rhudaur; part of the wider predator guild of the region.

creatures.name: Wargs
creatures.note: Malign, intelligent wolves associated with Angmar’s forces; used as mounts or terror weapons in the Witch-king’s campaigns.

creatures.name: Trolls
creatures.note: Cold-climate trolls with lairs (‘troll holes’) described in detail; formidable monsters dwelling in remote hills and mountains.

creatures.name: Giants
creatures.note: Giants in the northern Misty Mountains and adjacent highlands, used as high-level threats in Angmar-focused adventures.

creatures.name: Dragons (e.g., Zarak Dûm / Scorba, Corlagon the Red)
creatures.note: Named dragons lairing near the northern Misty Mountains and Angmar’s sphere of influence, each associated with immense hoards and catastrophic potential.

creatures.name: Orcs
creatures.note: Common in Angmar’s forts and patrols, serving as the basic infantry for the Witch-king’s armies.

flora.description: Despite Angmar’s harshness, the module provides rich herb-lore centered on Dúnedain and healers, with plants used for healing, poisons, and general survival in a cold frontier landscape.

plants.name: Aloe
plants.note: Succulent cultivated by Dúnedain healers; its juice or salve doubles the healing rate of minor cuts and burns and cures 1–4 points of burn damage.

plants.name: Arfandas
plants.note: Wild and garden flower with pale yellow petals; stems are used in poultices, often with comfrey, to promote proper bone knitting.

plants.name: Arkasu
plants.note: Mixture of three or four herbs, prepared as a salve; antiseptic, pain-killing, and doubles the healing rate of major wounds while restoring 2–12 hits.

plants.name: Arlan
plants.note: Small garden plant with blue flowers; leaf poultices ease bruises, healing 1–6 hits if wild and 4–9 if cultivated.

plants.name: Arlians Slipper
plants.note: White forest flower; boiled roots produce vapours that are inhaled to relieve congestion.

plants.name: Armanumas
plants.note: Tall garden plant with long leaves; used on bruises, sprains, and damaged tendons or ligaments, doubling healing and reducing swelling, whether fresh, dried, or in weaker salve form.

plants.name: Arunya
plants.note: Red flower grown for beauty and medicine; root juice, taken in measured doses, induces deep sleep or unconsciousness for surgical procedures.

plants.name: Athelas
plants.note: Dúnedain herb cultivated wherever they dwell; crushed leaves in hot water produce baths and vapors that ease physical pain and mental distress, cure many diseases, counter weak poisons, cure Black Breath, slow strong poisons, and triple the healing rate of any wound.

plants.name: Attanar
plants.note: Moss from cool, shady stream banks applied as a poultice to reduce fevers.

plants.name: Barani
plants.note: Tiny forest-floor plant; tea from its leaves reduces nausea and vomiting.

plants.name: Culan
plants.note: Herbal powder or soluble mix used as an effective antispasmodic against some poisons; notably foul-tasting.

plants.name: Darsurion
plants.note: Wild and garden shrub; its silver leaves rubbed on skin relieve minor concussions, healing 1–6 hits.

plants.name: Elbin’s Basket
plants.note: Common meadow flower with pink basket-shaped petals; an extract of its roots acts as a heart stimulant, doubling movement rate for one round.

plants.name: Elendil’s Spear
plants.note: Very rare tall red flower of the tundra; its ground or boiled root yields an infusion that slows poisons and purifies water.

plants.name: Gildarion
plants.note: Rare herbal mixture used in salves to repair major organs and damaged arteries and veins.

plants.name: Harlindar
plants.note: Liquid preparation that, taken with water, ensures safe childbirth and corrects nutritional imbalances during pregnancy.

plants.name: Kelventari
plants.note: Rare plant with silver-white flowers; berry juice cures third-degree burns without scarring.

plants.name: Latha
plants.note: Herb from cold lakeshores; mashed and boiled stems provide a mild analgesic for headaches, toothaches, and colds.

plants.name: Maiana
plants.note: Herbal mixture taken diluted as a respiratory decongestant.

people.description: Angmar’s population mixes free and enslaved men with strong contingents of professional soldiers and ex-soldiers from warrior cultures such as Rhudaur, Rûn, and the Estarav of the Anduin, supported by orcs, trolls, and other creatures in the Witch-king’s service.

list.name: Men of Rhudaur
list.description: Men of the former kingdom of Rhudaur; many soldiers, especially land-hungry peasants, enlist in Angmar’s armies and later settle as soldier-farmers in fortified villages.

list.name: Men of Rûn
list.description: Eastern (‘Rhunish’) peoples from rigidly hierarchical, warlike cultures; peasant soldiers can rise in status through service in Angmar and retire as relatively prosperous veterans on frontier farms.

list.name: Estarav (Northrons of the Anduin Valleys)
list.description: Northmen of the Great River valleys; some young men, especially those with strong warrior values, join the Witch-king’s forces seeking wealth and opportunity.

list.name: Freeholders of fortified villages
list.description: Ex-soldiers of Angmar settled as freemen in fortified villages along the borders; they farm, patrol daily, escort supply trains, and provide additional troops in wartime.

list.name: Slaves
list.description: Captives and subjugated people from various backgrounds who work the land to support the warrior elite, although the harsh climate forces some warriors to continue agricultural labor.

list.name: Orcs of Angmar
list.description: Bulk of the Witch-king’s infantry, garrisoning forts, border castles, and strongholds throughout the realm.

list.name: The Witch-king of Angmar
list.description: Supreme ruler of Angmar and lord of the Nazgûl; directs long-term political and military strategy based on attrition, terror, and manipulation of frontier peoples.

land.name: Angmar (general region)
land.description: Harsh territory north of Arnor with poor soils, severe climate, and primarily military function; described as land where only hardened peasants can survive and where warfare is an accepted part of life.
land.biome_hint: cold frontier land with tundra, rocky hills, and scattered forests

land.name: Fortified frontier villages
land.description: Small walled communities tied as fiefs to one of three frontier castles; each village must patrol its surroundings daily, escort supply trains, and supply soldiers for campaigns.
land.biome_hint: small agricultural pockets in cold, windswept valleys and slopes

land.name: Agricultural fields of Angmar
land.description: Small fields bounded by low stone walls to break the wind; rye, barley, oats, potatoes, cabbage, turnips, and beets form the basic crops, supplemented by peas, beans, occasional eggs, and hardy apple trees.
land.biome_hint: marginal agriculture on cold plateaus and valley bottoms

land.name: Border castles of Angmar
land.description: Three major castles guard the borders, collect tribute, coordinate conscription and supplies, and rule the villages; examples include Morkai and Eldanar, with multi-level donjons and complex defenses.
land.biome_hint: stone strongholds on hills or rocky spurs in severe climate

land.name: Carn Dûm
land.description: Capital fortress-city of Angmar in the northern mountains, with extensive outer and inner defenses; principal political, military, and logistical center of the Witch-king.
land.biome_hint: mountain fortress in subarctic conditions, with steep access routes

land.name: No Man’s Land of Angmar
land.description: Border buffer zone marked by towers, small keeps like Cargash Keep, and minor castles like Eldanar, serving as a defensive cushion and theatre for raids and skirmishes.
land.biome_hint: broken, cold uplands and ravines designed for defense-in-depth

land.name: Streams and rivers of Angmar
land.description: Network of cold streams with shaded banks where mosses like Attanar and other herbs grow; provide fish for bears and villagers as a key food source.
land.biome_hint: cold streams in narrow valleys with sparse but important riparian vegetation

regions.notes: This object treats Angmar as a political and geographic region in the mid-Third Age at the height of the Witch-king’s power; subregions such as Carn Dûm or specific castles can be split into separate site objects for higher resolution. A rocky, barren, and bleak highland plateau with frozen soil, little water, and severe wood shortages. Populated by Orcs, Trolls, and dark-hearted Men who serve the Iron Crown of Carn Dûm.
regions.elevation: average: 3,746 feet. lowest point: confluence of the Angsiril with the Angsîr 2,100 feet. highest point: western end of the Angirith 5,673 feet.

regions.name: Rohan, Wold, Westmarch
regions.description: Rohan is a beautiful territory and one of the jewels of the Gondorian crown, a wide land of rolling pastures and coombes dominated by the towering peaks of the White Mountains, home of the semi‑nomadic Horse‑lords who became the Rohirrim after T.A. 2510.

climate.description: Rohan usually enjoys a mild and happy climate, albeit somewhat unpredictable; the summers are pleasantly warm and the winters are almost always moderate.
climate.temperature_pattern: Winter is a cool, moist time; deep winter grips the land for about eight weeks after Yule, but even then Rohirrim continue outdoor life, and the White Mountains are colder and more severe than the plains.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Eastern and northern Rohan, particularly the Wold, are very windy and rather dry, relying on infrequent but pleasant Anduin valley showers; the Westmarch is unprotected by the mountains and receives more rain than any other region in Rohan, frequently troubled by unpredictable storms turning inland from the Great Sea or striking up from the Cape of Andrast.
climate.notes: Even during the coldest winters, a warm, dry foehn wind periodically blows north from the Bay of Belfalas across the mountains, melting snow and increasing avalanche danger; heavy snow cover can melt suddenly and flood the lowlands.

fauna.description: Rohan is blessed with a wealth of animals that make it ideal for the Riders: great horse‑herds on the meadows, abundant cattle and sheep, game in the wooded foothills and mountains, and several notable predators and game animals described in detail.

creatures.name: Horses of Rohan
creatures.note: Vast herds range the Emnets, Westfold, and especially the Westmarch, whose grass is said to raise the best horses; the mearas line descends from the white stallion captured by Lód and renamed Felaróf by Eorl.

creatures.name: Cattle and sheep
creatures.note: Sheep graze mostly in the Emnets and the Wold, cattle mostly in the Westfold; they feed primarily on the rich natural grass, with hay reserves for lean winters.

creatures.name: Wild Boars
creatures.note: Dangerous quarry hunted for sport; fully grown boars stand three to four feet high, with eight‑ to twelve‑inch tusks, bad‑tempered and ferocious, often rushing foes when startled and capable of killing hunters and even kings.

creatures.name: Grass Cats
creatures.note: Once the main large hunter of Calenardhon, now extremely rare due to the disappearance of their principal prey, the Wild Kine; shy and seldom seen, but their blood‑curdling screams can stampede horse herds.

creatures.name: Grey Wolves
creatures.note: Still common in the mountains but virtually absent from the lowlands; winter packs may descend from coombes or cross a frozen Limlight in severe winters to hunt in the plains.

creatures.name: Bears (Black, Brown, and Blue)
creatures.note: Three kinds of bears live in the White Mountains, attracted by cool trout‑laden streams and ground‑bee honey; black bears are rarely dangerous unless provoked, while brown and blue‑grey bears can reach over ten feet and may attack simply from bad temper, especially in hot mating seasons.

creatures.name: Ground Bees
creatures.note: Nests are common in the soil of vales and high grasslands; they provide honey valued by both bears and Rohirrim.

creatures.name: Red and White Foxes
creatures.note: Prey on rodents of the vales and high grasslands.

creatures.name: Grass Grouse and Green Pheasants
creatures.note: Tasty and pugnacious game birds native to Rohan’s meadows and fields.

creatures.name: Hawks, Falcons, and Eagles
creatures.note: Hawks and falcons abound, roosting in White Mountain cliffs and migrating along the Misty Mountains; the great Eagles occasionally glide over the West Emnet, revered as sacred and never molested despite preying on sheep and small cattle.

flora.description: The meadows of Rohan are well‑watered and fertile, with thick black topsoil more than twenty feet deep in parts of the Westfold, producing the richest pastures in western Middle‑earth; aside from the Wold, soils are uniformly exceptional, supporting lush grasslands, crops, and extensive wildflowers.

plants.name: Rohirrim pasture grasses
plants.note: Tall, dark green grasses of the West Emnet, Westfold, and Westmarch provide superb grazing for horses; in the Wold the grass is short and springy, withering brown without recent rain.

plants.name: Wildflowers of Rohan
plants.note: In spring and fall, wildflowers ‘parade across the swaying fields’, and skies take on palettes of greys, blues, reds, oranges, purples, and pinks.

plants.name: Heather and moorland flora of the Wold
plants.note: The Wold’s tortured ridges carry heather and tough moorland plants adapted to permeable rock and mineral‑poor soil.

plants.name: Field crops
plants.note: Oats, rye, barley, wheat, berries, and hay are principal crops of the Westfold and Eastfold; hay is stacked in tall, carefully lashed mounds around pits for winter storage.

plants.name: Forests and woods
plants.note: Firienwood and Snowbourne Forest in the Eastfold form the most heavily wooded areas, while foothill woods and pine forests cloak lower White Mountain slopes and provide excellent hunting.

people.description: The Horse‑lords are a wandering people whose history passes through three main stages—othraim in southern Rhovanion, othod in the vales of the Anduin, and Rohirrim in Rohan—remaining semi‑nomadic and horse‑centered but evolving from scattered clans to a unified kingdom at the heart of western trade and war.

list.name: Othraim (Horse‑clans of southern Rhovanion)
list.description: Six tribes of Northman horsemen occupying the southern Rhovanion plain until T.A. 1856–99; semi‑nomadic herders who called themselves the Othraim, ancestral culture of both Othod and Rohirrim.

list.name: Othod (Horse‑folk of the Anduin vales)
list.description: Descendants of Northman refugees who migrated across the Anduin after the Wainrider wars and settled first near the Gladden Fields and later in the upper Anduin vales; united horse‑folk who became known as the Othod.

list.name: Rohirrim
list.description: Horse‑lords who migrated to Calenardhon in T.A. 2510 after saving Gondor at the Field of Celebrant, renaming the land the Riddermark or Rohan; semi‑nomadic Riders who shun towns except in winter or on market and festival days and owe loyalty to one Lord of the Mark.

list.name: Dunlendings
list.description: Rugged descendants of the Daen Coentis in Dunland and the White Mountains; ancient enemies of the Rohirrim whose clans once dominated the mountains and upper meadows now claimed by Rohan.

list.name: Woses (Druedain)
list.description: Short, stocky Wild Men inhabiting primeval woodlands like Taur Andrast and other remnants; unexcelled woodcrafters who carve Púkel‑men and sometimes interact with Rohan in times of war.

land.name: The Riddermark (Rohan proper)
land.description: The Green Province of Calenardhon after T.A. 2510, granted to the Othod by Steward Cirion; bounded roughly by the Anduin on the east, the Isen on the west, the White Mountains on the south, and Fangorn and the Limlight on the north.
land.biome_hint: broad temperate grasslands with high plains, coombes, and mountain foothills

land.name: The Wold
land.description: Steeply folded, semi‑arid downs forming the northeastern part of Rohan; green but treeless hills of short, springy grass and heather, with porous chalky rock where rain drains off quickly.
land.biome_hint: windy chalk downs suited to sheep pasture

land.name: East Emnet and West Emnet
land.description: Vast carpets of tall grass forming the central plains; East Emnet stretches between the Wold and the Entwash, West Emnet between Fangorn, the Misty Mountains, and the Deeping Stream.
land.biome_hint: deep fertile grasslands ideal for horse herds

land.name: Eastfold and Westfold
land.description: Rich, well‑watered pastures along the northern flanks of the White Mountains, cut by streams from the high valleys; Westfold holds the Deeping Coomb and Hornburg, Eastfold contains Harrowdale and the Snowbourne.
land.biome_hint: rolling foothill pastures with coombes and stream‑cut valleys

land.name: Westmarch
land.description: Westernmost area of Rohan, lying west and south of the Gap between the Angren (Isen) and Adorn; lightly settled, often threatened by Dunlendings, with the best grass and finest horses in the realm.
land.biome_hint: windswept frontier grassland with high rainfall and superb grazing

land.name: Gap of Rohan
land.description: Forty‑mile‑wide strategic pass between the southern Misty Mountains and the northwestern White Mountains, only about 3,700 feet high at its center, providing a grassy doorway between Eriador and lands east and south.
land.biome_hint: broad, windy mountain gap with river valleys

land.name: White Mountains (Ered Nimrais edge)
land.description: Snow‑covered limestone and marble range rising steeply from Rohan’s meadows, with jagged peaks like the Thrihyrne, Starkhorn, Irensaga, and Dwimorberg; carved by glaciers into alpine valleys such as the Deeping Coomb and Harrowdale.
land.biome_hint: alpine mountains with snowfields, cliffs, caves, and conifer woods on lower slopes

land.name: Major rivers of Rohan
land.description: The Isen and Adorn feed the Westmarch; the Entwash rises in Fangorn and winds through the heart of Rohan before breaking into a fen‑filled delta at its mouths; the Snowbourne rises in the White Mountains at Harrowdale; the Limlight separates the East Emnet from Lórien.
land.biome_hint: cold snow‑fed rivers, fens in the Entwash delta, and trout streams in mountain valleys

regions.notes: This region object focuses on Rohan in the Rohirric Age (T.A. 2510 onward), but the same land supports earlier Othod settlements; sub‑sites like Edoras, Helm’s Deep, and Dunharrow can be handled as separate site objects tied to this region.

regions.name: Dor-en-Emyl
regions.supplement: Havens of Gondor: Land of Belfalas
regions.description: Dor‑en‑Ernil, the Land of the Prince, is a princely fief in south‑central Gondor encompassing Belfalas and neighboring lowlands and highlands; ruled from the coastal city of Dol Amroth, it is famed for its White‑ships, Elven‑tinged nobility, and constant tension with Umbar and the Eredrim highlanders.

climate.description: Dor‑en‑Ernil lies along the Bay of Belfalas with rugged coasts, hilly peninsulas, and river valleys, giving it a generally mild, maritime climate with strong coastal winds, sea fogs, and seasonal storms rolling in from the Great Sea.
climate.temperature_pattern: Temperatures are tempered by the sea; coastal areas are cooler in summer and milder in winter than inland Gondor, while high mountain valleys in the Ered Tarthonion experience cooler, more alpine conditions.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Sea‑driven storms and onshore winds bring regular rain to the coastal belt and windward highlands, while leeward valleys are somewhat drier; marshes and river deltas around the Morthond provide humid microclimates.
climate.notes: Winds, currents, and tides in the Bay of Belfalas are important enough to receive dedicated tables, reflecting a seafaring culture that reads and rides frequent swells, tides, and shifting winds.

fauna.description: The fauna of Dor‑en‑Ernil and Belfalas reflects coastal, riverine, marsh, highland, and open‑sea environments, including marine predators, sea turtles, whales, kraken, as well as land animals like goats, boars, wolves, and bears in the Ered Tarthonion.

creatures.name: Sharks of the Bay of Belfalas
creatures.note: Several shark species prowl the bay, appearing in encounter tables as significant hazards to sailors and swimmers and potential threats near wrecks and battle sites.

creatures.name: Sea‑turtles and Fell‑turtles
creatures.note: Large turtles inhabit coastal waters and islands; fell‑turtles are dangerous variants that can threaten boats or divers.

creatures.name: Whales
creatures.note: Whales roam the deeper waters of the bay, impressive but usually not hostile, occasionally appearing as hazards or omens during voyages.

creatures.name: Kraken
creatures.note: Legendary sea monsters of the Bay of Belfalas, capable of destroying ships; listed explicitly among ‘beasts of the bay’ as the greatest sea threat.

creatures.name: Coastal and river animals
creatures.note: Crocodiles occur in some marshes and river deltas; hunting lizards and various poisonous snakes or spiders appear in warm lowland environs.

creatures.name: Highland fauna
creatures.note: Goats and other mountain animals populate the Ered Tarthonion, where Eredrim herders and bandits share the rugged slopes with predators.

flora.description: Dor‑en‑Ernil’s land ranges from hilly Belfalas peninsula and Ered Tarthonion highlands to river deltas and marshes, supporting coastal scrub, vineyards, cultivated fields, forest patches, and sacred groves used by the Eredrim and Dúnedain alike.

plants.name: Coastal and cliff vegetation
plants.note: Hardy shrubs, grasses, and salt‑tolerant plants cling to the granite and limestone cliffs around Dol Amroth and along the Belfalas shore.

plants.name: River‑delta and marsh plants
plants.note: Reeds, rushes, and marsh grasses dominate the Morthond’s delta near Edhellond, providing cover, nesting habitat, and resources for both Men and Elves.

plants.name: Highland pines and woodlands
plants.note: The Ered Tarthonion, ‘Mountains of Lofty Pines’, bear coniferous forests and mixed woods in high valleys where Eredrim villages cluster.

plants.name: Vineyards and orchards
plants.note: Fertile valleys and lowland slopes support vineyards, orchards, and grain fields that feed Dol Amroth, Linhir, and surrounding manors.

people.description: Dor‑en‑Ernil’s population mixes Dúnedain princes and lowland commoners with Eredrim highlanders descended from the Daen Coentis, independent Elves of Edhellond, and sailors, merchants, privateers, smugglers, and Corsair enemies across the bay.

list.name: Princes of Dol Amroth
list.description: Hereditary Dúnedain rulers of Dor‑en‑Ernil, holding a status just below the King and Steward; their line carries Elven blood and rules almost autonomously from Dol Amroth, symbolized by a silver Swan‑ship on deep blue.

list.name: Dúnedain of Dor‑en‑Ernil
list.description: High Men descended from Númenóreans who settled the coasts; the nobility maintain a Dúnadan character, ruling river valleys and lowlands and intermarrying with local folk.

list.name: Eredrim (Mountain Folk)
list.description: Dunlending‑related highlanders of the Ered Tarthonion, descendants of the Daen Coentis; rugged village dwellers ruled by their own chieftains, paying tribute in kind, practicing animistic and sometimes dark rites and resisting assimilation.

list.name: Common folk of the lowlands
list.description: Intermingled Dúnedain and Eredrim commoners of inland river vales, forming a mixed culture that abandoned cattle‑theft and cremation, adopted Dúnadan law and Valar worship, but retained warrior respect and some distrust of Elves.

list.name: Elves of Edhellond
list.description: Sindar and Silvan Elves of the Elf‑haven in the Morthond delta; Sindar provide shipwrights, captains, and leaders, while Silvan Elves form most of the common folk, hunters, and fishers.

list.name: Corsairs of Umbar
list.description: Pirates from Umbar and Harad whose sleek warships threaten Gondor’s south coast; long‑standing enemies of Dol Amroth, frequently raiding and warring across the Bay of Belfalas.

land.name: Dor-en-Ernil
land.description: Princely fief in south‑central Gondor between the Gilrain/Serni and Morthond/Ringló rivers, including Belfalas peninsula, coastal towns, inland river vales, and Ered Tarthonion highlands.
land.biome_hint: coastal hills and peninsulas with river valleys, deltas, and mountain spurs

land.name: Belfalas
land.description: Great, hilly peninsula in southern Gondor jutting into the Bay of Belfalas; sometimes used as an alternative name for the southern half of Dor‑en‑Ernil.
land.biome_hint: rugged coastal hills with cliffs, coves, and sea‑exposed slopes

land.name: Bay of Belfalas
land.description: Broad bay south of Gondor whose waters carry White‑ships, Elven swan‑ships, and Corsair warships; filled with islets, shoals, tides, and storms that shape the region’s seafaring life.
land.biome_hint: temperate open bay with strong winds, currents, and rich marine life

land.name: Ered Tarthonion
land.description: Highlands of Dor‑en‑Ernil, forming a transverse section of the White Mountain range that rises in central Belfalas; a rugged country of pine‑clad peaks and Eredrim villages.
land.biome_hint: steep pine‑covered mountains and high valleys

land.name: Morthond and Edhellond
land.description: The Morthond, or Black‑root, rises by the Paths of the Dead in the White Mountains, flows past Erech, and enters the Bay of Belfalas; its delta hosts the Elf‑haven of Edhellond with tunnel‑harbors and swan‑ships.
land.biome_hint: cool river and marshy delta emptying into a temperate bay

regions.notes: This object treats Dor‑en‑Ernil as the broader Land of the Prince; sites like Dol Amroth, Linhir, Edhellond, and Castle Amrûnaur can be represented as separate site objects referencing this region.

regions.name: Forovirian
regions.description: Mount Gundabad is the northernmost and one of the highest peaks of the Misty Mountains, a triple‑spired volcanic giant honeycombed into a vast Orc‑city and forge complex, capital of the Orcs of the Mountains and a key client realm of Angmar.

climate.description: The region around Mount Gundabad is bleak and storm‑ridden, with perpetually overcast skies, frequent storms, and a long, dismal, bitter winter shaped by the clash of warm Anduin air and the chills of the Forodwaith.
climate.temperature_pattern: Winter endures nearly half the year, bringing deep snows to foothills and mountains; spring and fall are short, while summers are cool in the highlands but warm enough in the lowlands to melt snow and create extensive swamp.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Storms with heavy snow and rain are common, especially where warm valley air meets northern cold; lightning often strikes Gundabad’s peaks, and the advancing and retreating Oglorb Glacier scours slopes as it melts and grinds forward.
climate.notes: High winds tear across Gundabad’s slopes, and sudden gusts have been known to sweep climbers from precarious handholds; fumes from Orcish furnaces dirty the snow and foul the mountain’s upper air.

fauna.description: The surface around Mount Gundabad has been deforested and scoured, so large surface animal life is sparse; most living things of interest dwell underground in the Pits and tunnels, including specialized fungi and cave organisms that feed the Orcs.

creatures.name: Orcs of Mount Gundabad
creatures.note: Six thousand or more Orcs in arms and many more noncombatants throng the tunnels and Pits; they form the largest and most powerful goblin city in the northern mountains and supply levies and war gear for Angmar.

creatures.name: Captive slaves
creatures.note: Men, Dwarves, and others are brought as captives to labor in the Pits, mines, and forges until they perish, forming a grim but important component of the living population.

flora.description: Centuries of Orc occupation have stripped the lands around Mount Gundabad of trees and most surface vegetation; the primary ‘flora’ of interest is now underground, where numerous fungi species grow in the damp dark of the Pits and tunnels.

plants.name: Twisted pines and thorn‑scrub
plants.note: Only scattered, solitary twisted trees, scrub pines, and thorny bushes survive on the slopes and foothills, along with patches of tough goat grass and pallid lichen.

plants.name: Gundabad edible fungi
plants.note: Dragons Claw, Purple Trumpet, Giant Blackling, Slimy Gorkin, Sorcerer’s Butter, Malodorous Milkcap, Bearded Fang, and Warg’s‑blood Slime are among the many mushrooms and slimes eaten by Orcs, some as delicacies reserved for high rank.

plants.name: Gundabad poisonous fungi
plants.note: Witch’s Pie, Skull Cap, White Destroyer, Deadly Akra, Shagu‑bitter, and Traitor’s Goblet provide paralytic or deadly toxins, often distilled for use on Orc weapons.

plants.name: Phosphorescent and medicinal fungi
plants.note: Goblin Lantern and Luminous Vrak emit eerie light in darkness; Luminous Vrak also halts bleeding and is used in Orcish healing salves.

plants.name: Hallucinogenic fungi
plants.note: Black Cackler, Dung‑reveling Psarch, and Other‑eyes induce laughter, altered perception, or temporary sight of hidden things, sometimes sought by Orc shamans.

plants.name: Wood‑like fungi
plants.note: Great Ironstem and Banded Kalhn can be treated to take on wood‑like consistency, used in Orc constructions such as liquor barrels.

people.description: Mount Gundabad is overwhelmingly an Orc realm, ruled by a Goblin‑king recognized as overlord of the Orcs of the Mountains under the distant suzerainty of the Witch‑king of Angmar; it also holds numerous slaves from surrounding lands.

list.name: Orcs of the Mountains
list.description: Principal goblin tribes of the northern Misty Mountains, united under the lord of Gundabad as Ashdurbuk, the ‘One All‑Ruler’, providing levies and tribute to Angmar and fielding enormous war hosts in the Trollshaws, Eriador, and Rhovanion.

list.name: Goblin‑king of Gundabad
list.description: Title for the tyrannical overlord who rules Mount Gundabad and claims suzerainty over all Orc tribes of the mountains; his court in the spires is rife with intrigue, assassination, and revolt.

list.name: Priests of Darkness
list.description: Orcish priests who preserve records of goblin deeds over two Ages and guard secret lore and rituals in hidden chambers, including tales of the coming from Morgoth’s realm.

list.name: Slaves of Gundabad
list.description: Captives from the Free Peoples, brought to labor in mines, Pits, and forges without hope of release, sustained only by fading memories of freedom.

land.name: Mount Gundabad (triple‑spired peak)
land.description: Northernmost peak of the Misty Mountains, its central Great Spire rising to about 12,985 feet and flanked by the Twisted Spire and Cloven Spire; its south face bears the carved visage of an open‑jawed fire drake that forms an infamous entrance.
land.biome_hint: barren volcanic peak with sheer rock faces, minimal vegetation, and extensive underground tunnels

land.name: Twisted Spire and Cloven Spire
land.description: Sub‑cones of the original volcano; the Twisted Spire bends and turns back on itself like a crooked wizard’s cap, and the Cloven Spire is split from its summit for some two thousand feet as if hewn by a mighty axe.
land.biome_hint: extreme high‑altitude rock spires subject to storms and lightning

land.name: Gundalok Shelf
land.description: Plateau southeast of Mount Gundabad between the arms of the Misty Mountains and Ered Mithrin; rises about 400 feet above the Anduin Vales, with narrow, easily defended trails leading up from the lowlands.
land.biome_hint: stony, well‑watered but thin‑soiled upland with limited vegetation

land.name: River Langwell and falls
land.description: Headwater of the Anduin traversing the Gundalok Shelf from the Misty Mountains; runs east to the shelf’s edge and then plunges over in a spectacular foaming cascade.
land.biome_hint: cold, fast mountain river and waterfall carved into plateau edge

land.name: Oglorb Glacier and Lake Hyord
land.description: The Oglorb Glacier descends for twenty leagues from a northern spur of the Misty Mountains, melting just north of Mount Gundabad to form the chill waters of Lake Hyord, a deep lake five to ten or more miles across that protects Gundabad’s northern flank.
land.biome_hint: glacial ice and a cold, metallic‑tasting mountain lake in a tundra fringe

land.name: Den Loke (north–south corridor)
land.description: Easily traversed corridor between the Misty Mountains and Ered Mithrin dominated by Mount Gundabad, serving as the main passage between the Forodwaith and Rhovanion/Anduin Vales.
land.biome_hint: high, cold corridor between mountain chains with rocky ridges and sparse scrub

land.name: Angirith (western route)
land.description: Rugged route cutting through the northern Misty Mountains westward toward the Angmar Vale, with Mount Gundabad commanding its eastern end.
land.biome_hint: rocky, storm‑prone high pass linking Gundabad to Angmar

regions.notes: This object focuses on the Gundabad region at T.A. 1640, when it stands as an unconquered Orc capital and key armory for Angmar; later events like the War of the Dwarves and Orcs and the Battle of Five Armies can be modeled as time‑variant overlays.

regions.name: Ered Luin
regions.supplement: Lindon: The Kingdom of the Elves (fan-made Realm #3003)
regions.description: Ered Luin, also known as the Blue Mountains, is an ancient mountain range separating Lindon from Eriador. Extending 590 miles from the Ice Bay of Forochel down to Orod Elu in the south, the range is formed of grey rock with a bluish tint caused by a high concentration of copper ores. The mountains are riddled with natural caverns, marble and limestone formations, and once held precious metals, though Dwarvish mining has left them depleted of gold except for minor remote veins. Geology consists of marble and limestone with folded sandstone and shale foothills. abundant copper, iron, and tin ores are actively mined by the dwarves (naugrim).

climate.description: Ered Luin has a harsh, high-mountain alpine and sub-alpine climate with cold, severe winters and moderate, short summers. Mean annual temperature: 35-50° F. Average low: Narwain -10° F. Average high: Úrui 80° F. Average annual precipitation: 30-40 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: Mean annual temperatures range between 35°F and 50°F. Winters are extremely cold, with average lows in Narwain (January) reaching down to -10°F, while average summer highs in Urui (July) reach 80°F. Rapid temperature drops of 3.6°F per 1,000 feet of altitude are common.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Average annual precipitation is 30–40 inches. Western slopes, facing the sea, receive substantial rainfall and snow, which feed cold glacial ice fields and snowfields that remain year-round on the taller northern peaks.
climate.notes: Snow-melt floods from the high peaks keep surrounding rivers cold year-round. High-altitude climbs are hazardous due to steepness, year-round snow caps, and freezing winds.

fauna.description: The rugged mountains and high forests support hardy conifer-adapted species and dangerous predators, with reclusive Giants inhabiting the high peaks.

creatures.name: Mountain predators and large mammals
creatures.note: The range supports deer, wild boars, wolves, and bears in the forested slopes and valleys, with predators adapting to the steep terrain.

creatures.name: Blue Mountain Giants
creatures.note: A few dozen reclusive Giants dwell in both sections of the Ered Luin. Although long-lived and considered by Elves to be Mountain-Maiar or Faeries, they are unpredictable when dealing with Dwarves and Men.

flora.description: The slopes of Ered Luin feature conifer-dominated forests and alpine scrub, with tree growth disappearing entirely above 5,000 feet.

plants.name: Pine forests of the slopes
plants.note: Pine trees cloak the lower slopes, contributing to the hazy blue silhouette of the range when viewed from a distance, but they do not grow above 5,000 feet.

plants.name: Lowland mixed forests
plants.note: The lowlands and foothills feature mixed deciduous and coniferous trees, including oak, ash, poplars, and holly, growing in folded sandstone and shale soils.

people.description: The Ered Luin is primarily inhabited by the Dwarves of Nogrod and Belegost's descendants, alongside scattered Elven wanderers and mountain Giants.

list.name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
list.description: Descendants of the Firebeards and Broadbeams who maintained Belegost and Nogrod. They are skilled miners, smiths, and weapon-crafters, mining abundant copper, iron, and tin.

list.name: Mountain Giants
list.description: Extremely long-lived, reclusive beings of immense size who dwell in the high peaks and avoid contact with other races.

land.name: Northern Ered Luin peaks
land.description: The taller northern section of the range, home to the peak of Lossotil (9,497 feet) and several glacial ice fields.
land.biome_hint: alpine peaks with year-round ice caps and glaciers

land.name: Southern Ered Luin peaks
land.description: The lower southern section of the range, including Orod Elu (6,824 feet), which experiences seasonal winter snow cover.
land.biome_hint: sub-alpine peaks with seasonal winter snow

regions.notes: Ered Luin stands as a vital mining zone and barrier separating Lindon from Eriador, with secret, rune-guarded passes. The Blue Mountains are ancient and run north-to-south, heavily composed of marble and limestone with massive natural caverns. Snowfields and icecaps are present year-round on the northern range, while pine trees disappear over 5,000 feet. Mineral copper deposits give the peaks their bluish tint.
regions.elevation: average peak elevation: 7,500-8,500 feet. lowest peak: Amon Othar 6,023 feet. highest peak: Lossotil 9,497 feet.
regions.administrative_organization: King, descending from the lineage of both tribes, advised by council of 49 Elders, offices distributed by the King according to age and merit. Property nominally held by the Houses of Dwalin and Thrár, but subdivided by private ownership.
regions.military: 490 Warriors. 2,100 Warrior Levy.
regions.products: Finished goods, iron, tin, copper, lead.
regions.symbol: A Hammer surmounted by Three Tongues of Flame

regions.name: Ered Luin Vales
regions.supplement: Lindon: The Kingdom of the Elves (fan-made Realm #3003)
regions.description: The Ered Luin Vales encompass the central valleys, river passes, and sub-alpine basins of the Blue Mountains. This region is centered around the Nan-i-Naugrim (Dwarven Vale) and the headwaters of the River Annuduin, where the Dwarves established a complex network of fourteen holds, including Merlost, built on the ruins of ancient Belegost.

climate.description: The vales enjoy a sheltered sub-alpine valley climate, less exposed to high mountain winds but subject to cold winter pooling and year-round cold waters.
climate.temperature_pattern: Winters are cold and snow-filled due to cold air pooling in the valleys, while summers are brief and mild. Mean temperature averages 40–50°F.
climate.precipitation_pattern: Precipitation is moderate (30-40 inches), with heavy winter snowfall blocking passes and keeping valley floors damp and green.
climate.notes: The rivers in the vales are fed by melting glaciers and northern snowfields, keeping their waters cold throughout the year.

fauna.description: Valley fauna consists of riverine and forest animals adapting to sub-alpine valleys and cold mountain rivers.

creatures.name: Cold-river fish
creatures.note: Cold-water trout and other migratory species thrive in the glacier-fed headwaters of the River Annuduin.

creatures.name: Small valley mammals
creatures.note: Rabbits, hares, voles, and mountain goats dwell in the grassy margins and rocky slopes of the vales.

flora.description: Vales are rich in sub-alpine scrub, mountain grasses, and coniferous forests descending from the slopes.

plants.name: Mountain pine and birch
plants.note: Pine trees cover the surrounding slopes up to the treeline, while birch and mountain shrubs grow along the valley streams.

people.description: The primary population of the vales is Dwarves of Durin's Folk and descendants of Belegost and Nogrod.

list.name: Dwarves of the Dwarven Vale
list.description: Firebeards and Broadbeams living in the fourteen holds of Nan-i-Naugrim. They maintain active smithing traditions and weapon-crafting at Merlost.

land.name: Nan-i-Naugrim (Dwarven Vale)
land.description: The central valley of the Ered Luin, home to fourteen Dwarven holds situated along the headwaters of the River Annuduin.
land.biome_hint: sub-alpine valley with cold rivers and coniferous margins

regions.notes: The Dwarven Vale serves as a central hub of trade and industry in western Eriador, connecting Elven Lindon with the kingdoms of Men.
regions.administrative_organization: King, descending from the lineage of both tribes, advised by council of 49 Elders, offices distributed by the King according to age and merit. Property nominally held by the Houses of Dwalin and Thrár, but subdivided by private ownership.
regions.military: 490 Warriors. 2,100 Warrior Levy.
regions.products: Finished goods, iron, tin, copper, lead.
regions.symbol: A Hammer surmounted by Three Tongues of Flame

regions.name: South Harlindon
regions.supplement: Lindon: The Kingdom of the Elves (fan-made Realm #3003)
regions.description: South Harlindon is a green, quiet coastal and forest region south of the Gulf of Lhûn. Historically a fief under Celeborn, Harlindon is characterized by lowlands cloaked in rich deciduous and coniferous forests and a long-term peaceful rural atmosphere. The eastern foothills are used by Dwarves for active coal and iron mines. Geology consists of folded sedimentary rock (slate and sandstone) with rich iron lodes and abundant coal deposits in forlindon.

climate.description: South Harlindon has a mild, wet maritime-temperate climate moderated by the Elven Sea (Belegaer). Mean annual temperature: 50-60° F. Average low: Ninui 15° F. Average high: Úrui 75° F. Average annual precipitation: 40-60 inches.
climate.temperature_pattern: Lows of around 15°F in winter and pleasant, cool summer highs reaching 75°F. The mean annual temperature is 50–60°F, providing a prolonged spring and soft autumn.
climate.precipitation_pattern: High precipitation of 40–60 inches annually, with frequent sea-mists and rainstorms coming off Belegaer.
climate.notes: The mild coastal air keeps winters much gentler than in the adjoining Ered Luin mountains.

fauna.description: The lush coastal forests support rich bird life, deer, and common woodland creatures alongside cold-water marine life in the estuaries.

creatures.name: Woodland birds and game
